Make the setting and assumptions on the distribution p_F explicit: define what it means for F ~ p_F to be a "trained model", state whether p_F is supported on all of \mathcal{F} or only a subset, and explicitly connect the minimization over \mathcal{F} used in the theory to sampling F ~ p_F (e.g., clarify whether theorems/minimizations should be over supp(p_F) rather than all of \mathcal{F}).
Provide the missing proof (or a precise reference) for Proposition 3.1; if it is in an appendix, add a pointer and ensure the proof is complete and self-contained.

Clarify the definition and role of \hat{f}(x): explain formally whether \hat{f}(x) is a binary correctness indicator, how \hat{f} is an element of \hat{\mathcal{F}}, and how these binary-valued functions should be treated in expectations and subsequent derivations.

Explain and justify the application of the function φ to averages of binary quantities (e.g., φ(E_{F~\hat{p}_F}[F(X)]) and φ(\hat{f}(x)) in Eq. (8)): state what φ is intended to measure, why it is valid to apply it to an average of 0/1 indicators, and under what conditions this operation preserves the intended properties.

Address the implicit equal-accuracy requirement used in Lemma B1 and Theorem 4.1: either (a) justify the assumption E_x[\hat{f}(x)] = E_x[\hat{g}(x)] for all \hat{f},\hat{g} (e.g., by restricting attention to models sampled from p_F with equal empirical accuracy), or (b) modify the statements/proofs so the minimization and expectations are taken over the support of p_F (or otherwise relax the assumption).

Formally define the set of trained models \mathcal{F}: give a concrete criterion (for example, models whose empirical loss is below a threshold or whose training accuracy exceeds a specified level) so readers can determine membership in \mathcal{F} used by the theory.

Describe precisely how the distribution p_F is derived from the training procedure: state the sources of randomness (e.g., initialization, SGD noise, data order), the optimization algorithm assumptions, and how these induce a distribution over trained models used in the analysis.

Provide a rigorous proof or precise conditions showing when and how the distributional equivalence property is preserved by the surrogate construction f -> \hat{f}: specify necessary/sufficient conditions on the transformation and on p_F under which \hat{p}_F inherits distributional equivalence from p_F.

Discuss the implications of distributional equivalence for model accuracy and generalization: if \hat{p}_F (or p_F) implies equal accuracies across sampled models, explain how this relates to the notion of "well-trained" models and the models' performance on unseen data, and state any assumptions required for these conclusions.

Provide empirical evidence (or propose experiments) to justify the simplifying assumption that model output probabilities collapse to one-hot vectors: measure and report the distribution of output probabilities across models and inputs, and if the collapse does not hold, either adapt the theory to operate on output probabilities or motivate the choice of φ as a confidence measure rather than operating on binarized outputs.
The claim of 'distributional equivalence' may not generalize to other loss functions; run experiments with alternative losses (e.g., MSE, label smoothing) and report whether the property still holds.
Clarify which loss function was used in Section 4.1 and discuss how the choice of loss could influence the observed distributional equivalence.

The assumption in the proof of Theorem B.1 that P(ĥf(x)) = P(ĥg(x)) for arbitrary ĥf, ĥg and all x is too strong; provide a rigorous justification for this assumption or weaken it and re-derive the results.
Provide theoretical analysis (e.g., bounds) quantifying how deviations from exact distributional equivalence affect ensemble performance when the property only holds approximately.
Empirically investigate scenarios where distributional equivalence might break down, such as different training regimes (varying learning rates, batch sizes) and report the effects.
Test the distributional equivalence property across different model architectures and datasets with varying complexity to evaluate its robustness and scope.

In Section 4.1, incorporate analyses that use more of the full-class probability distribution (for example, l = p(y) - p(ŷ) where ŷ is the predicted class) instead of focusing only on the target-class probability.
Analyze gradients of different loss functions with respect to model parameters to explain how loss choice might influence convergence and resulting output distributions.
Provide a clear practical methodology or metric (a diagnostic suite) that practitioners can use to quantify the degree of distributional equivalence between models in an ensemble.
Explore interventions (e.g., adversarial training, data augmentation) that might promote distributional equivalence and evaluate whether these techniques improve ensemble performance.
Provide a detailed analysis explaining why the majority vote classifier produced unsatisfactory results and explicitly relate that explanation to the discovered distributional equivalence property.
Evaluate the distributional equivalence property on larger, more realistic datasets (e.g., ImageNet or similar) to test whether the phenomenon generalizes beyond CIFAR-10.

Run experiments that apply stronger regularization and report results to test whether distributional equivalence is an artifact of overconfident models (e.g., include runs with weight decay, dropout, and batch normalization).
Include a theoretical derivation and empirical results for Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL) analogous to the analyses and experiments presented for the Brier score.
Add error rate (classification accuracy/error) plots to Fig. 6 in addition to the loss plots to provide a more intuitive performance measure.
Include experiments with different optimizers (e.g., SGD with momentum, Adam) to evaluate how optimizer choice affects ensemble performance and the distributional equivalence property.
Include experiments using data augmentation strategies and report their impact on the observed distributional equivalence and ensemble behavior.
Quantify model confidence and calibration (e.g., report ECE or reliability diagrams) and analyze whether overconfidence drives the observed distributional equivalence.

Clarify what you mean by "only differ in the training stochasticity": list all sources of stochasticity considered (random initialization, dataset shuffling, data augmentation order, dropout, mini-batch ordering, etc.) and state which are controlled or varied in experiments.

Test whether the observed distributional equivalence is driven by bias in the fixed dataset: run experiments with different train/test splits, controlled label/noise injections, or synthetic data to see if the effect persists.
Assess the role of architecture: evaluate distributional equivalence across multiple architectures (e.g., depth/width variants, convolutional vs transformer) to determine architectural dependence.

Assess the role of optimization choices: run experiments with different optimizers, learning rates, batch sizes, weight decay, and other hyperparameters to see how they influence output distribution similarity.

Quantify the impact of different random seeds for initialization and data shuffling separately: report metrics (e.g., distribution distances, KL divergence) and statistical significance to show how each seed source affects final outputs.

Evaluate distributional equivalence across diverse datasets: include datasets with different sizes, class imbalance, modalities, and levels of noise to establish the scope and limits of the phenomenon.

Investigate connections to generalization theory: measure loss-landscape flatness (e.g., Hessian spectrum, sharpness metrics) for ensemble members and test whether flatter minima correlate with stronger distributional equivalence.

Analyze the relationship between loss sharpness and ensemble diversity: compute ensemble diversity metrics (e.g., pairwise disagreement, prediction entropy) and correlate them with sharpness measures to determine causality or association.

Provide concrete, step-by-step guidance for applying the two-model ensemble-performance prediction method in practice, including required inputs, algorithmic steps, hyperparameter choices, and diagnostic checks.

Report computational cost and scaling: provide complexity analysis and empirical runtimes/memory costs for the two-model method as ensemble size and model size increase, and compare to training full ensembles.

Explicitly state the method's limitations and failure modes: identify conditions where the two-model prediction or distributional equivalence does not hold (e.g., heterogeneous ensembles, transfer learning, small-data regimes) and provide diagnostic tests to detect them.
Provide empirical tests or theoretical justification for the assumption of neural collapse across a wider range of datasets and model configurations, and discuss when this assumption may fail.

Evaluate distributional equivalence on datasets with varied label noise levels (including high label noise) to assess whether neural collapse and distributional equivalence break down under noisy labels.
Test distributional equivalence on higher-dimensional and different-modality datasets (e.g., NLP corpora and multi-modal data) rather than only CIFAR-10 and TinyImageNet, and report results.
Investigate how dataset characteristics such as class imbalance and varying feature dimensionality affect the distributional equivalence property, with targeted experiments.
Quantify the similarity of prediction distributions using explicit metrics (e.g., Wasserstein distance or other distributional similarity measures) and report these metrics in the experiments.

Analyze the sensitivity of the distributional equivalence property to training hyperparameters (at minimum: learning rate, optimizer choice, batch size, and random initialization seed) and report robustness results.

Study how distributional equivalence affects uncertainty quantification by measuring prediction diversity across ensemble members (e.g., variance or entropy of predictions) and reporting those statistics.
Calculate per-example prediction variance across ensemble members and correlate that variance with ensemble confidence and calibration to assess impacts on uncertainty estimates.
Differentiate the effects of distributional equivalence on aleatoric vs epistemic uncertainty with targeted analyses or experiments designed to isolate each type of uncertainty.

Examine whether distributional equivalence leads to overconfident ensemble predictions in specific scenarios, and if found, propose and evaluate mitigation strategies (e.g., temperature scaling, ensemble diversification).
Provide a detailed analysis of the limitations of using only two models to estimate ensemble performance, including cases where the two-model estimate diverges from larger-ensemble behaviour.

Characterize the conditions under which two-model performance estimation is reliable (e.g., properties of model diversity, dataset size, or training regime) and provide practical guidelines for users.
Empirically evaluate whether estimation accuracy improves when using more than two models (e.g., 3, 5, 10) and report the trade-offs between computational cost and estimation accuracy.
Discuss and experiment with ensemble construction strategies or model selection approaches that could improve two-model estimation when models are not perfectly distributionally equivalent.
Provide a hyperparameter sensitivity analysis showing how performance (accuracy), FLOPs, and pruning time vary when you change each hyperparameter.
Explore adaptive hyperparameter tuning (e.g., Bayesian optimization or reinforcement learning) to automatically adjust hyperparameters by dataset and sparsity level.
Provide practical guidelines or heuristics for selecting hyperparameter values for new datasets and architectures based on your sensitivity analysis.

Release a clear, working implementation in a widely used framework (PyTorch or TensorFlow) with documentation and runnable examples.
Investigate and describe ways to simplify the implementation (e.g., more efficient gradient calculation techniques or a modular design for easy integration into existing codebases).

Analyze and discuss trade‑offs between performance, implementation complexity, hyperparameter sensitivity, and computational overhead to help practitioners choose methods.
Evaluate DPaI on a wider range of tasks and model architectures to demonstrate applicability and robustness across datasets and sparsity levels.
Update the literature review and experimental comparisons to better situate the work within recent progress on pruning at initialization and related baselines.

Clarify how the differentiable formulation of the Node-Path Balancing Principle specifically addresses PaI challenges (e.g., avoiding layer collapse, ensuring effective initial topology) and why these challenges require a different approach than pruning trained networks.

Provide a detailed comparison with post-training differentiable-mask pruning methods across objectives, optimization strategies, and observed impacts on performance to highlight concrete differences and trade-offs.

Extend ablation studies to more diverse network architectures and multiple sparsity levels to validate the robustness of DPaI across settings.

Provide a theoretical justification for choosing the logarithm in the Node-Path Balancing (NPB) principle, explaining why log is preferable to other transforms in this setting.

Justify the novelty of using the Straight-Through Estimator (STE) here by citing prior STE-based work and explaining what is new or different in your application.

Provide empirical or theoretical evidence that the specific design choices in RePrompt yield meaningful advantages over existing approaches, addressing the novelty concerns and showing measurable improvements.

Investigate and empirically validate whether the LLM reliably identifies the truly 'impactful' input parts; compare LLM-identified impactful parts to other techniques (explainable AI, human annotations).
Provide more transparency about the 'summarizing team': detail how their data was collected, annotation procedures, study/IRB approvals, and any instructions or training provided to annotators.
Discuss potential ethical implications and failure modes of using LLMs to automatically refine prompts, especially for sensitive domains, and propose mitigation strategies.

Clarify novelty relative to Reflexion: explicitly describe how RePrompt differs from Reflexion (not just superficially), or state up front that this work is an application/adaptation of Reflexion for APE.

Either provide a rigorous mathematical formulation that justifies using terms like 'gradient-based' and 'loss optimization' (including explicit loss function and gradient computation), or replace those terms with accurate descriptions such as 'iterative prompt refinement' or 'feedback-driven prompt improvement'.

Resolve inconsistent component naming (e.g., 'LLM summarizer' vs 'summarization team') and adopt consistent labels for all LLMs and human roles throughout the paper and figures.

Define 'asymptotic closure' as used in the manuscript and clarify whether the end time of Phase II can be infinite and how you approximate or detect this closure in practice.
Define the variable r at its first appearance (line 481) and state its role in subsequent equations/arguments.

Provide a more explicit explanation linking spectral gaps in the Jacobian to concrete changes in the generative/sampling process — e.g., detail how the score function changes across phases and how those changes affect sample quality or dynamics.

For Figure 7 and other experiments, analyze and show how the observed eigenspectrum transitions correspond to the paper's three-phase theoretical framework (provide time-resolved spectra and interpretation).
Expand empirical validation beyond MNIST to additional, more complex datasets, and report eigenspectra at multiple time points to test the generality of the three-phase behavior.
Fix formatting issues: conform to the provided paper template and standardize/correct the bibliography entries and formatting.
Section 3.2 and other parts are confusing; rework this section with unambiguous notation and step-by-step exposition so readers can follow the method.
Provide a formal mathematical or algorithmic specification of the method (e.g., symbols, equations, or pseudocode) rather than only prose descriptions.

Include a concrete, worked example that walks through a single iteration of the method showing how feedback is collected, summarized, and used to update the prompt, including the exact prompt changes made.
Explicitly define what you mean by a "gradient-based" prompt optimization: state the gradient analog, the directionality of feedback, and how updates are computed.
Compare your approach in detail to prior gradient-analogy prompt optimization work (e.g., TextGrad, Pryzant et al. 2023), and clarify what is novel relative to those methods.
Clarify and justify the claim that RePrompt does not require a solution checker — explicitly contrast the mechanism with ReAct and Reflexion and state the substantive differences.
Describe the PromptAgent baseline used in the Travel Planner experiment within the experimental section (methods and/or appendix) so readers know how the baseline was implemented.

Define the convergence/termination criteria for the iterative prompt update (lines 209–219): specify formal stopping conditions (e.g., empty feedback, all proposed solutions present, max iterations) and how they are detected.

Explain what is meant by "manually encoded solutions in the optimizer" (line 226): list the common problems covered, why they require manual encoding, and why manual encoding is preferable to automatic handling.

Clarify how in-context learning and zero-shot generation are used in the experiments (lines 243 and 293): state where examples are provided, whether prompts use few-shot or zero-shot setups, and how ICL impacts results.

Provide a concrete comparison (e.g., empirical ablation or illustrative examples) showing how the summarization step reduces early-stage errors and randomness compared to iterative self-reflection methods.

Explicitly list the novel contributions of RePrompt in one place and provide concrete evidence (theoretical argument or experiments) that these design choices yield advantages over existing approaches.
Include an algorithm block/pseudocode in the paper that outlines the whole RePrompt pipeline (feedback summarizer, prompt optimizer, LLM interactions) to reduce ambiguity.
Define what you mean by an "LLM-based system agentic" in the introduction and justify why agentic behavior matters for the problem you address.
Expand evaluation beyond the two datasets (TravelPlanning and Guan et al.) to a larger, more diverse set of tasks and domains to strengthen the generalizability of the claims.
Provide a clearer analysis of why improvements on the Guan et al. dataset are limited (e.g., why only the Household domain shows gains) and identify failure modes.
Investigate and explain the low final pass rate on TravelPlanning (3.89%), and directly compare or replicate the recent bank-of-critics approach (e.g., arXiv:2405.20625) to quantify differences.
Demonstrate the method on tasks where the mapping from intermediate feedback to final outcome is indirect or complex, to show limits of the approach.
Provide experiments or analysis showing how interdependent attribute changes (e.g., budget, accommodation in TravelPlanning) are handled, and whether batch fixes capture these dependencies.
Describe and evaluate the scalability of the batch-fix procedure, including how many iterations are typically required and whether the approach remains tractable for larger datasets.
Include computational cost measurements (compute hours, cost, model calls) for the iterative prompt-refinement process to assess real-world applicability.
Provide a detailed analysis of convergence behavior: report the number of iterations to reach satisfactory performance and how this varies by dataset and task.
Describe exactly how batch-level fixes are generated (algorithm, prompts, heuristics) and quantify their impact on overall performance (ablation studies).
Explore and evaluate alternative methods for generating batch-level fixes (rule-based systems, human-in-the-loop, or hybrid approaches) and compare them experimentally to the LLM-generated fixes.
Investigate sensitivity to the quality and quantity of intermediate feedback by running experiments with noisy, sparse, or partially missing feedback and reporting robustness metrics.
Quantify and discuss the potential bias and instability introduced by using an LLM to summarize feedback and propose mitigations (e.g., ensembling, calibration, human verification).
Validate the LLM-identified "impactful parts" of the input against human annotations or other explainability methods to confirm reliability.
Discuss and evaluate alternatives to LLM-based identification of impactful input parts, such as explainable AI techniques, feature attribution methods, or domain-specific heuristics.
Provide a more thorough justification or empirical evidence for the claim that intermediate feedback can replace a final critic, including scenarios where it fails.
Explain whether and how the batch-fix method scales when feedback pieces interact non-linearly, and consider modeling interactions explicitly rather than only applying independent fixes.
Add a focused discussion of ethical considerations and potential harms from automatically refining prompts with LLMs, especially for sensitive domains, and propose safeguards.
Provide full details about the summarizing team: how data was collected from them, recruitment, annotation protocol, and IRB/ethics review information.

Explicitly clarify the novelty relative to Reflexion: state whether RePrompt is a novel algorithmic contribution or primarily an application of Reflexion to APE, and highlight concrete technical differences.
Include a direct experimental or algorithmic comparison to Reflexion (reproduce Reflexion baseline or its core components) and discuss performance and behavioral differences.

Address the known dependence on ReAct-style exemplar-query similarity (cite [2]): explain if RePrompt inherits this limitation and provide analyses or mitigations for out-of-distribution or novel queries.
Either include Gundawar et al. (LLM-Modulo) [3] as a baseline in experiments or explicitly justify why it was excluded and why its approach is not comparable.
Clarify the use of ML terminology such as 'gradient-based', 'loss optimization', and 'standard fine-tuning': state that these are analogies if so, or avoid these terms if they are inaccurate.

If claiming a 'gradient-based' or optimization-like process, provide a rigorous mathematical formulation: define the loss/objective, what is being optimized, and how gradients (or their analogue) are computed.
Define 'train RePrompt' (line 312) concretely: describe the training step, the learning signal, what parameters or prompt components are updated, and how learning is performed.

Provide concrete evidence that the PDDL verifier is impractical for iterative use (e.g., runtime benchmarks, scalability numbers, or modified problem settings) rather than simply asserting it is 'too slow'.
Explain why Travel Planning verifiers cannot be used as final evaluators in your setup; if you modified the task to remove verifiers, describe those modifications and motivate them.

Either align experiments with the paper's motivation by including domains where final evaluation is genuinely expensive or infeasible (examples: creative writing, complex scientific design), or rewrite the motivation to match the chosen benchmarks.

Modify or justify the exemplar/example selection process to reduce sensitivity to nearest-example selection (e.g., alternative selection strategies), and provide experiments showing RePrompt's robustness to poor or OOD exemplars.
Add comparisons with other Automatic Prompt Engineering (APE) methods as baselines (even if assumptions differ) and discuss how RePrompt compares; if omitted, provide clear justification.
Include or cite existing prompt engineering work on the TravelPlanner benchmark (or similar datasets), and explain how your experimental setup relates to prior work on that benchmark.

Run an ablation study that isolates the Act component: optimize prompts with and without Act and report downstream performance when inferencing both with and without Act (e.g., four conditions: optimize+infer with Act, optimize+infer without Act, optimize-without-Act+infer with Act, optimize-without-Act+infer without Act).

Provide an empirical comparison between prompt optimization that uses only final outcomes versus optimization that incorporates intermediate Act feedback (quantify gains from using intermediate feedback).
Explicitly state whether the Act feedback is used directly for optimization or is processed first; if processed, describe the processing steps and any transformations applied to the feedback.
Add a formal problem formulation (notation) and an algorithm box or high-level pseudocode that outlines each procedural step of the method so readers can reproduce and reason about it.
Make component names and roles consistent across figures and text (e.g., reconcile the 'LLM summarizer' in Figure 1 with references to a 'summarization team' in the prose).
Provide a precise definition of 'focus point' (what it is, format, and purpose); if it is produced by an LLM prompt, include the exact prompt or a clear specification of the extraction task.

Include the specific prompts used for each LLM role (summarizer, feedback provider, focus-point extractor, etc.) in the appendix, or at minimum provide template prompts and expected input/output formats.
Describe the summarization component in detail: which LLM model was used, the prompt, input batching strategy, and the exact output format consumed by downstream steps.

Specify the feedback provider concretely: what kinds of feedback are produced, how they are generated (LLM/human), the feedback schema, and exactly how feedback is incorporated into the prompt optimization objective.

Explain how the framework extends to agents that perform multiple rounds of generation and/or use multiple different LLMs (give algorithmic details and, if possible, experiments demonstrating applicability).

Provide evidence or argumentation that the Act module is necessary (e.g., show performance drop when Act is removed or replaced), addressing the reviewer's concern that the system might reduce to a standard prompt-optimization pipeline without Act.
Correct inaccurate claims about prior work (e.g., ProTeGi) and update the related-work and comparison sections to accurately reflect what prior methods do (such as minibatch optimization).

Add a more detailed empirical and qualitative comparison with existing prompt-optimization methods, including computational cost, sensitivity to hyperparameters, and generalizability to other domains.

Improve reproducibility by providing complete implementation-level details (pseudocode, hyperparameters, batching/minibatch procedures, and any human-in-the-loop steps) sufficient for others to apply the method to new domains.

The paper's novelty appears limited for a top-tier ML conference; either strengthen the methodological novelty (new algorithms, theory, or substantially different modeling) or explicitly reframe the submission for a journal/benchmark track.
Clarify whether the leave-one-subject-out evaluation is intended as domain generalization or domain adaptation, and justify this choice given known inter-subject variability in EMG data.

Report and justify the model selection protocol in detail (train/validation/test splits, hyperparameter tuning, and whether validation uses held-out subjects) so results are reproducible and comparable to domain generalization benchmarks.

Evaluate robustness under the experimental setups and model selection scenarios proposed by Gulrajani & Lopez-Paz (2020) and the WOODS benchmarks (Gagnon-Audet et al., 2022) to show whether reported results hold under standard OOD/ DG protocols.
Include comparisons to established domain generalization baselines and WOODS benchmark methods, or justify why those baselines are not applicable to your EMG setting.
Provide an analysis of how inter-subject variability affects performance (e.g., per-subject breakdowns, failure cases, and whether any adaptation or normalization steps mitigate these effects).

Relate the effectiveness of each normalization method to specific physiological or recording sources of EMG variability (e.g., electrode placement, perspiration, temperature changes, tissue thickness) and explain why certain methods handle particular causes better.
Include plots of raw EMG traces from multiple users alongside the same traces after each normalization method, so readers can visually assess how normalizations alter signal shape and magnitude.

Clearly state exactly how normalization statistics are computed and applied: whether means/variances are computed per training subject or globally, whether normalization is per-channel, and whether FT (fine-tune) data statistics were used to normalize all other datasets.

Report all training hyperparameters separately for pre-training and fine-tuning (learning rates, optimizers, batch sizes, weight decay, schedules) and state if the same settings were used for both stages.

Quantify the impact of pre-training on final performance (e.g., ablation or comparison with models trained only on FT data) and report results for the experiment where other-subject data are used only to compute normalization parameters while training occurs solely on FT data.
Clarify how the validation set was used: specify whether it was used only for early stopping or also for tuning other hyperparameters and selecting models.

Explain the differing confusion patterns observed in Figure 3 between Min-Max normalization and other methods, and provide analysis or hypotheses for why different class confusions emerge under different normalizations.

Revise figure captions to include detailed information on what data are plotted (pre-training/training subject data vs. fine-tuning/test/validation data), which normalization and model stage produced the plots, axis labels/units, and a legend mapping colors to classes.

Provide additional ML-relevant insights beyond empirical comparisons by relating observed performance differences to types of EMG variability and by discussing implications for model selection or deployment in real-world EMG settings.

Expand the methodology section with detailed explanations and justifications for all major choices (e.g., preprocessing steps, normalization methods, model architecture, training hyperparameters), so readers can understand and reproduce the work.

Clarify the rationale for converting normalized EMG data into heatmap images: explain how the heatmaps are constructed (mapping of channels/time to pixels, color scaling), why this representation was chosen, and how it is expected to affect the deep learning model's performance.

Specify the criteria and metrics used to select the 'best-performing' model during periodic validation in fine-tuning (e.g., validation accuracy or loss, selection frequency, early-stopping rules, and whether selection used mean/median or single best epoch).

Describe how target subjects were chosen for the leave-one-subject-out experiments: indicate whether selection was random or followed specific inclusion criteria, and explain any steps taken to ensure consistency in the normalization process across chosen subjects.

Provide a deeper analysis of why inter-subject normalization outperforms intra-subject normalization: include hypotheses, evidence (e.g., distribution comparisons, feature-space visualizations, ablation studies), and discussion of how added variability improves generalization.

Precisely define 'intra-subject normalization' in the paper: state whether it means using the same individual's data across multiple sessions, repeated movements within a single session, or something else, and describe the exact procedure used.

Replace or regenerate Figures 3 and 4 at higher resolution and clarity to eliminate blurriness and ensure all axes, labels, and data markers are legible.

Selecting different percentages of target-subject data (1%, 5%, 10%) changes the test sets and may compromise fairness; specify a fixed splitting methodology that ensures the test set is identical across percentage experiments (or otherwise justify the chosen approach).

Provide a precise description of the train/validation/test split protocol for all reported scenarios (including 'w/o RN & FT'): state exact counts or percentages, whether splits are subject-wise or sample-wise, and how the held-out test set is defined.
Define the abbreviation 'RN' and all other acronyms in the manuscript and specifically explain what 'w/o RN & FT' denotes in Table 1.

Fully describe pre-training and fine-tuning strategies: which datasets were used for pre-training, which layers/weights were frozen or fine-tuned, hyperparameters (learning rate, epochs), and exactly which data were used during fine-tuning.

Detail the intra-subject and inter-subject normalization techniques applied (or considered); if different normalization strategies were used in different scenarios, list them explicitly and report their effects on performance.

Expand the set of baseline models beyond ResNet-18 or justify why ResNet-18 is sufficient; include additional architectures (e.g., simple ConvNet from related sEMG work, other ResNet variants, lightweight/mobile models) and/or classical baselines to strengthen comparative claims.

Compare the proposed method with existing normalization approaches (for example Lin et al., 2020) that do not require heavy pre-training/fine-tuning; if direct comparison is infeasible, provide a clear rationale and discussion of expected differences.

Clean up or document the code repository: remove unrelated folders (e.g., diffusion_augmentation) or mark them as unrelated, and provide a README listing the exact scripts/files used for experiments and which models in the 'Model' folder are actually used.

Describe the EMG-to-heatmap conversion pipeline in detail (preprocessing steps, mapping procedure, parameters) and include or point to the exact code files in the repository that perform this conversion.
Linear transport maps may not capture complex, non-linear relationships in high-dimensional activations; investigate non-linear transport estimators such as neural-network-based or kernel-based maps.
If adopting non-linear estimators, evaluate and report their computational overhead and robustness; consider low-rank or other approximations to retain computational efficiency.
Evaluate the stability and robustness of composing transport maps across multiple layers; run experiments that measure error accumulation or instability when interventions are chained.
The effectiveness of ACT depends on representativeness of source and target samples; implement more rigorous sample selection procedures to better capture target distributions.
Develop and test robust estimation techniques for noisy or incomplete source/target data, for example using unbalanced optimal transport or robust statistical estimators.
Experiment with alternative cost functions (e.g., absolute deviation or p-norms) instead of squared Euclidean distance and systematically evaluate their impact on transport-map quality.
Use active learning or data augmentation strategies to improve coverage of source and target distributions used to estimate transport maps, and report gains from these techniques.
Provide empirical evidence of ACT across a broader range of behavioral modifications, including challenging and ethically sensitive tasks such as misinformation suppression and bias mitigation.
Develop and use more sophisticated evaluation metrics and benchmarks tailored to nuanced behavior changes (beyond simple classification) to better assess intervention quality.
Replace ad-hoc layer selection with a systematic method (e.g., sensitivity analysis, gradient-based influence measures) to identify the most effective layers for intervention.
Quantify ACT's impact on downstream tasks by running concrete evaluations that measure any performance degradation on representative downstream benchmarks.
Investigate whether improved data selection for estimating linear transport maps can outperform switching to non-linear estimators; run ablations comparing data-selection vs estimator-complexity.
Test ACT interventions on unseen, real-world data to assess generalization and robustness of the learned transport maps under distribution shift.
Evaluate Linear-ACT on established safety benchmarks and concrete harmful-content scenarios (e.g., hate speech, misinformation) rather than only general toxicity mitigation.
Define and report concrete safety evaluation metrics (for example, frequency and severity of harmful outputs) and compare Linear-ACT to baselines using these metrics.
Measure and report trade-offs between safety interventions and other performance aspects (e.g., generation quality, fluency, and diversity) to show impact of Linear-ACT in practice.
Directly compare Linear-ACT to vector projection methods (e.g., the method in https://arxiv.org/abs/2303.02536) by adapting that projection approach to the same tasks and reporting results.

Evaluate methods that intervene on interactions between multiple neurons (multi-neuron interventions) and compare their effectiveness and granularity of control against Linear-ACT's single-neuron adjustments.

Include comparison of interpretability and computational cost (runtime, memory, training/inference overhead) between Linear-ACT and projection-based or multi-neuron intervention methods, not only final performance metrics.

Provide empirical evidence for the claim that Linear-ACT avoids out-of-distribution activations: visualize and quantify activation distributions before and after steering and compare those distributions to other methods.
Discuss and empirically investigate potential failure cases and scenarios where Linear-ACT might not be effective, to clarify limitations and applicability.
Extend experiments beyond single-modality interventions: evaluate AcT in multi-modal settings and report how interventions interact across modalities.
Evaluate non-linear mapping functions instead of only linear mappings: test sigmoid-based transforms and more complex mappings that include cross-variable effects.
Explicitly state in the paper that focusing on single-modality and linear mappings limits applicability, and describe this limitation in the limitations/future work section.

Provide a layerwise analysis of activation distributions before and after applying AcT, comparing these distributions to the true target distribution to show how interventions affect internal representations.
Compare AcT against other intervention methods (e.g., vector-based approaches) on the same tasks with respect to how well they prevent representations from becoming OOD.

Report quantitative distributional-shift metrics (for example KL divergence, Wasserstein distance, or MMD) between post-intervention activations and the target distribution to support claims about OOD prevention.
Run an ablation study that directly compares pooling operations (average vs max vs last-token) across multiple layers and report both final task metrics and changes to internal activations.

Perform controlled ablations to disentangle the effect of pooling choice from the AcT intervention (e.g., hold AcT fixed while varying pooling, and vice versa) to isolate each component's contribution.
Add a theoretical discussion or intuition for the pooling choice: explain why average pooling might be more robust to outliers or better preserve activation distributions compared to alternatives.

The paper relies on a linear transmission assumption (Linear-ACT); investigate non-linear mappings (e.g., neural networks, kernel methods) to capture more complex activation relationships and report trade-offs in control fidelity, compute, and overfitting.
Evaluate ACT on highly multimodal activation distributions to test whether linear transport is sufficient; compare linear ACT to non-linear transport methods on these distributions.

Improve mapping generalization by increasing sample diversity: perform experiments with larger and more diverse sampling, data augmentation, or synthetic examples and measure effects on mapping quality and downstream control.

Experiment with alternative optimal-transport cost functions and unbalanced transport formulations to handle source/target supports that differ and to improve robustness to out-of-distribution inputs.

Extend the framework to multi-objective control: implement and evaluate methods to combine multiple transport maps (e.g., weighted combinations) or to learn a single transport map parameterized by control variables.
Quantify and analyze interference between conflicting objectives when combining transport maps; design experiments that measure trade-offs and failure modes when objectives conflict.

Analyze sensitivity to model/layer selection (e.g., ITI-C sensitivity): provide systematic experiments across layers and model sizes and propose automated selection heuristics or robust default choices to reduce manual tuning.

Provide a detailed computational-cost analysis: report time and memory for estimating transport maps and for inference across different model sizes, with step-by-step breakdowns and comparisons to baseline methods.

Study sensitivity to the choice of transport support (Q_o vs Q_∞): run ablations varying support choices, report effects on control quality, and provide guidelines for selecting supports for different tasks.

Evaluate potential limitations in output diversity and creativity when using ACT for concept induction: include quantitative diversity/novelty metrics and human evaluations to detect reduced creativity.

Investigate biases introduced by activation transport: audit transported outputs for demographic and semantic biases, and experiment with mitigation strategies (e.g., adversarial training, explicit bias constraints, or post-hoc correction).
For hyperparameter sensitivity (λ and others), provide systematic sweeps showing performance across ranges, identify stable regimes, and recommend practical tuning procedures or default values.

Re-evaluate the claim that the dataset is 'large-scale' or justify it with concrete comparisons; if it is not comparable to million-scale datasets, relabel it as medium-scale and explain the rationale.

For each chosen dataset, provide and discuss the eigenvalue distribution (e.g., spectrum plots or summary statistics) to connect empirical behavior to the theoretical bounds on large eigenvalues.

Expand empirical baselines to include additional optimizers such as SGD with momentum, RMSprop, and specialized GNN optimizers, and compare convergence speed and final performance against these baselines.
Explain the choice to plot Figures 1 and 2 on a log scale: justify why log scaling was used, and describe how that choice affects interpretation of convergence and performance comparisons.
Include direct runtime and scaling experiments (wall-clock time and/or complexity vs. graph size and feature dimension), using synthetic graphs if necessary, to validate theoretical runtime claims.
Discuss the limitations of HERTA's utility on small datasets (e.g., Cora and Citeseer): explain when optimization is not the bottleneck and when HERTA is or is not likely to yield benefits.
Add ablation studies that isolate the contribution of key components (preconditioner construction, spectral sparsification, etc.) so readers can determine which parts drive performance gains.
Test whether the empirical gains are driven by favorable spectral properties (e.g., a large gap between first and second eigenvalues) by reporting such metrics and correlating them with performance.
Evaluate HERTA on more complex model architectures beyond the linear f(X;W)=XW setting (e.g., multi-layer GNNs or deeper networks) to demonstrate applicability to realistic ML models.
Provide systematic hyperparameter sensitivity and stability analyses: report how performance varies with key parameters and give guidance on selecting them in practice.
Where possible, tighten theoretical bounds or provide refined statements that align with empirical observations, and clearly state remaining assumptions and limitations for practitioners.

Correct the running-time claim: do not drop the d^3 term by appealing to 'd << m' without justification. Either provide rigorous conditions/assumptions under which d^3 is negligible relative to m, or keep the full complexity O((m+nd)(log(1/ε))^2 + d^3) and discuss when each term dominates with empirical dataset examples (e.g., PubMed, ogbn-arxiv).

Empirically validate efficiency: add experiments that measure wall-clock time and peak memory (space) usage for the proposed method and baselines across datasets and model sizes to support the claimed runtime/space advantages.

Clarify and correct the statements about n_λ and its asymptotics: Definition 5.1 and Theorem 1.1 rely on n_λ = O(n/λ^2), but the claim that n_λ → n as λ → ∞ is incorrect. Precisely define n_λ, state its dependence on λ, and correct any incorrect limit statements.

Justify the connection between eigenvalues of ĤL and the regularizer Tr(Z^T ĤL Z): provide a formal argument or lemma relating the spectrum of ĤL to the spectrum (or quadratic form) of Z^T ĤL Z for arbitrary Z, or revise the text to avoid equating eigenvalue counts of ĤL with the number of eigen-directions where the regularizer is 'large.'

Replace or supplement the log(train loss - min) plots: report absolute training loss curves (not shifted by an unknown 'min'), include the reported minimum values, and/or use plots that do not amplify small negative differences so readers can assess true convergence behavior.

Fix the derivation in Appendix C: correct the ordering swap between g^(t) and ∇ℓ(ω^(t)) so that subsequent equations (C.5) and (C.8) hold, and either generalize the derivation beyond the ℓ1-norm or explicitly state that the current derivation only applies to ℓ1 regularization.
Define notation: explicitly state which operator norm ||M|| denotes in Section 3 (e.g., spectral norm / largest singular value) and include any assumptions needed for subsequent bounds.

Report predictive performance statistics: add accuracy (or other relevant metrics) and variance (e.g., mean ± std over multiple runs) for the proposed method and baselines to justify claims about generalization.

Compare against SOTA baselines: include experimental comparisons to current state-of-the-art methods on the evaluated benchmarks, and discuss relative strengths/weaknesses to support claims of effectiveness.

Move Theorem 1.1 out of the Introduction (or replace it with an informal statement). Theorem 1.1 currently references objects and objectives defined much later; either relocate the formal theorem to the section where those objects are defined or present only an informal, non-technical version in the Introduction to avoid forward references.

Reduce the level of technicality in the Introduction: remove detailed complexity analysis and instead provide an informal discussion of the core ideas behind HERTA and/or present the Unfolded GNN (Eq. 4.1) at a high level so readers understand the contribution without jumping to later sections.

Introduce the HERTA algorithm (at least an overview describing its general steps and intuition) earlier in the paper, before presenting convergence results or Theorem 5.1, so readers see the algorithmic content prior to the theoretical analysis.

Reorganize the paper to reduce forward references to later sections (many lines currently point to definitions/results later in the text). Consider restructuring so definitions, algorithm descriptions, and key objects appear before their complexity or convergence analyses to improve exposition flow.
Clarify the optimization direction in the manuscript (e.g., line 166): explicitly state whether the objective is being minimized or maximized to avoid ambiguity.

Add a code availability statement and release the implementation (or state plans to release it upon acceptance). If code cannot be released, explain why and provide sufficient implementation details to reproduce experiments.

Discuss the potential extension of TWIRLS/HERTA to the graph learning setting (jointly learning the representation Z* and the graph Laplacian L̂). Outline how HERTA would need to be adapted for joint graph learning and comment on expected challenges or directions for future work.

Address the treatment of algorithm hyperparameters (e.g., step-size, regularization λ): explain why these were not learned in a data-driven way in this work, discuss the implications of learning them (as is common in unrolled optimization), and indicate whether HERTA could be extended to learn such hyperparameters.

Clarify the definition of the H-norm in Equation 5.2: explicitly state what H is (matrix/operator), give the mathematical formula for the norm (e.g., ||x||_H = sqrt(x^T H x)), list required properties of H (positive definiteness, symmetry), and add a brief example or intuition and how this norm is used in later proofs/derivations.

Formalize the informal phrase 'with a proper step size η and number of iterations T' in the proof of Theorem 5.1: provide explicit sufficient conditions or bounds on η and T (for example η ≤ 1/L or dependences on Lipschitz/smoothness constants and required error tolerance), derive how T scales with problem parameters to guarantee the stated convergence, or, if exact bounds are difficult, state clear assumptions and cite relevant convergence lemmas.

Add experiments comparing HERTA to additional baseline methods from within the family of Unfolded Graph Neural Networks (the prior papers you cite): select representative unfolded-GNN baselines from your references, run the same evaluation protocol, and report the same performance metrics so readers can see HERTA's advantages specifically within that framework.

Include comparisons with other types of GNN architectures (e.g., GCN, GAT, GraphSAGE, message-passing networks): run these standard architectures on your tasks, report metrics such as accuracy, training runtime, convergence behavior, and model size, and provide a short discussion of where Unfolded GNNs perform better or worse.

When presenting comparisons to standard GNNs, explicitly enumerate and quantify the specific benefits of Unfolded GNNs (training speed, accuracy, interpretability, etc.): for each claimed benefit, include a concrete metric (e.g., seconds per epoch, test accuracy, number of iterations to converge, qualitative or quantitative interpretability measure) and corresponding plots/tables to support the claim.

The current definition of "sharp function" claims the max function depends on a constant number of inputs; this is incorrect. Revise the definition to remove the false claim that max inspects only a constant-size subset of inputs.

Replace or supplement the current sharpness definition with a formal notion that captures the computational process of selection (e.g., minimal number of comparisons or information needed to determine argmax), and state this definition precisely.

Theorem 2 is stated imprecisely: you write "\mathcal{X} \in \mathbb{R}^m be an m-dimensional input feature space" and later assert |\mathcal{X}| < \infty, which is a mathematical contradiction. Fix this inconsistency in the theorem statement.

Explicitly clarify whether \mathcal{X} is intended to be a continuous feature space or a finite set. If it is a finite set, define how the set is constructed; if it is a continuous space, remove any finite-cardinality assumptions.
Provide a precise definition of the term "feature" as used in the paper (is it a coordinate, a feature vector, an element of \mathcal{X}, etc.), and use consistent terminology throughout.

Clarify the relationship between the feature space \mathcal{X} and the input to the softmax layer: explicitly state what mapping or preprocessing produces the softmax inputs and how \mathcal{X} relates to those inputs.

Strengthen the mathematical rigor of Theorem 2 and its proof: list all assumptions explicitly, correct notation, and provide a complete, formal proof or move the theorem to a clearly labeled conjecture if it cannot be fully proved.

If you prefer an empirical framing, explicitly acknowledge the theoretical shortcomings in the paper, retract strong theoretical claims, and reframe the work as primarily empirical with heuristic motivations for the proposed method.

Add a more thorough discussion of the limitations and scope of applicability of the proposed mitigation method (e.g., which OOD tasks it helps, failure modes, computational costs, and assumptions required).
Audit the manuscript for mathematical typos and ambiguous notation (e.g., misuse of 'space' vs 'set', ambiguous quantifiers) and correct them to ensure each mathematical statement is well-posed.
Provide a comprehensive evaluation on real-world datasets to quantify how significant the softmax dispersion problem is in practical settings.

Strengthen empirical evidence that the adaptive-temperature mechanism yields meaningful improvements (beyond the incremental gains in Table 1 and minor visual changes in Figure 6) with clearer metrics and significance testing.

Evaluate the adaptive-temperature method on real-world NLP tasks (e.g., question answering or text summarization) to test whether it improves generalization to answers or outputs of different lengths.

Clarify the implementation details in Section 4.2: explicitly describe how adaptive temperature is applied in the CLRS-Text ablation during fine-tuning and at inference (what parameters are learned, when the module is active, and any differences between training and inference usage).

Run controlled experiments that systematically vary input length and measure performance of standard softmax versus the adaptive-temperature model to identify when the adaptive mechanism is necessary.

Provide a detailed analysis of how the softmax distribution (sharpness/entropy) changes with input length and how those changes affect generalization to longer sequences, performed on both toy and real-world datasets.

Provide a rigorous justification for the specific form of the adaptive-temperature function used (the polynomial fit); either derive it theoretically or motivate it with principled arguments relating it to relevant properties (e.g., entropy).
Evaluate alternative functional forms for the adaptive temperature (not just the polynomial), compare their performance, and report which forms are most robust across tasks and datasets.
Conduct a more careful ablation study on the CLRS-Text benchmark to isolate the effect of adaptive temperature from other confounding factors in training and model changes.
Analyze per-algorithm results to explain why the adaptive temperature is effective for some algorithms but not others, and report any algorithmic characteristics that correlate with benefit.

Address the dataset-dependence of the optimal adaptive-temperature function by proposing or implementing methods to automatically learn the function from data or by providing concrete heuristics for selecting it.
Report the computational cost (training and inference overhead) and scalability implications of computing and applying the adaptive temperature, including any latency or memory impacts.
Expand empirical validation across a diverse set of datasets to assess how dataset-dependent the softmax dispersion problem and the chosen adaptive-temperature solution are.

Improve clarity of presentation around claims that adaptive temperature 'fixes' dispersion by showing stronger, reproducible examples where dispersion materially harms performance and where the adaptive method clearly remedies it.
Provide a clear, rigorous definition of "out-of-distribution" as used in this paper, formalized in the context of softmax dispersion and the tasks considered.
Clarify whether "out-of-distribution" refers to shifts in the input feature distribution, changes in the number of input elements, or both, and state which case(s) the method targets.

If the focus includes changes in the number of input elements, specify theoretically and empirically how model performance is expected to degrade when presented with significantly larger numbers of inputs than seen during training.

Clarify and formalize the definition of a "sharp" function and explicitly explain why the max function depends on only a constant number of inputs (provide a formal argument or correct the statement).

Add experiments on more diverse, realistic datasets and tasks (e.g., standard NLP tasks such as text classification or machine translation) with varying sequence lengths to demonstrate practical applicability.
Evaluate the method on high-dimensional datasets to assess robustness and generalizability across different data characteristics.
Include scalability experiments that measure how the adaptive temperature mechanism performs (accuracy and compute) as data dimensionality and volume increase.

Provide a detailed analysis of the computational overhead introduced by the adaptive temperature mechanism (time and memory), and compare these costs to baseline techniques for mitigating softmax dispersion.

Measure and discuss the tuning complexity introduced by adaptive temperature—particularly in models with many attention heads or large-scale architectures—and suggest strategies to reduce tuning burden.
Explain concretely how the adaptive temperature mechanism addresses the specific type(s) of out-of-distribution scenarios targeted (mechanism, assumptions, and limitations).
Describe how the adaptive temperature parameter(s) are chosen, include sensitivity analyses showing performance vs. parameter values, and provide practical guidance for selecting them.
Investigate and report whether adaptive temperature can introduce instability or oscillations during training, and propose experiments or remedies to detect and mitigate such instabilities.

Assess the potential for the adaptive temperature mechanism to overfit to training data (especially with small or unrepresentative datasets) and provide experiments or regularization strategies to mitigate overfitting.

Provide a precise mathematical definition of "disperse" near the start of the paper and state formally what properties (e.g., convergence to uniformity, moments, tails) you will use in proofs and experiments.
In Lemma 2.1, clarify the meaning of the notation Θ(1/n): state whether this is asymptotic, in probability, in expectation, specify constants and the ranges of n for which it applies.
Replace or supplement the Θ(1/n) claim in Lemma 2.1 with a rigorous derivation or explicit bounds (with constants) and precise assumptions under which the bound holds.

Analyze the exponential term in equation (4): show how the bound depends on δ, provide regimes where the bound is tight or loose, and explain why the behavior does or does not reduce to Θ(1/n) for large δ.

Explicitly acknowledge and discuss the limitations of Theorem 2.2: state that it is a worst-case result and clarify the extent to which it applies to trained networks versus arbitrary parameter settings.
Extend the theoretical analysis to account for training/optimization effects or, if not possible, discuss how typical training dynamics could change the conclusions and make the assumptions explicit.

Quantify the sensitivity of softmax outputs to distributional shift: propose concrete metrics (e.g., KL divergence between softmax outputs, Lp changes in logits, or worst-case output perturbation) and use them in analysis or experiments.

Empirically verify Proposition 3.1: provide experiments that reproduce the proposition's claims, including detailed descriptions of the model architectures, datasets, random seeds, sample sizes, and evaluation metrics.
Design experiments that measure how adding irrelevant or noisy inputs affects softmax outputs and report quantitative results using the sensitivity metrics suggested.

Provide a more thorough empirical evaluation of the adaptive-temperature method: report datasets, baselines, hyperparameters, statistical significance, and ablations to explain why improvements are small.
Analyze and report the computational cost and implementation considerations of the adaptive-temperature method (runtime, memory, extra hyperparameters, and training/inference overhead).
Discuss potential biases or new failure modes introduced by adaptive temperature and propose mitigation strategies or diagnostic checks to detect such issues.
Add a dedicated preliminary/background section that introduces transformer basics (attention mechanism, multi-head attention, softmax) for readers unfamiliar with the architecture.
Introduce and motivate notation gradually instead of presenting all symbols and definitions only inside Theorem 1.
Define nonstandard terms on first use (e.g., “attentional head”, “attention coefficients”) and maintain consistent terminology throughout the paper.
Expand Figure 2 caption to explicitly state what “batch” refers to and what each row in the figure represents.
Explain the different curves and the shaded blue region in Figure 3 (what each curve measures and what the shaded area denotes).
Clarify the x-axis in Figure 7 and make the legend prominent and present for all subfigures so it cannot be easily overlooked.

Formally describe the adaptive-temperature procedure: precisely how temperature is computed and applied, and how it is applied across multiple self-attention modules and layers during inference and/or training.
Explicitly state the paper’s primary contribution (theory, algorithm, or experiments) and restructure the paper to emphasize and strengthen that component.
Provide stronger motivation and interpretation for Theorem 2: explain the practical consequences of the claimed “dispersion” of attention coefficients for downstream reasoning or retrieval tasks.

Address the specific technical scenario where the ground-truth token has coefficient O(log n / n) and other tokens O(1/n): discuss whether the increasing ratio mitigates or still causes failure, and clarify which metric (absolute mass vs ratio) determines success.

Either narrow Theorem 2’s statement or add conditions/assumptions to make it more informative (e.g., specify which types or layers of self-attention it applies to), rather than claiming an overly broad result.
Analyze whether later (deeper) transformer layers suffer more or less from the dispersion phenomenon—provide layer-wise theory or empirical analysis to support any claims.

Acknowledge that adaptive temperature does not fundamentally solve dispersion and either improve the algorithm or clearly delineate its limitations and the scenarios where it provides meaningful gains.
Strengthen experimental evaluation beyond the max-retrieval task to demonstrate generality to other reasoning tasks and datasets.
For the CLRS-Text experiments, run ablations that separate effects of applying adaptive temperature during fine-tuning versus only at inference, and report which produces the observed gains.

Provide experiments or analyses that quantify ‘sharpness’ (or other proposed explanatory metrics) and correlate those measures with downstream performance to support claims about approximating sharpness.
Discuss the broader implications of your results for general reasoning tasks and clarify what practitioners should change (if anything) in model design or usage based on your findings.
Add citations and discussion of potentially relevant related work, specifically literature on length generalization and the ‘attention sink’ phenomenon, and explain how your contributions differ.

c_gen.text: Define and explain the mathematical notations (e.g., \mathcal{T}, \mathcal{U}, \mathcal{V}) when first introduced: state what each operator/matrix represents, how it is constructed from the source/target covariances, and give an intuitive description or small example of their roles in the analysis.

Clarify the definitions and roles of the effective ranks r_k and R_k: provide intuition about their scales, give concrete examples of typical values, and explain precisely how different ranges of these quantities affect the excess risk bounds and practical performance.

Report full experimental details to improve reproducibility: specify the data generation procedure, sample sizes, noise distribution and level, random seeds, hyperparameter settings (e.g., ridge regularization parameter), evaluation metrics, and whether results are averaged over multiple runs.

Explicitly list the eigenvalues (\lambda_j) used for the source and target covariance matrices in each simulation: include the exact sequences or parameterization (e.g., polynomial/exponential decay rates), and justify these choices as representative of the regimes studied.

Describe the procedure used to select the number of principal components in PCR in the experiments: state whether k was chosen by cross-validation, by fixed rule, or otherwise, and report the chosen k values and sensitivity of results to this choice.

Include an explicit in-distribution baseline in the experiments: run and report the same analyses under no covariate shift so readers can directly compare in-distribution and OOD performance and verify that the OOD phenomena claimed are distinct from standard behavior.

Analyze and report settings where PCR does not outperform ridge regression: either provide theoretical discussion or run experiments where the signal is concentrated in minor directions of the source (or otherwise misaligned) to show the limitations and trade-offs of PCR.

Quantify sensitivity to the condition number L of the source covariance: characterize how bounds and empirical performance degrade as L grows, and provide practical guidance or remedies (e.g., recommended regularization, preconditioning, or dimensionality reduction) for ill-conditioned source covariances.

Provide an explicit comparison between your bounds and prior results (e.g., Tsigler & Bartlett 2023; Ge et al. 2024): state which assumptions differ, show how constants and rates match or differ, and, where possible, give inequality-level comparisons that demonstrate recovery of prior bounds.

Expand the conclusion: more fully summarize the main theoretical and empirical takeaways, discuss practical implications for ML practitioners, state clear limitations of the current work, and outline specific directions for future research.

Connect the linear-model results to the non-parametric OOD literature: add a discussion that compares mechanisms and guarantees, explains which insights might extend to non-parametric settings, and identify obstacles to such extensions.

c_real.text: Add simulation experiments that reproduce and confirm the theoretical convergence rates O(1/√n) and O(1/n) (e.g., using a small Gaussian example) to support the proofs empirically.
Systematically vary the quantity ||T|| in simulations and report how changes in ||T|| affect the observed excess risk and convergence rates.
Systematically vary the ratio tr[U]/tr[V] in simulations and report its effect on the observed excess risk and convergence rates.
Plot excess risk versus sample size n under different parameter settings (||T|| and tr[U]/tr[V]) to provide visual confirmation of the theoretical predictions.

Include simulation studies to investigate selection or tuning of the ridge regularization parameter, showing how the choice impacts excess risk and whether theoretical guidance matches empirical optima.

Investigate PCR performance when the number of components k is misspecified: run simulations with varying k (both under- and over-specified) and quantify how excess risk degrades as k deviates from the optimal value.
Study the impact of the eigengap (λ_k - λ_{k+1}) on PCR via simulations: generate covariance matrices with different eigenvalue spacings and report how PCR excess risk depends on the eigengap.

Clarify in the paper whether the PCR theoretical results assume the number of relevant components k is known a priori and explicitly discuss (or provide theory/simulations for) the effect of mis-specifying k.

Extend simulations beyond Gaussian linear models to assess robustness: include non-Gaussian error distributions, non-linear relationships between covariates and response, and model misspecification scenarios to test how well the theoretical results generalize.
Derive a matching lower bound for the out-of-distribution (OOD) setting analogous to the in-distribution lower bound (Tsigler & Bartlett, 2023), or clearly explain why such a bound may not exist.
Provide a focused analysis of the variance term in the OOD risk and develop any new techniques necessary to obtain a matching lower bound (the current upper-bound analysis is insufficient).
Explicitly discuss the main technical obstacles in proving a matching lower bound for OOD and whether the proof techniques from the in-distribution case can be adapted or must be replaced.
Characterize the conditions under which the derived upper bound is tight, and provide concrete examples or constructions where the bound is achieved.
Provide empirical experiments or synthetic examples that demonstrate when the upper bound is attained (or nearly attained) to validate tightness claims.

Investigate alternative estimators or regularization schemes that may be more robust to covariate shift, and compare their theoretical and empirical performance against ridge regression and principal component regression.

Discuss how much the covariance-alignment-based analysis (which relies on square loss and linear estimators) is expected to transfer to other tasks, specifically propose extensions or counterexamples for linear classification.
Either extend the theoretical results to more general settings (e.g., non-linear models or other types of covariate shift) or clearly state the limitations and barriers to such extensions.
Provide a more detailed comparison with prior work: clearly state which limitations of previous approaches are overcome, which arguments are reused, and which parts of the analysis are novel.
Give a more thorough discussion of the assumptions used (e.g., distributional, model, and covariance alignment assumptions), including their practical implications and scenarios where they may fail.

Clarify and correct the statement about over-parameterized models and LLM training (L39-42): distinguish parameter/token counts, cite relevant sources (e.g., Brown et al.), and explain how this distinction affects the applicability of the paper's over-parameterization regime.
Explicitly discuss the limitations of the theoretical analysis for real-world applications and provide practical guidance for practitioners on when and how to apply the results.
Add a dedicated section or subsection near the beginning that summarizes all key assumptions used throughout the paper.
Move or restate the assumptions that underlie each main result out of the appendix and into the main text (either inside the theorem statement or immediately before it).
Explicitly include in Theorem 5 the additional assumptions it relies on (for example, that β^*_{-k} = 0 and that there is a large overlap gap between major and minor directions).
Include simulation studies that validate and illustrate the theoretical findings rather than presenting only asymptotic or analytical results.
Run experiments on real-world datasets (when feasible) in addition to synthetic simulations to demonstrate practical relevance of the theoretical claims.

Design simulations that systematically vary key parameters (e.g., magnitude of covariate shift, regularization parameter, degrees of shift in major and minor directions) to show how these factors affect excess risk.
Provide clear visualizations (plots) in the experiments to display the theoretical rates and how performance changes with different parameter values.
Expand the discussion in Section 3.1 to give more intuition on why the stated conditions result in slower rates of convergence.
Add concrete examples or counterexamples that illustrate the importance of the conditions in Section 3.1 and how violating them changes the rates.
Discuss whether the conditions for slow rates (Section 3.1 / Theorem 2) are merely sufficient or also necessary, and, if possible, provide formal or empirical evidence for necessity.

Explicitly discuss the limitation that PCR's fast rates follow directly from the assumption that the true signal lies primarily in major directions; add examples or simulations where this assumption fails and PCR performs poorly.

Perform a sensitivity analysis for the choice of cutoff k: show how predictive performance changes with k, quantify degradation from misspecification, and provide guidance for practitioners on safe ranges of k.

Develop or evaluate adaptive estimators/selection procedures that do not require knowing the true k a priori (e.g., cross-validation, information criteria, shrinkage-based PCA, or regularized PCA) and report their theoretical/empirical performance.
Either prove an adaptive fast-rate result for PCR (an estimator that attains the fast rate without knowing k) or clearly state and prove the technical barriers that prevent such adaptivity.
Attempt to generalize the instance-specific lower bound for ridge regression to a broader class of instances, or else provide a clear statement of why a general lower bound is not attainable.

Elaborate on the main mathematical obstacles to deriving a general lower bound for ridge regression under covariate shift, identifying which properties of covariance matrices or shift operators break existing proof techniques.
Characterize special cases or sufficient conditions under which ridge regression can achieve fast rates despite large shifts in minor directions; give concrete examples or theoretical conditions.

Explore and compare alternative methods that do not rely on the strong signal-in-major-directions assumption (e.g., shrinkage estimators, domain-adaptive regularization), both theoretically and empirically.

Include an explicit discussion and empirical comparison of computational costs for PCR, ridge regression, and any proposed adaptive methods, so readers can weigh practicality against statistical guarantees.

Clarify the paper's technical novelty and contributions relative to existing work on over-parameterized models and OOD/covariate shift, so non-expert readers and reviewers can assess the incremental advance.
