matches.gen_text: Provide an explicit comparison between your bounds and prior results (e.g., Tsigler & Bartlett 2023; Ge et al. 2024): state which assumptions differ, show how constants and rates match or differ, and, where possible, give inequality-level comparisons that demonstrate recovery of prior bounds.
matches.real_text: Clarify the paper's technical novelty and contributions relative to existing work on over-parameterized models and OOD/covariate shift, so non-expert readers and reviewers can assess the incremental advance.

c_gen.text: Provide full implementation details and reproducible algorithmic description: include pseudocode or an algorithm box in the main text/appendix, and list all training hyperparameters (optimizer type, learning rate schedule, batch size, number of training steps/epochs, weight decay, gradient clipping), model architecture details (which layers are unfrozen/frozen), random seeds, and exact data preprocessing steps.

Clarify baseline implementations and ensure fair comparisons: explicitly state how DPO, IPO, MORL and other baselines were configured (mixing ratios of helpfulness and safety data, whether the same reference/pre-aligned model and tokenizer were used, hyperparameters, number of training steps), and rerun comparisons where necessary to match your experimental setup.

Add statistical rigor to empirical results: report means and standard deviations across multiple random seeds for all metrics, include confidence intervals, and perform appropriate significance tests (e.g., t-tests or bootstrap tests) to demonstrate that improvements are statistically significant.

Justify and analyze the labeling function constants: provide a principled rationale or derivation for chosen constants (e.g., B1=3, B3=0.5), and include a sensitivity analysis showing how varying these constants affects final helpfulness and harmlessness.

Evaluate and report reliability of the safety classifier: measure the classifier’s error rate and calibration on your datasets by comparing its labels to human judgments (report confusion matrix, precision/recall), and discuss how classifier mislabeling could affect training and evaluation.

Provide detailed compute-efficiency measurements: quantify the 'less than 10% compute' claim with concrete metrics (FLOPs, GPU-hours, wall-clock time, number of training iterations) and present side-by-side compute and cost comparisons to each baseline using the same hardware setup.

Include failure-mode and trade-off analysis: describe scenarios where Bi-FAPO underperforms (edge cases), present examples of failure cases, and analyze trade-offs between safety and helpfulness including any observed regressions when optimizing for one objective.

Demonstrate generalizability to other objectives and non-bilinear combinations: either extend experiments to additional conflicting objectives (e.g., truthfulness, politeness) or provide theoretical discussion/derivation showing how the method would generalize beyond a bilinear safety-helpfulness combination.

Test on additional model architectures and sizes: evaluate Bi-FAPO on other widely used base models (e.g., Llama, Falcon, different sizes) to support claims about general applicability and report any architecture-specific adjustments required.

Report how pre-aligned models were used and control for their effect: clearly state whether and how pre-aligned models (e.g., Zephyr-7b-beta) were used as initialization or references in both your method and baselines, and provide ablations showing the impact of starting from pre-aligned vs. non-aligned weights.

Provide ablation results for the shift parameter alpha on helpfulness: beyond reporting its effect on generative harmlessness, show how varying alpha affects helpfulness metrics and demonstrate whether larger alpha values risk overfitting to safety at the expense of helpfulness.

Disclose evaluation dependence on external benchmarks and classifiers: discuss limitations introduced by using particular public benchmarks (UltraFeedback, PKU-SafeRLHF) and classifier models, and, where feasible, validate main findings with human evaluations or alternative benchmarks to reduce reliance on a single automated evaluator.

c_real.text: Revise the claim that the method 'does not need human annotations'—explicitly acknowledge that the public preference datasets used were created via human annotation and rephrase to state that the method reduces the need for new human annotations rather than eliminating human involvement.

Provide a general rewrite of the method section to improve clarity: remove unexplained annotations, define all symbols when first used, and ensure each algorithmic step is described in plain language before introducing formal notation.
Expand Equation 5: include a step-by-step derivation or detailed explanation of each term, its origin, and how it relates to the overall objective instead of only citing prior work.

Clarify the meaning of 'softmax' applied to θ on L347: state precisely what θ represents (model parameters or logits), how softmax is used, and how this yields a probability distribution in your formulation.
Explicitly define the action space used by the model: list its elements, dimensionality, and how actions map to model outputs or decisions in your framework.

Clarify the term 'buffered training': explain why you chose this name, describe the exact buffering mechanism (e.g., sampling strategy from safety and helpfulness datasets), and justify any connection or distinction from buffering concepts in RL.

Justify the decision to freeze selected LLM layers for fine-tuning: provide theoretical or empirical reasons for this choice and discuss potential biases or limitations introduced by freezing versus training all parameters.

Evaluate BFPO using PEFT methods (e.g., LoRA) as an additional baseline or ablation: include experiments showing BFPO performance with LoRA (and optionally other PEFTs) to demonstrate fairness and robustness of your fine-tuning strategy.

Provide a detailed comparison between your fine-tuning strategy and alternative approaches (full-finetuning, LoRA, other PEFTs): report differences in compute, parameter updates, sample efficiency, and final performance to assess the impact on experimental results.

Clarify the difference between your label function and the reward model used in Llama (helpfulness/safety): describe both formulations (e.g., piecewise function), explain how they influence training, and state why your label function is preferable or different in effect.

If claiming that existing preference datasets can be combined without new annotations, provide experiments or analyses that quantify how much new annotation effort is avoided (e.g., ablation showing performance vs. amount of new annotated data).
Ablation experiments are incomplete; provide a more comprehensive ablation study rather than the current limited experiments.
Alpha (α) is only tested in two scenarios ('\u03b1=0 only' and '\u03b1=0 & no buffer'); expand experiments beyond these two setups.
The manuscript is inconsistent about \u03b1 (e.g. \u03b1=0.5 in §3.4 vs. \u03b1=-0.5 in §4.2); reconcile and clearly report the \u03b1 values used in each experiment and their rationale.

Run a systematic sweep over \u03b1 (suggested values: 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9), report helpfulness and harmlessness for each value in a table or graph, and analyze trends to identify any optimal range and explain why certain \u03b1 values perform better or worse.

Perform sensitivity experiments for the temperature/scale hyperparameter \u03c4 (suggested values: 0.01, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0), report effects on helpfulness and harmlessness, and analyze how \u03c4 affects training dynamics, convergence, and final performance.
Investigate interactions between \u03b1 and \u03c4 via a grid search (multiple combinations), analyze for synergistic or antagonistic effects, and report which combinations yield best trade-offs.
Report and discuss the computational cost of tuning \u03b1 and \u03c4 and provide practical strategies for efficient hyperparameter selection (e.g., coarse-to-fine search, resource-aware defaults).
Run experiments on diverse, real-world, publicly available datasets instead of relying solely on a synthetic dataset.
Include datasets that require complex reasoning, multi-turn dialogues, or external knowledge to test BFPO on more realistic tasks.
Evaluate BFPO on datasets known to be challenging for existing alignment methods to demonstrate robustness.
Report both aggregate metrics and a detailed per-subset analysis (e.g., by task type or difficulty) to highlight strengths and weaknesses.
Improve clarity of exposition: define all technical terms and explain the method steps more precisely.
Provide complete experimental details for reproducibility: hyperparameters, training procedure, random seeds, and evaluation metrics.
Add visualizations illustrating key results and trade-offs—for example, a figure showing the safety vs. helpfulness trade-off compared to baseline methods.
Provide a detailed description of the 'safety' and 'helpfulness' labels: exact definitions, label schema, and how labels are assigned.
Demonstrate label consistency and reliability: report inter-annotator agreement or other validation measures for the labeling process (or for the synthetic labeling function).
Clarify Section 3, Equation (7): explicitly define g(x,y) (or correct the omission) and explain its role in the equation.
Discuss limitations of BFPO in capturing subtle differences in complex human preferences and how labeling choices may introduce biases.
Analyze failure cases where BFPO does not capture nuanced human preferences and provide insights into the underlying causes.
Explore and evaluate alternative labeling functions or preference-capture methods that might mitigate identified limitations.
Explain the result in Table 1 where Alpaca outperforms BFPO for DPO: provide analysis and possible reasons for this outcome.

Fix inconsistent notation: unify reward and score notation (e.g., use r(y|x) and g(y|x) everywhere), define symbols used (including y ≻ π) and add a notation table explaining each symbol and its meaning.

Extend Appendix B.4 with an ablation/sensitivity analysis that measures how varying B1 affects model performance (use quantitative metrics) rather than only giving the algebraic constraint B3(B1-1)=1.
Provide an ablation/sensitivity study for B3 showing how changes in B3 (respecting the constraint) impact helpfulness and harmlessness metrics.

Run a grid search or controlled experiments over B1, B2, and B3 to characterize their interactions and report the resulting trade-offs between helpfulness and harmlessness (visualize as curves or a Pareto frontier).

Explain the practical implications of the theoretical equivalence to multi-objective RLHF: explicitly state expected effects on training stability, sample efficiency, computational cost, and algorithmic design choices.
Add a discussion of limitations: analyze potential biases introduced by the label function and quantify sensitivity to the choice and quality of reward models.
Compare BFPO to other multi-objective optimization techniques in the literature, listing concrete advantages and disadvantages (e.g., convergence properties, implementation complexity, scalability).
Expand experiments with a qualitative error analysis: categorize typical error types, provide representative example prompts and model outputs, and explain why the model failed in each case.

Investigate and report cases where the model unnecessarily sacrifices helpfulness for safety (and vice versa): identify patterns or prompt types that trigger this behavior and suggest mitigation strategies.

c_gen.text: Evaluate 3DTrajMaster on real-world video datasets (or real captured multi-view sequences) to demonstrate generalization beyond the synthetic 360°-Motion Dataset.
Compare against existing 3D-aware or 3D-based video generation methods (not only 2D baselines) to establish the novelty and relative performance of the proposed 3D motion control.
Perform a comprehensive ablation isolating the domain adaptor and the annealed sampling strategy, reporting per-component impacts on motion accuracy and video quality.

Provide a direct comparison of the gated self-attention fusion with alternative fusion strategies (e.g., cross-attention, multi-head attention, simple concatenation), including quantitative metrics and qualitative examples.
Report statistical significance (e.g., confidence intervals or p-values) and run multiple seeds for key experiments to show robustness of reported improvements.
Include quantitative evaluations for non-human entities (animals, cars, robots) rather than only humans, and analyze how well entity editing works across these categories.

Explain and justify the constraint to a maximum of three entities, and provide experiments or analysis on scalability to more entities (e.g., failure modes, memory/compute scaling, or proposed solutions).
Fully document the GPT-based trajectory generation pipeline: provide the prompts used, how spline interpolation is applied, how orientations are derived, and any postprocessing steps.
Validate the realism and diversity of GPT-generated trajectory templates using quantitative metrics (e.g., distributional comparison to real trajectories) or human perceptual studies.

Provide detailed architecture and training details for the pose encoder (layer sizes, activation, downsampling ratios, training losses, learning rates, and any pretraining), and ablate design choices.
Clarify why the domain adaptor is frozen during object injector training and include an ablation that compares frozen vs. fine-tuned adaptor to measure the impact of this choice.

Demonstrate or analyze how the model handles occlusion (especially multi-entity occlusions) and, if not robust, propose concrete strategies (e.g., occlusion-aware training, additional supervision) to improve it.

Provide more details and validation on the 360°-Motion Dataset construction (asset selection, rendering parameters, camera rigs, and any biases introduced by UE/GPT choices) and release dataset details for reproducibility.
Ablate and report different annealing schedules and sampling hyperparameters to quantify the trade-off between motion adherence and visual quality, and recommend default settings.

If synthetic-to-real domain gap is a concern, evaluate domain adaptation techniques (e.g., fine-tuning on small real datasets, domain randomization, or diffusion-domain alignment) and report their effect.

c_real.text: Cite ControlNet and explicitly discuss its relevance to injecting conditional control into image/video diffusion models.
Expand the related work to include techniques that inject control signals into video foundation models via additional encoders that project control inputs into latent embeddings.
Include and discuss methods that achieve control by directly manipulating attention maps or video latents, not only encoder-based approaches.
Clarify and document how control signals are integrated into the base model (e.g., concatenation, addition, insertion) and which integration strategies were attempted or considered.
Compare the strengths and weaknesses of encoder-based control modules (learnable conv/linear/attention layers or frozen pre-trained encoders) versus attention/latent-manipulation approaches.
Broaden the dataset beyond human/animal categories and urban scenes to include generic object categories and diverse environments (e.g., forests, deserts, mountains).
Expand the dataset's motion diversity to include non-human/non-animal movements and more complex multi-object interactions.
Apply data augmentation strategies such as domain randomization or synthetic data generation to increase training diversity without prohibitive manual annotation.
Analyze and state whether the method is restricted to a small number of entities (e.g., N=2) and whether that restriction stems from training data distribution or model design.
Provide quantitative experiments or ablations that evaluate performance as the number of controlled entities increases (e.g., N=1,2,4,8).
Discuss concrete strategies to scale control to many entities, for example by using more powerful text encoders or new mechanisms to associate trajectories with specific text entities.
Add a detailed limitations section that documents challenges in generating videos with many diverse entities and how these limitations relate to the underlying video foundation model.
Relying primarily on synthetic data may hinder real-world generalization; evaluate and mitigate this reliance.
Using a dataset constrained to a 'city' setting limits output diversity; expand training domains beyond the city environment.
Unclear how the model performs beyond the MatrixCity platform; validate on external, real-world 3D/4D datasets.
No real-world evaluations or human perceptual feedback are reported; run user studies or human evaluations for perceptual quality.
Do not compare against recent 4D generation baselines (e.g., TC4D); add direct comparisons with such trajectory-controllable methods.
Clarify whether dataset and code will be released publicly after acceptance.
Expand training data to include a wider range of environments, object categories, and motion patterns.
Incorporate synthetic data variations that simulate different lighting, weather, and object-interaction conditions.
Increase the number and variety of assets to cover more object categories and associated motion characteristics.
Apply domain adaptation techniques (e.g., adversarial training) to reduce the synthetic-to-real domain gap.
Include at least small amounts of real-world video data in training or finetuning to improve generalization.
Develop methods to generate more complex, non-linear 3D trajectories rather than relying on simple linear motions.
Evaluate model robustness across variations in camera angles, lighting conditions, backgrounds, and occlusions.
Use data augmentation strategies to improve robustness to visual and motion variations.
Evaluate and report the model's ability to handle interactions among multiple entities in the scene.
Investigate transfer learning approaches to adapt the model to new environments and object types.
Include qualitative analyses (example videos, failure cases) assessing visual quality, realism, and adherence to specified 3D trajectories.
When comparing to baselines, report metrics focused on trajectory-control accuracy, perceptual realism, and computational efficiency.
Explore and report advanced evaluation metrics that better capture human perceptual quality (e.g., human-rated scores or perceptual-model-based metrics).
Expand dataset environments beyond the current City setting (e.g., add forests, deserts, indoor scenes) to improve generalizability.
Broaden the set of motion types beyond walking/jumping to include running, dancing, sports, social interactions, and navigation on complex terrain.
Acquire or synthesize a wider range of motion sequences (additional motion-capture datasets or procedurally generated motions) to realize the expanded motion diversity.
Resolve prevalent foot skating/floating by improving synchronization between entities' local (relative) motion and their global motion trajectories.
Align motion-capture animations explicitly with the 3D scene geometry during data generation so feet remain grounded and motion is spatially consistent.
Apply inverse kinematics (IK) to adjust limb poses based on global trajectories to produce more realistic foot placement and reduce sliding/floating.
Introduce pose and velocity constraints during data generation or training (e.g., limits on joint velocities, continuity constraints) to prevent unnatural movements.
Enforce explicit feet-ground contact checks or constraints (e.g., require contact events or bound the vertical distance of feet to ground) to eliminate floating artifacts.

Remove or justify the term 'ControlNet-like' for the Object Injector: either rename it or provide an accurate description of its architecture, initialization, and functional differences from a true ControlNet module.

Describe the Object Injector's exact placement (it is after the 2D spatial self-attention layer) and explain how that placement affects its behavior compared to a parallel zero-initialized ControlNet module.

Clarify and justify the statement on Line 334 that a static motion prompt 'amplifies movement' and that this reflects learning of 3D motion representations—provide mechanistic reasoning or empirical evidence.

Add analyses of the model's internal representations (e.g., activation patterns, ablations, or probing experiments) to support claims about learning 3D motion representations and responses to motion prompts.

Replace or supplement human-only pose evaluation with quantitative pose accuracy metrics: run a 3D pose estimator on generated videos, compare estimated poses to the Unreal Engine ground-truth, and report measures such as average joint distance error.

Investigate the poor alligator gait: analyze why the alligator's legs appear static and include diagnostics (e.g., frame-by-frame pose comparisons, visualization of predicted vs. ground-truth joint motions) to pinpoint the failure mode.
Evaluate the method with weaker, open-source base video generation models to test generality: run experiments on one or more less-capable models and report performance differences and failure cases.

Perform a systematic analysis of failure cases to identify patterns: characterize which types of trajectories, motion styles, or entity interactions lead to poor results and quantify their prevalence.
Expand the evaluation dataset by generating videos with a wider range of trajectories, entity types, and interactions so limitations can be identified across diverse scenarios.

Conduct ablation studies to determine which components are critical for success and which contribute to failures; report quantitative and qualitative differences when components are removed or altered.
Provide detailed computational resource and reproducibility information: report training time, hardware specs (GPUs/TPUs, CPU), memory usage, and inference costs for the reported experiments.

Discuss and/or experimentally evaluate scalability: assess whether the method can handle larger numbers of entities and more complex scenes, and describe limitations or necessary adaptations for scaling.

c_real.text: Provide a clear explanation (intuitive and/or theoretical) of why knowledge learned from one graph can transfer to an unseen and unrelated graph; justify why the LinearGNN probes and the permutation/dimension-invariant attention should capture "common" graph knowledge.

Augment the experimental baselines with stronger, more recent methods that handle both homophilous and heterophilous graphs (e.g., include the methods from refs [1],[2],[3] recommended by the reviewer).
Include ACM-GNN (or other heterophily-focused architectures explicitly mentioned) as an additional baseline to better evaluate performance on heterophilous graphs.
Expand the set of graph operations used in LinearGNN beyond {X, AX, A^2X, (I-A)X, (I-A)^2X}; concretely experiment with Chebyshev polynomial graph filters to capture multi-hop information.

Incorporate normalized Laplacian (and other Laplacian-based) operations as additional LinearGNN probes to provide alternate views of connectivity.

Clarify the reported time complexity in Table 1 by describing the computational cost of computing the pseudo-inverse in LinearGNN; if pseudo-inverse is used, provide its complexity, implementation details, and any approximations or optimizations used.
Perform a targeted failure-mode analysis on the large datasets: test whether the model fails on specific motifs/subgraphs or structural patterns and report results broken down by such patterns.

Analyze whether specific node feature distributions cause the attention module to misbehave (e.g., feature skewness, sparsity, or scale); report diagnostics and, if necessary, apply preprocessing/normalization to mitigate these issues.

Experiment with potential remedies for poor generalization on large graphs: try more sophisticated attention mechanisms, preprocessing steps, alternate training strategies (e.g., curriculum learning or adversarial training), and study the effect of training-graph size on performance with proposed mitigation strategies.

Provide a step-by-step description (including any preprocessing and normalization) of the procedure used to generate Figure 5 so the experiment is reproducible.
List and describe all learnable parameters in the attention module (e.g., weight matrices, biases) and state their dimensionalities.

Report and discuss per-dataset results where GAT outperforms GraphAny (18/31 graphs): explain reasons for inferior performance on these datasets and any patterns observed.
Include an ablation study for the hyperparameter t: show how performance varies with different t values across multiple datasets and provide guidance for choosing t.
Clarify whether LinearGNNs are intended as learned models or simply an analytical data-preprocessing step; explicitly differentiate the contribution from preprocessing versus model learning.
Avoid or justify the claim that GraphAny is the 'first fully-inductive' method—define 'fully-inductive' precisely and explain why other analytically solvable models would not meet that definition.
Cite and compare to recent related work that also targets fully-inductive transfer (e.g., GraphControl [WWW24]); explicitly discuss differences and where GraphAny improves upon them.

State clearly whether LinearGNN parameters are solved using any labeled data on each downstream dataset (i.e., zero-shot vs. requiring per-dataset labels) and describe the exact procedure used to obtain those parameters.

If GraphAny uses downstream labels to solve LinearGNN parameters, run and report fair comparisons against semi-supervised baselines with the same labeled-data budget and explicitly discuss how pre-training advantages affect results.
Provide a quantitative analysis of the computational cost of analytically solving LinearGNNs for each new dataset (time/memory) and compare it to the training cost of competing GNNs.
Explain the inductive attention mechanism in more detail: what graph patterns it is intended to capture, how attention weights are computed, and why those patterns should transfer across domains.
Visualize attention weights (e.g., heatmaps or example graphs) to show which LinearGNNs the model selects for different graph types and how those selections correlate with performance.
Evaluate sensitivity of GraphAny to the choice and diversity of LinearGNN encoders: run ablations that vary the set of LinearGNNs and report performance variance.
Analyze the effect of the number of LinearGNNs on performance and computational cost (scaling study) to justify the chosen ensemble size.
Add a dedicated limitations section discussing potential weaknesses such as sensitivity to LinearGNN quality, dependence on downstream labels (if any), and scalability concerns.
If downstream labels are used to compute LinearGNN parameters, explicitly state the number/percentage of labeled examples used per dataset and justify that choice.

Provide a rigorous analysis of trade-offs between the analytical LinearGNN approach and traditional trained GNNs (accuracy vs. compute vs. data requirements), supported by experiments or complexity estimates.
Include the GFM ("One for All") baseline or other single-model-for-all-datasets approaches to evaluate whether GraphAny outperforms models explicitly trained to generalize across graph datasets.

Add baselines that use meta-learning or domain-adaptation techniques for cross-dataset generalization (even if not designed for arbitrary graphs) to strengthen the comparison in the fully-inductive setting.

Compare GraphAny to a baseline that combines the five LinearGNN components using random or fixed (e.g., uniform) coefficients to isolate and quantify the benefit of the learned inductive attention mechanism.

Compare DIT to additional offline-RL baselines that could be adapted to in-context use (e.g., CQL, BCQ, AWAC), and explain how those baselines were adapted if necessary.

Run a fair comparison where DIT and baseline methods (e.g., DPT) both use optimal historical data, report whether DIT still outperforms, and analyze the source of any advantage.

Design experiments that probe whether the model learns an explicit representation of τ by querying with task-indicative states or sequences (e.g., specific start locations or action sequences leading to landmarks) and analyze model outputs.
Systematically evaluate sensitivity to the quality and quantity of in-context examples by varying the number of demonstration trajectories and measuring performance.
Assess robustness to noisy or sub-optimal in-context examples by injecting noise or sub-optimal behaviors into demonstrations and reporting the effect on performance.
Investigate sensitivity to the order and formatting of in-context examples (prompt order effects) and report how ordering impacts performance.

Include a discussion of limitations of in-context learning for RL, specifically sensitivity to prompt choice/order and potential failure to generalize to tasks substantially different from pretraining.
Evaluate and report model performance on a more diverse set of tasks (different dynamics and reward structures) to test for overfitting to the pretraining task distribution.

Make notation consistent: if π(a | s; τ) and π_τ(a | s) denote the same concept, choose one notation or formally define one in terms of the other.

Include DPT as a baseline in the bandit experiments and report comparative results so readers can assess DIT versus transformer-based methods in the bandit setting.
Provide a quantitative analysis of the computational overhead introduced by the advantage-based weighting mechanism, including training time and memory usage comparisons versus baselines.

Discuss how DIT specifically differs from or improves upon advantage-weighted/offline-RL methods such as A2PR and AW/RW in the related work and methodology sections, and add experiments if possible to illustrate empirical differences.

Broaden experiments to test generalization across different types of suboptimal data (e.g., data from different behavioral policies, varying noise levels, levels of suboptimality) to characterize when DIT helps or fails.

Discuss limitations and potential biases introduced by the in-context learning paradigm (e.g., how in-context prompts or transformer inductive biases might skew advantage estimates or policy behavior) and propose or evaluate mitigation strategies.

The paper does not demonstrate sufficient novelty relative to existing exponential advantage-weighted regression methods (e.g., AWAC, AWR) and conservative policy iteration; explicitly justify what is new beyond applying these ideas to an in-context setting.

c_gen.text: Expand SFT experiments beyond a single model and single epoch: run SFT on multiple MLLMs and with varied training durations/learning schedules, and report results to assess robustness of the approach.
Include human evaluation of model responses—particularly for subjective categories like genre and creative—to validate automated judgments and capture nuances automated judges may miss.

Explicitly define the instruction complexity levels (basic, intermediate, advanced, creative, complex) and document the criteria used for each level.
Provide a detailed dataset breakdown: counts per sub-instruction category, counts per complexity level, distribution of image sources, and any selection criteria used for the 400 image-prompt pairs.
Open-source the MIA-Bench dataset and evaluation code (or provide a concrete release timeline and link) so others can reproduce experiments and adopt the benchmark.
Conduct and report a granular error analysis: categorize the types of failures for each instruction category (e.g., omission, incorrect detail, format violations) and provide examples.
Quantify agreement between automated judges and other evaluators: report statistical agreement metrics (e.g., Cohen’s kappa, Spearman/Pearson correlations) between GPT-4o, Claude-3, and human raters.

Describe the manual validation procedure for the SFT data in detail: how were the 100 sampled responses selected, what checklist or criteria were used to verify instruction adherence, and who performed the checks?
Test the SFT approach on additional models (for example Phi-3-Vision, CogVLM2) and provide statistical tests of significance to demonstrate generalizability of the SFT gains.
Complement GPT-4o scoring with alternative evaluation metrics (e.g., human rankings, task-specific automatic metrics) and report comparative results to mitigate potential judge bias.

c_real.text: Evaluate the SFT approach on multiple MLLM architectures and sizes (not only LLaVA‑NeXT) to test whether the reported improvements generalize across models.
Systematically vary the SFT training dataset size and composition (e.g., different ratios of instruction types) and report how these changes affect performance on each instruction category.
Assess and report evidence of overfitting from the SFT procedure (e.g., performance gaps between training, validation, and held‑out benchmarks or cross‑model transfer tests).
Experiment with data augmentation and adversarial training during fine‑tuning to improve robustness and evaluate their impact on instruction adherence.
Perform a detailed error analysis of MIA‑Bench results across multiple models and instruction categories to identify systematic failure modes and potential benchmark design biases.
Generate and evaluate adversarial or slightly modified instructions to probe MIA‑Bench robustness and determine whether small formulation changes cause large performance shifts.
Release the MIA‑Bench dataset and benchmark code to enable external validation and independent analyses of potential biases.
Do not rely on Claude‑3‑Opus as the sole automatic evaluator; include multiple diverse reference models when using model‑based scoring to detect evaluator‑specific biases.
Include human evaluation (or a mixed human+automated protocol) for a subset of tasks to obtain a more nuanced ground truth for instruction adherence.
Investigate ensemble scoring methods (combining multiple evaluators) to reduce the impact of bias from any single evaluation model.
Explicitly analyze potential shared biases between Claude‑3‑Opus and GPT‑4o (or other evaluators) by comparing their scoring patterns and calibrating or adjusting scores accordingly.

Provide additional experiments to support the claim that MIA‑Bench specifically measures strict instruction following (rather than reflecting dataset construction artifacts), e.g., controlled comparisons with other benchmarks under matched conditions.
Report the standard deviation of performance metrics when evaluating LMMs across multiple trials using the same judge model.
Document the judge model's exact generation parameters (e.g., temperature, top-p, top-k, num_beams) used during evaluation.
Investigate whether the judge model's generation configuration (temperature, top-p, top-k, num_beams) has a substantial impact on evaluation scores.
Run the same judge model with different generation configurations (for example, multiple temperature and top-p settings) on identical model outputs and report how the scores change.

Report detailed performance metrics for evaluated LMMs using multiple judge models and versions (e.g., Claude-3-Opus, GPT-4o variants, GPT-4v, GPT-4-1106-preview, and open-source models such as Qwen2.5 and LLaMA-3.1).
Report the consistency of model rankings across different judge models, not only the absolute scores.

Clearly distinguish and report the source of the reported standard deviation: whether it arises from multiple inferences of the evaluated model or from multiple evaluations of the same outputs by the judge.
Explicitly state the units and magnitude of reported STD values (e.g., clarify if 0.00747 means 0.747% or 0.00747%).
Provide a clear rationale for the selection of judge models and justify why particular models/versions were chosen or excluded.
Include a detailed discussion of the limitations and potential biases introduced by using a single judge model for evaluation.

Complement automated judge-model evaluations with alternative methods (for example, human evaluation) and discuss how results might vary across evaluation methods and the implications of those variations.

Run and report experiments to detect potential data leakage/data contamination from source datasets (COCO 2017, SBU, TextVQA, Flickr) into the open-source models evaluated on MIA-Bench, and show whether and how you mitigated any contamination.
State clearly whether the Table 3 experiments on LLaVA-Next-13b were performed after preference-aligned fine-tuning (SFT); if they were, report the exact fine-tuning setup and checkpoints used.

Conduct and report experiments that evaluate the impact of mixing generated instructions with original instructions during instruction-tuning (e.g., different mix ratios), showing effects on MIA-Bench and other benchmarks.
Evaluate how model performance on MIA-Bench changes as you increase the number of generated instructions beyond 5,000 (e.g., 10k, 50k), and report the scaling behavior.

Provide a documented manual audit of the 400 annotated instructions and their sub-instructions to verify reasonableness (including checks on sub-instruction clarity, length limits, and appropriateness).

Perform a manual verification (human evaluation) of a sufficiently large, representative sample of GPT-4o scoring outputs to validate the accuracy and reasonableness of automated scores, and report inter-annotator agreement and discrepancies.
Explain and justify the procedure used to determine the number of sub-instructions per instruction (e.g., annotation guidelines, heuristics, or algorithmic rules).

If not already present, provide a difficulty grading or metadata indicating instruction difficulty (and justify the scheme); analyze whether more sub-instructions correlate with higher task difficulty.

Include concrete real-world scenarios or user-centric examples showing where layered/complex instructions in MIA-Bench lead to improved user experience or task performance compared to simpler instructions (or run user studies demonstrating this).

Perform a focused analysis to explain the lack of correlation between MIA-Bench and other benchmarks, explicitly testing whether discrepancies stem from instruction content, dataset composition, or the evaluation metrics.

Provide a detailed error analysis comparing the types of mistakes MLLMs make on MIA-Bench versus their errors on other standard benchmarks to identify specific failure modes when following complex, layered instructions.

Run ablation experiments testing different instruction encoding methods or model architecture components (e.g., attention variants) to measure their impact on MIA-Bench performance and report the results.

Report performance analyses grouped by underlying LLM architecture and training data (e.g., decoder-only vs encoder-decoder; pretraining corpora), not only by model parameter size, to reveal architecture- or data-driven differences in instruction following.

Justify the relevance of evaluating complex instruction-following focused on writing-style constraints (length, genre) for MLLM users, or alternatively include tasks that reflect more typical MLLM use cases; if retained, motivate why these abilities are important for multimodal models rather than only LLMs.

Demonstrate the generalizability of MIA-Bench results by reporting correlations with other established multimodal benchmarks or by evaluating the submitted models on additional datasets to show whether strong MIA-Bench performance transfers to other tasks.

Clarify how instructions in MIA-Bench are constructed: explicitly state whether each instruction is tied to the corresponding image or randomly sampled from a general instruction bank; if not image-specific, discuss the implications and consider creating/annotating image-grounded instructions.
Control for the LLM backbone when reporting performance: group the results in Table 1 and Table 2 by the LLM model and size used in each MLLM, so differences attributable to LLM capacity are visible.

Provide ablation experiments where the same LLM is paired with different visual encoders (and vice versa) to isolate the contribution of the visual module versus the LLM to instruction-following performance.

c_real.text: State explicitly whether you will release the textbook dataset; if not, provide concrete access options (e.g., restricted release, data use agreement) or a clear rationale for withholding it.
Provide contamination studies quantifying overlap between the textbook pre-training corpus and all evaluation/test sets used in the paper.
Describe the contamination detection method in detail (e.g., exact string matching, fuzzy/semantic matching, thresholds, tools) and justify why it is appropriate.
Report contamination rates broken down by evaluation subset and task type (e.g., by dataset, question type, domain) so readers can see where contamination is more likely.

Publish a detailed description of the textbook pre-training corpus composition: number of textbooks, counts per subject (physics, chemistry, biology, etc.), and counts per educational level (high school, undergraduate, graduate).

Analyze and report possible biases in the textbook corpus (e.g., geographic origin, publisher concentration, language/register) and discuss how these biases could affect model performance and generalizability.
Provide a full reproducible data-processing pipeline for the textbooks, including scripts, parameters, and sequence of steps used to clean and normalize the text.
Explain specific preprocessing decisions: how you tokenize/normalize domain-specific terminology, handle OCR errors, remove boilerplate, and any deduplication strategies.
Describe how you handle mathematical content and special characters during preprocessing (e.g., LaTeX preservation, formula tokenization, mapping strategies) and justify those choices.

Explain how images, figures, and tables in the textbooks are processed or excluded, and provide details on any multimodal handling (OCR for captions, table parsing, image embedding) used for pretraining.
Provide empirical analysis linking types of textbooks to performance gains in SciRiff: which subjects, levels, or textbook formats most improve which downstream tasks.
If you claim 'low' contamination, provide quantitative thresholds and sensitivity analysis showing how contamination level affects evaluation metrics.
Provide a breakdown of contamination detection results by similarity method (e.g., results from exact match vs. semantic search) to show robustness of the contamination assessment.
Include justification for each major preprocessing choice and, where possible, ablation studies showing the impact of different preprocessing steps on model performance.
Investigate and improve the PDF processing pipeline because using a simple parser like PyPDF2 can introduce formatting and syntax errors that degrade corpus quality.
Quantify the extent of text extraction errors from the chosen PDF parser (e.g., frequency of mixed footnotes, misinterpreted tables/figures, and formatting mistakes).
Provide a detailed analysis showing how the 'Reformat & Grammar Correction' step mitigates the extraction errors introduced by the PDF parser.
Compare parsing tools by processing a sample of documents with alternative libraries (e.g., Papermage and other academic parsers cited by Lo et al.) and report differences in extracted-text quality.

Perform a controlled ablation study that directly compares a model fine-tuned only on the instruction dataset versus the full SciLitLLM pipeline including pre-training, to isolate the contribution of pre-training.
Fine-tune other model families (e.g., Llama or Qwen2.5, Llama3) on the same instruction dataset to evaluate whether results generalize beyond the Qwen family.
Avoid or clarify the potentially unfair comparison to SciTulu (a Llama-2-based model) in Table 3; ensure comparisons are made between comparable model families and training setups.

Reorganize experimental tables and results presentation to group models by training/tuning regime (pre-trained only / instruction-tuned / domain-specific tuned) rather than by an arbitrary model-size bin like '10B'.
Include evaluations on explicit factual-consistency and hallucination metrics (or other metrics that directly measure grounding) in addition to current benchmarks.
Report and discuss the computational cost of the pre-training stage (GPU hours, hardware used) and analyze trade-offs between compute, model size, and performance.

Explore and report on alternative, more efficient approaches (e.g., efficient pre-training techniques or knowledge distillation) that could reduce computational overhead while achieving similar performance.

Improve experimental design and presentation clarity (e.g., clearly delineate which steps or datasets contribute to each result and include ablations/sensitivity analyses) to better support claims about the method's effects.
Randomly sample a small subset of the CPT and SFT datasets and perform human annotations to validate data quality; report inter-annotator agreement and summary statistics.
Explicitly describe how the new CPT dataset differs from the Dolma dataset (scope, sources, generation process, filtering criteria, and intended use cases).
Evaluate the model fine-tuned on the Dolma dataset (or other existing scientific datasets) and report comparative results to demonstrate generalizability.
Report the model's performance when fine-tuned only with SciLitIns (i.e., ablate other pretraining/fine-tuning components to isolate SciLitIns effect).
Expand the ablation study to measure the impact of varying the size of the CPT and SFT datasets on final performance.
Include ablations that analyze the effect of different fine-tuning hyperparameters (learning rate, batch size, number of epochs, etc.) on model performance.

Provide a detailed error analysis of the unfiltered LLM-generated data: categorize error types (incorrect facts, hallucinations, formatting issues, contradictions, etc.) and quantify their prevalence.
Perform a sensitivity analysis over prompt variations used to generate CPT and SFT data and report how prompt design affects data quality and downstream performance.
Include more comprehensive comparisons with other models fine-tuned on similar scientific datasets, ensuring comparable evaluation protocols and training data where possible.
Evaluate the model on a broader set of scientific tasks (e.g., information extraction, relation extraction, question answering over scientific text) to better characterize capabilities.
Move key evaluation results and core analyses from the Appendix into the main paper to improve readability and ensure important evidence is presented centrally.
Clarify the exact evaluation metrics reported in Tables 2 and 3: specify what the reported 'score' is (e.g., top-1 accuracy), and provide a mapping of which metric is used for each task.
Provide interpretation and explanation for experimental results rather than merely restating table numbers; discuss why certain models/settings perform better or worse.
Analyze and quantify potential biases introduced by relying on LLMs for dataset creation, and discuss how these biases could affect downstream evaluation.
Show concrete evidence (statistics and examples) that the applied filtering mitigates specific issues such as repetitive or contradictory generations.
Address the fairness of the comparison with SciTulu-7B by either controlling for architectural differences (e.g., same backbone) or explaining how architecture impacts the results.
Discuss limitations of the evaluation metrics used (e.g., sensitivity to dataset imbalance, metric suitability per task) and how these limitations affect result interpretation.
Discuss how the relatively small CPT corpus (12.7B tokens) might impact learned representations across different scientific subject domains.

Explicitly define the action space used by the model: list its elements, dimensionality, and how actions map to model outputs or decisions in your framework.

Run a systematic sweep over \u03b1 (suggested values: 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9), report helpfulness and harmlessness for each value in a table or graph, and analyze trends to identify any optimal range and explain why certain \u03b1 values perform better or worse.

Provide complete experimental details for reproducibility: hyperparameters, training procedure, random seeds, and evaluation metrics.

Include a discussion of limitations of in-context learning for RL, specifically sensitivity to prompt choice/order and potential failure to generalize to tasks substantially different from pretraining.
Evaluate and report model performance on a more diverse set of tasks (different dynamics and reward structures) to test for overfitting to the pretraining task distribution.

Discuss the computational cost and sensitivity to hyperparameters of the proposed method, and include guidance or ablations showing how performance depends on key hyperparameters.

Add comparisons to recent offline RL baselines (e.g., Conservative Q-Learning (CQL) and other SOTA offline RL methods) so DIT's performance on suboptimal datasets is contextualized against established offline-RL approaches.
Include DPT as a baseline in the bandit experiments and report comparative results so readers can assess DIT versus transformer-based methods in the bandit setting.
Provide a quantitative analysis of the computational overhead introduced by the advantage-based weighting mechanism, including training time and memory usage comparisons versus baselines.

Discuss how DIT specifically differs from or improves upon advantage-weighted/offline-RL methods such as A2PR and AW/RW in the related work and methodology sections, and add experiments if possible to illustrate empirical differences.

Clarify the model training scheme: state whether the in-context task-identification transformer, the Q model, and the value model are trained once and shared across tasks or trained separately per task; specify how many instances are trained for different task sets.

When comparing to offline RL methods, report explicit metrics that matter for this comparison (e.g., average return and any constraint satisfaction metrics) to better demonstrate DIT's strengths and limitations relative to those methods.

Provide a detailed scaling analysis (table or graphs) showing how training time and memory usage grow with dataset size and number of tasks/task complexity, and discuss practical implications and limits on applicability.

In the bandit results section, add a focused analysis explaining observed performance differences between DIT and DPT (e.g., effects of advantage-weighted learning versus using optimal action labels) and potential reasons for those differences.

Clarify and emphasize the paper's novel insight: explicitly state what new understanding or capability arises from combining in-context learning with advantage-weighted regression, rather than presenting the work primarily as an application of known techniques.
Compare the adopted Q/V training objective (Eq.9) with offline-RL methods that provide in-sample optimality guarantees (e.g., IQL), and report performance and stability differences.

Provide a theoretical analysis or formal argument that Eq.9 converges to an optimal solution (or conditions under which it does), and explain why it should not suffer from the common overestimation issues in offline RL.

Add empirical diagnostics to show whether Eq.9 produces Q-value overestimation (e.g., compare estimated Q to Monte Carlo returns, track overestimation metrics, or include ablations demonstrating effect on downstream policy).
Evaluate robustness to errors in estimated advantage values: run sensitivity experiments with noisy, biased, or systematically suboptimal advantage estimates and report how performance degrades.
Run direct experiments comparing DIT and DPT trained on the exact same suboptimal datasets to isolate their differences; avoid indirect comparisons that mix dataset quality or training regimes.

Broaden experiments to test generalization across different types of suboptimal data (e.g., data from different behavioral policies, varying noise levels, levels of suboptimality) to characterize when DIT helps or fails.
Rewrite and clarify the notation paragraph (and any ambiguous symbols) to improve precision and make mathematical definitions and assumptions easier to follow.

Cite and discuss closely related recent works that apply RL or chain-of-thought to in-context decision making (e.g., 'Q-value Regularized Transformer for Offline RL', 'Rethinking Decision Transformer via Hierarchical RL', 'In-Context Decision Transformer'), and explain how your approach differs and contributes relative to them.

Discuss limitations and potential biases introduced by the in-context learning paradigm (e.g., how in-context prompts or transformer inductive biases might skew advantage estimates or policy behavior) and propose or evaluate mitigation strategies.

The paper does not demonstrate sufficient novelty relative to existing exponential advantage-weighted regression methods (e.g., AWAC, AWR) and conservative policy iteration; explicitly justify what is new beyond applying these ideas to an in-context setting.

Add and discuss the relevant prior work (e.g., Simple and Scalable Off-Policy RL; AWAC; Behavior Proximal Policy Optimization) and clearly state the theoretical and practical differences between those works and the present paper.
Provide a deeper comparison of the proposed exponential weighting scheme to alternative weighting or regularization approaches instead of only presenting the exponential variant.
Analyze sensitivity of the method to the quality of the advantage estimates; discuss how estimation errors affect the weighting and downstream policy updates.
Include ablation or sensitivity studies that vary the advantage estimation method (and its amount of noise/bias) to quantify the impact on performance.

Compare experimentally and/or theoretically to methods that incorporate uncertainty or explicitly remove bad-action updates (e.g., uncertainty-weighted actor-critic, approaches that zero weights for bad actions, Tsallis regularization).

Explicitly address the known problem that exponential weighting can assign non-negligible weight to very bad actions: show how your method avoids amplifying bad actions or modify the weighting to mitigate this risk and report results.
Systematically evaluate alternative weighting strategies (including uncertainty-aware or truncation/zeroing schemes) and report trade-offs in stability, convergence, and sample efficiency.
Clarify the unique theoretical contributions of the paper: state what is novel in the proofs/analysis beyond standard CPI techniques and why the in-context setting changes the theory.

Provide theoretical or empirical analysis of generalization across tasks and convergence behavior in the in-context setting (e.g., how performance scales with number/diversity of tasks and per-task data).
Discuss limitations of the proposed in-context advantage estimation when tasks are diverse or when there is limited data per task, and propose practical remedies or failure-mode diagnostics.

Provide precise implementation details for each data augmentation technique: algorithms or libraries used, exact hyperparameters (e.g., random deletion percentage, synonym selection method, swap/insertion rules), and back-translation language pairs.
Give full descriptions of the three datasets: sources and licenses, number of examples and tokens, character/dialogue distribution, preprocessing steps, and representative examples.
Include a clear baseline model trained without augmentation (with identical training regimen) to enable fair comparison of augmentation effects.
Compare the augmented models against prior methods from the literature and report how performance differs relative to established baselines or state-of-the-art approaches.
Run ablation studies that isolate the individual impact of each augmentation technique (and common combinations) and report those results.

Operationalize and define "robustness" for this study (e.g., preservation of character-specific tone, consistency metrics) and provide concrete evaluation protocols and metrics, including human evaluation procedures if applicable.

Perform statistical validation of results: report significance tests (e.g., t-tests, ANOVA), confidence intervals, and include an error analysis documenting common failure modes and when augmentations fail.
Justify the choice of LLaMA3 and LoRA and discuss whether and how the findings would generalize to other base models or adaptation techniques (e.g., adapters, full fine-tuning).
Report all training and fine-tuning hyperparameters (learning rates, batch sizes, epochs, LoRA rank, random seeds) and provide code/configuration files or supplementary materials for reproducibility.

Strengthen the empirical contribution by either proposing a novel augmentation technique targeted at personalized character modeling or providing analytical/theoretical explanations for why specific augmentations help or harm personalization.

c_real.text: Clarify the provenance of the training data: specify the sources, selection criteria, and curation pipeline used to assemble the dataset.
Describe how the proposed dataset relates to existing datasets and prior work, and clearly state which specific problems or gaps it is intended to solve.
Measure and report training data leakage: assess whether the TV shows in the dataset appear in common LLM pretraining corpora and quantify any overlap.
Include a baseline fine-tuning experiment without data augmentation so the effect of augmentation can be isolated and quantified.
Compare fine-tuning results to few-shot and other prompt-based scenarios (e.g., in-context learning) to show whether tuning is necessary or advantageous.

Explicitly describe the fine-tuning procedure used for character-specific models: how are training examples constructed and how is the autoregressive loss computed when multiple characters speak in a single example?
Shorten or relocate the extended background on low-rank fine-tuning (LoRA) and reallocate space to provide more experimental details and results.

Resolve the discrepancy between abstract and experiments: either include results for the third dataset mentioned in the abstract or revise the abstract to reflect which datasets are actually evaluated.
Fix the supplementary material packaging: provide a proper decompressible zip (or alternative readable format) so reviewers can access the supplementary files.
Evaluate how well prompt-based solutions perform on the same tasks and report quantitative comparisons (e.g., few-shot prompts, instruction tuning-free baselines).
Test whether the reported results generalize to existing dialogue datasets by running the same fine-tuning and evaluation pipeline on one or more public dialogue corpora.
Substantially shorten Sections 2 and 3 that describe past techniques (EDA, paraphrasing, LoRA); focus those sections only on methods directly used or on how prior work motivates your contributions.

Develop and evaluate novel data augmentation techniques specifically tailored to character-based dialogue rather than relying primarily on well-known methods like synonym replacement and backtranslation.
Include additional datasets covering a wider range of conversational styles, character personalities, or domains to demonstrate generalizability beyond the two narrow, character-specific datasets.
If adding more datasets is not possible, perform a detailed analysis of dataset characteristics and explain why particular augmentation techniques succeed or fail on these datasets.
Investigate and report on methods to mitigate paraphrasing overfitting, e.g., alternative paraphrasing algorithms, regularization strategies, or domain-adaptive paraphrasing.
Experiment with off-the-shelf large language models for data augmentation and evaluate them as stronger baselines in comparison to the current augmentation methods.
Explore sentiment-aware and context-sensitive paraphrasing approaches that preserve character voice and semantic constraints when generating augmented dialogue.

Provide a clear, explicit discussion of the computational trade-offs from using LLaMA3-8B: what memory/performance adjustments were made, and how these choices may limit model performance or generalization.

Fix invalid entries in the reference list (e.g., remove or correct 'Unknown Author') and expand the reference list to include more recent and relevant literature on augmentation and LLM-based augmentation.
Revise Figure 3 so it contains substantially more information or compress/remove it if it cannot justify occupying a full page.
Adjust Table 1 formatting (reduce font size or reformat) so the table does not appear artificially enlarged to fill space.
Add stronger experimental baselines (explicitly include LLM-based data augmentation and other competitive methods) to better contextualize the contributions.
Clarify the major claim/contribution of the paper: state a single, explicit research question and list the paper's contributions in the introduction.
Provide formal descriptions and detailed explanations of each data augmentation technique mentioned (e.g., include pseudocode, equations, or algorithmic steps) rather than only naming them.

Add complete experimental details: datasets used, preprocessing steps, train/validation/test splits, model architectures, hyperparameters, random seeds, and training procedures so experiments are reproducible.
Report full quantitative results in tables: include exact metric values, variance (standard deviation or confidence intervals) across multiple runs, and clear comparisons to appropriate baselines.

Improve all plots and figures: include clear axis labels, legends, units, error bars where appropriate, and captions that explain what is plotted; replace or redesign plots that currently show no discernible trends.

Discuss and analyze unexpected or 'mysterious' results: provide hypotheses, additional experiments, or diagnostic analyses to explain surprising outcomes or re-run experiments if results are unreliable.

Remove screenshots taken from other papers or explicitly cite and obtain permission for reused figures; if reusing results, recreate the figures with proper attribution and ensure they are clearly marked as reproduced.

The data augmentations only perform local word/structure permutations and do not address the thematic-framework distributional shift you motivate; design augmentations that alter theme-level content or otherwise diversify the thematic context to better target the stated core challenge.
The paper fails to attribute the augmentation methods to prior work; add proper citations and discuss how your augmentations relate to and differ from existing studies.
You did not evaluate a finetuning-only baseline (no data augmentation); add this baseline to isolate the effect of augmentations.

BLEU and ROUGE scores are extremely low — include qualitative examples of generated outputs and an analysis explaining why automatic scores are so low; consider additional or alternative evaluation metrics more appropriate for personalization.

The observed decreasing loss could reflect language collapse (e.g., model learning to output Chinese); explicitly evaluate forgetting and language collapse by reporting language-specific metrics (perplexity or accuracy per language) and the distribution of output languages.

The evaluation setup is unclear and may simply test phrase repetition in context; clarify the evaluation objective and add downstream role-playing evaluations (e.g., interactive/human evaluations or task-based measures) to demonstrate suitability for real-world roleplay.
The study uses only two role-playing characters and lacks broader coverage; expand experiments to more characters and to community datasets to better contextualize results.
There are no comparisons to simple baselines such as prompting-based roleplay; include comparisons to prompting and other straightforward baselines.

Sections and evaluations unrelated to personalization (e.g., Unsloth, external benchmark evaluations in Figure/Table 1) distract from the core contribution; move these to the appendix and prioritize space for substantive experiments on personalization.
Figure 3 has mislabeled or duplicated legend entries (e.g., 'BT Loss' repeated); fix all figure legends and verify that plots and labels are correct and unambiguous.
The references contain incorrect metadata/placeholders (e.g., 'Journal Name', 'Unknown Author'); correct citation details and ensure each referenced work has accurate author and venue information.

The manuscript devotes excessive space to background and delays concrete experiments until halfway through; compress or relocate background material and present the experimental setup earlier for clearer narrative flow.
There are duplicated or repeated phrases in the text (e.g., 'Wei and Zou (2019) Wei & Zou (2019)'); thoroughly proofread and remove duplicated content and other similar editorial errors.

c_gen.text: Provide the exact Llama2 prompt templates and formats used for caption perturbation (include full example prompts, temperature/top-k settings, constraints or post-processing rules) so others can reproduce the perturbations.

Report the exact threshold(s) and selection procedure used in the VoG filtering strategy (how thresholds were chosen) and include a sensitivity analysis showing how performance varies with different threshold values.

Give full implementation details for fine-tuning the Stable Diffusion model: learning rates, number of steps/epochs, optimizer, batch size, checkpoint initialization, random seeds, and any data preprocessing or augmentation applied.

Describe concretely how gradient variance (VoG) is computed: whether variance is per-sample or per-batch, which layers/parameters are measured, the time window or number of iterations used, normalization or smoothing applied, and exact formulas.

Provide a theoretical justification or empirical analysis for the core VoG assumption that in-distribution samples exhibit higher gradient variance (e.g., toy experiments, statistical tests, or references) to support the heuristic.

Expand baseline comparisons to include other relevant methods such as class-balanced synthetic generation and standard augmentation techniques (e.g., CutOut, MixUp, RandAugment), and report these results in the main comparisons.

Evaluate the quality of the generated synthetic images using standard metrics (e.g., FID, IS) and provide representative qualitative examples to clarify whether improvements stem from realism or the feedback mechanism.
Report the computational cost of the Llama2 caption perturbation step (GPU/CPU time, memory, latency per sample) and compare it to alternative methods for increasing synthetic-data diversity.

Include experiments or analyses that demonstrate synthetic data does not cause overfitting in the synthetic-only setting (e.g., learning curves, validation on a held-out real set, regularization ablations, or cross-dataset evaluation).

Fix the typographical errors and inconsistent formatting (examples cited such as Algorithm 1 and misformatted figures/tables); provide a corrected manuscript with consistent figure/table captions and algorithm pseudocode.
Release code, training scripts, random seeds, and model checkpoints (or provide a reproducibility appendix) so other researchers can reproduce the method and verify results.

c_real.text: Demonstrate technical novelty: explicitly explain what is novel about the proposed algorithm beyond components borrowed from prior work.
Provide stronger experimental validation to support the claimed contributions (marginal sample selection, sample diversity, distribution alignment).
Quantify and discuss why the proposed components provide only small gains relative to using synthetic samples without the proposed changes (as seen in Table 3).
Provide analysis to determine whether performance gains come from the proposed synthetic augmentation pipeline or from distilled information present in Stable Diffusion's training data.
Explain how the marginal sample selection algorithm differs from Focal Loss (which also reweights samples based on classifier confidence/difficulty).
Define S_f in Algorithm 1 and clarify its role; currently S_f is referenced in line 13 but not defined in Section 3.4.

Clarify and justify how the specific combination of existing techniques (LLM perturbation, VoG filtering, marginal selection) constitutes a novel approach rather than a straightforward composition of prior methods.
Provide a detailed description of the LLM-based caption perturbation procedure (steps, prompts, parameters) and how it differs from standard prompt engineering approaches.
If VoG scores are used for filtering, describe any adaptations or modifications made to VoG to make its use unique in this context; otherwise justify its selection.
Provide a theoretical justification or formal analysis for the marginal sample selection method to explain why it should improve downstream performance.
Evaluate alternative sample selection methods (e.g., uncertainty sampling, entropy, random, class-balanced, diversity-based) and compare their performance to the proposed marginal selection.
Add ablation experiments that isolate the impact of each component (LLM perturbation, VoG filtering, marginal sample selection) on final classification performance.
Specifically evaluate LLM perturbation versus random perturbation and versus no perturbation to show its added value.
Specifically evaluate VoG filtering versus random filtering and versus no filtering to justify its use.
Specifically compare marginal sample selection with random selection and with selection based on alternative criteria to demonstrate its advantage.
Provide a more detailed analysis of the 'synthetic data only' setting and include comparisons to other state-of-the-art methods that use only synthetic data.
Expand the scaling ablation study to cover a wider range of synthetic-to-real data ratios and report results across multiple datasets.

Run experiments generating synthetic data from a generative model trained on a smaller or less diverse dataset to measure how much performance gain is due to the generator's pretraining data versus the augmentation method.
Test the pipeline with synthetic data from other generative models (not just Stable Diffusion) to verify whether performance gains generalize across different generators.
Provide measured runtimes and experimental setup details to substantiate the reported slowdowns (350x vs real-only and 2x vs Real-Fake) rather than only reporting relative factors.
Experimentally verify whether the on-the-fly data generation is the primary cause of the slowdown by measuring runtime with and without on-the-fly augmentation (ablation).
Provide a detailed time breakdown per pipeline component (e.g., marginal score calculation, caption perturbation, outlier filtering) to identify the most expensive steps.
Profile and report compute resource usage (GPU hours, CPU time, memory footprint) during training to quantify practical resource requirements.
Investigate converting the method to an offline mode by pre-generating synthetic data and compare training time and final accuracy between on-the-fly and offline setups.
Evaluate the trade-off between the amount of synthetic data used and model accuracy (plot accuracy vs synthetic-data volume) to identify a minimal effective dataset size.

Test and report more efficient implementation or approximation strategies for expensive components (e.g., caching, lower-precision, model distillation, approximate scoring) and report speed/accuracy trade-offs.
Study the impact of key hyperparameters on both training time and accuracy, and provide practical guidelines for choosing values under different resource constraints.
Include a thorough comparison with other data-augmentation methods that focuses on training time and memory usage in addition to accuracy.
Explicitly discuss the method's limitations and scope: evaluate effectiveness across different dataset types and tasks and report robustness to variations in the training data.

Clarify and analyze the trade-off between tailoring synthetic data generation to a specific downstream task (specialization) versus maintaining generality/applicability to other tasks; provide empirical or theoretical insights on how the method balances this conflict.
Evaluate the method's performance on out-of-distribution (OOD) test sets to determine whether synthetic data must be in-distribution, and report results and analysis for OOD generalization.

Provide a clear discussion and empirical evaluation of the reusability and sustainability of generated synthetic data, including whether and when generated datasets should be saved versus generated on-the-fly.

Explain the selection strategy for marginal training samples (those with higher variance in gradient updates): why these samples are chosen, how they relate to model difficulty, and the implications for training and robustness.
Relate the sample selection strategy to active learning literature and, if appropriate, compare the proposed selection criteria empirically against established active learning selection methods.

Expand the literature review to include closely related works such as 'A New Benchmark: On the Utility of Synthetic Data with Blender for Bare Supervised Learning and Downstream Domain Adaptation (CVPR 2023)' and discuss connections and differences to the present work.

Provide a mechanistic explanation or empirical evidence for the claim that maintaining a consistent prompt format between fine-tuning Stable Diffusion and generating synthetic data increases alignment between generated-data distribution and target data distribution.

Investigate whether incorporating domain adaptation (DA) techniques (as in the cited CVPR 2023 work) during LoRA fine-tuning improves downstream performance; report experiments and analysis on this synergy.

Analyze the risk of overfitting introduced by fine-tuning Stable Diffusion for a target dataset: include sensitivity experiments varying the degree of fine-tuning and report effects on performance across multiple tasks/datasets.

Experimentally assess robustness to distributional shift by evaluating the approach on datasets with controlled distribution shifts; include analyses varying VoG filtering (or equivalent outlier controls) to study the effect on OOD robustness.

Discuss techniques to generate more diverse and generalizable synthetic data within the proposed framework (e.g., augmentation, diversity-promoting objectives, multi-task fine-tuning), and evaluate whether such techniques improve reuse across tasks.

Report the computational cost of on-the-fly synthetic data generation (time, GPU/CPU resources) and provide recommendations or experiments on optimizations (e.g., caching, partial offline generation, model distillation) for practical deployment.

Clarify and justify the paper's novelty: explicitly state which components are novel vs derived from prior work, and add ablations or experiments that isolate and demonstrate the unique contribution(s) of the proposed method.
Provide a sensitivity study for the VoG metric: vary the epochs/checkpoints used to compute gradients (not just 10, 20, 30) and report how VoG scores and downstream classification accuracy change.
Provide a principled rationale for choosing specific epochs (10, 20, 30): tie the selection to model convergence behavior or training dynamics, rather than leaving it arbitrary.
Evaluate alternative checkpoint-selection methods for VoG (e.g., select checkpoints based on validation loss/accuracy or other automatic criteria) and compare results to the fixed-epoch approach.
Include robustness evaluations: report accuracy under common corruptions/perturbations and in adversarial settings, and compare the proposed method to baselines on these robustness metrics.
Test generalization across text-to-image generators: repeat key experiments using other popular generators (e.g., SD3, Kandinsky) and report whether gains hold across generation methods.

Test generalization to other discriminative CV tasks: run experiments (or at least pilots) on tasks such as image retrieval and object detection to assess whether the method transfers beyond classification.

Provide a detailed computational-cost vs performance analysis: quantify GPU hours and wall-clock time, and supply a component-wise breakdown (LLM caption perturbation, text-to-image generation, VoG filtering, marginal sample utilization) versus accuracy gains.

Investigate and report practical optimizations to reduce compute cost (e.g., use more efficient text-to-image models, reduce sample counts, or apply parallelization) and measure their impact on accuracy and cost.
Address potential overfitting to small benchmarks: run experiments on larger and more diverse datasets, and report whether the observed improvements persist at scale.

Strengthen the evidence behind the overfitting claim (line 483): provide more experiments and quantitative analyses (the current Pets dataset results are insufficient) to support any strong statements about overfitting.

Reconcile and/or experimentally address the contradiction with Mousterian (ICLR25): run experiments varying synthetic-data volume to test whether synthetic value diminishes quickly or remains correlated with accuracy, and discuss differences with that prior work.

Analyze learned representations: compare feature-level properties (e.g., feature transferability, robustness, or embedding separability) between models trained with your method and with other synthetic-augmentation baselines to demonstrate whether improvements are due to better generalization or overfitting.

Provide practical guidelines for applying VoG to other benchmarks/tasks: explain how to choose epochs/checkpoints, sample sizes, and thresholds so practitioners can reproduce the approach on new datasets.
