Release code or pseudocode for the Jacobian computation and all analysis scripts, or include detailed algorithmic steps in the appendix so others can reproduce the spectral analyses.

Conduct sensitivity analyses showing whether the observed spectral gaps on natural images depend on architecture choices, optimizer, random seed, or other training details, and report results across multiple runs.

Provide a principled method and justification for choosing the parameter Δ that defines gap width: include a sensitivity analysis, statistical thresholding approach, or a null-model baseline to support the chosen value.

Include validation that the inferred manifold dimensionality from spectral gaps corresponds to meaningful data structure—e.g., compare with PCA, manifold learning methods, synthetic ground-truth experiments, or topological analyses.

Quantify the identified phase transitions rather than qualitatively describing them: define metrics to detect phase boundaries, report transition times/temperatures, and provide confidence intervals or statistical significance.
Demonstrate how the different phases affect sample quality by correlating phase metrics with standard generation metrics (e.g., FID, IS, likelihood) or with reconstruction/denoising performance.

Expand ablation studies beyond variance and subspace configurations to include architectural variants, different training hyperparameters, noise schedules, and score network capacities to test the robustness of the spectral-gap phenomena.

Compare the spectral behavior of diffusion models with other generative models (e.g., VAEs, GANs, normalizing flows) to contextualize claims about avoidance of manifold overfitting and show whether the observed behavior is unique to diffusion models.

c_real.text: Strengthen the justification for the discrepancy reported in Section 7 between the analytical (replica) and empirical results; explain why the curves diverge and under what assumptions they should coincide.
Run the same experiments in the isotropic case (where an analytic solution exists) to determine whether the discrepancy persists and to help isolate its source.

Validate the numerical method used to compute the Jacobian / score: provide tests or diagnostics showing the accuracy/stability of the Jacobian computation and whether it could cause the observed discrepancy.
Investigate the effect of training dataset size on the empirical results: provide a convergence analysis showing how the learned score and spectral results change as dataset size increases.

Clarify the definition and intended role of the “support score” and the “stable latent manifolds” in Eq.(5); if these constructs are not used later, remove them or explicitly explain their connection to the subsequent analysis.

Provide a quantitative analysis in Section 8 explaining the absence/smoothing of spectral gaps on CIFAR-10 and CelebA, rather than a qualitative statement; for example, show eigenvalue density plots or other statistics that vary with dataset/manifold complexity.

Explain why the estimated MNIST manifold dimensionality (~700) is so large compared to the number of classes (10); discuss whether this estimate reflects local vs global dimensionality and provide evidence supporting the interpretation.
Validate the dimensionality estimates by applying alternative manifold dimensionality estimation methods and reporting whether they agree with the presented estimates.

Provide precise, rigorous definitions for the three phases (trivial, manifold coverage, consolidation) and specify criteria/metrics that determine transitions between them; reconcile these definitions with the behavior shown in Figures 3 and 4.

Fix the mathematical wording at Line 131: check whether the intended statement is ‘radius proportional to sqrt(t)’ rather than exactly ‘radius sqrt(t)’, and correct any related descriptions to be precise.

Explicitly state and discuss the limitations of the approach (e.g., finite data, numerical approximations, model capacity) and how these limitations impact the interpretation of analytical vs empirical findings.
The theoretical analysis is too narrow: extend it beyond a Gaussian on a linear submanifold to more general settings (non-linear manifolds and non-Gaussian densities).
Investigate how the eigenspectra evolve for mixtures of Gaussians and for manifolds with curvature (e.g., via perturbation methods or concrete analytic/empirical studies).
Provide theoretical bounds on the approximation error incurred when the score function is estimated by a neural network to explain discrepancies between theory and experiments.
Report the number of training samples drawn from the distribution used to train the score function (this is missing and needed for reproducibility).
Systematically vary dataset size in experiments to measure how the number of training samples affects the accuracy of the score approximation and the empirical eigenspectra.

For the dataset-size experiments, plot eigenvalue distributions for different sample sizes and quantify deviations from theoretical predictions using explicit metrics (e.g., gap size, L2 distance, KL divergence).

Provide full neural network and training details in the experimental section: architecture (number of layers and units), activation functions, optimizer, learning rate, batch size, epochs, and any regularization.

Design controlled experiments to disentangle causes of mismatch between empirical and theoretical eigenspectra (model misspecification, insufficient data, optimization failure, or estimator variance).

If the method fails to detect intrinsic dimensionality on real datasets, add a discussion on how analyzing the score-function eigenspectra could still be practically useful for training or understanding diffusion models (give concrete scenarios).

Explore and test approaches to increase robustness of the method, such as denoising the estimated score, regularizing eigenvalue estimates, or extracting alternative robust features from the eigenspectra.
Add a clear limitations section and suggest alternative methods for estimating intrinsic dimensionality on complex data manifolds, with guidance on when each approach may be preferable.
The linear manifold assumption is overly restrictive; either extend the theoretical analysis beyond linear models or explicitly discuss this limitation and its impact on generalizability.
Provide results for nonlinear manifolds, or if infeasible, add a substantive discussion of how the linear results might (or might not) extend to nonlinear settings.

Extend the analysis using local linear approximations: analyze the Jacobian spectrum in local tangent regions and explain how (if) those local results could be aggregated to inform global manifold behavior.
Consider applying kernel methods to implicitly map data to a higher-dimensional space where the manifold is more linear, and evaluate whether the existing theoretical framework can then be applied.

Improve the presentation and interpretation of the real-data experiments (CIFAR-10 and CelebA, Figure 7): make figures clearer, spell out experimental setup, and explain how the results support the paper's claims.
Systematically investigate how different noise levels affect the observed spectral patterns and report these results.
Systematically investigate how different sampling strategies affect the observed spectral patterns and report these results.
Use more robust spectral analysis techniques (less sensitive to noise and outliers) and compare their outcomes to the current analysis.
Compare the empirical results with other manifold learning and dimensionality-reduction methods to contextualize strengths and limitations of the proposed approach.
Provide a detailed discussion of dataset-specific characteristics (e.g., hierarchical structures, varying complexity) that might influence the observed spectral patterns.

Give a more rigorous theoretical justification for the conjecture in Section 5 that the observed phenomenology captures subspace separation in tangent spaces, rather than leaving it as an unsupported claim.
Analyze the relationship between the Jacobian spectrum and manifold curvature using appropriate differential-geometry techniques, or clearly state the obstacles to doing so.
Provide empirical tests on synthetic datasets with known manifold structures and varying curvature to validate the conjecture about tangent-space subspace separation.
Clarify the paper's scope and contributions: explicitly state whether the work is primarily theoretical, primarily empirical, or a conjectural synthesis, and align claims and titles accordingly.
Add a dedicated, earlier section that introduces the necessary mathematical background on manifolds to clarify the manifold overfitting problem and improve the paper's logical flow.
Explicitly define and distinguish the 'data manifold' and the 'latent manifold' in the paper, and state precisely how the diffusion process acts on or around each.
Include concrete examples or case studies showing how manifold overfitting manifests in likelihood-based generative models to motivate the need for the diffusion-based approach.

Clarify the purpose of the linear random-manifold model in Section 6.2: explicitly state whether it is intended to model random datasets or to serve as a simplified theoretical framework for structured data.
List and justify the assumptions of the linear manifold model (e.g., linearity, distributional assumptions), and discuss the limitations these assumptions impose on the generality of the results.

Discuss whether the phase behaviors observed in the linear random-manifold model are expected to carry over to non-linear or more realistic manifolds, and if not, state the expected differences and limitations.
Provide an intuitive rationale for choosing the specific linear random-manifold model for theoretical analysis and explain how key conclusions could be generalized to more realistic scenarios.
Provide a precise mathematical definition of M_t (introduced in Equation (5)), including its construction from the underlying data manifold and the noising process.

Clarify whether M_t should be interpreted as a union of spherical shells of radius √t around data/latent points; if this interpretation is incorrect, explicitly state the correct geometric description.
State the geometric properties of M_t (e.g., is it a smooth manifold, a stratified set, or another geometric object) and explain how these properties depend on the noise level t.
Justify why introducing M_t is necessary for the subsequent analysis (in particular for interpreting Equation (6)) and show how results rely on properties of M_t.
If M_t is mathematically ambiguous or problematic, reconsider this formalism and either propose an alternative mathematical object/notation or clearly motivate why M_t remains the best choice.

Clarify vague passages by adding explicit equations or precise citations where ideas are currently only described verbally; mark the specific equations/sections that are informal and replace them with formal statements.

Explain more clearly how the random matrix theory (RMT) analysis yields actionable insights about practical diffusion models; connect RMT-derived quantities to model behavior during training and sampling.
Explicitly compare your eigenspectrum transition results to 'Diffusion Models Encode the Intrinsic Dimension of Data Manifolds' and state what is novel or different in your findings and methods.

Explicitly compare and contrast your analysis with 'Dynamical Regimes of Diffusion Models', listing which results are new here (vs. prior work) and how your approach differs in assumptions and conclusions.

Provide a direct, step-by-step explanation (or a dedicated subsection) connecting the paper's three phases to the phenomenon of manifold overfitting, describing how each phase helps avoid or mitigates overfitting.

Clarify the statement at lines 044–045 that the observed decomposition cannot be explained as critical phase transitions because they are linear processes; show the reasoning and any required caveats or counterexamples.

State the main technical and conceptual differences between your work and 'B. et al. 2024a' regarding subspace separation, rather than implying similarity—point to specific equations or results that differ.

Elaborate the claim at lines 091–095 that divergence of the data distribution implies increasing model density during training: provide mathematical derivation or empirical evidence supporting this implication.

Provide an explicit comparative analysis showing whether and why diffusion models (with your three phases) are less prone to manifold overfitting than likelihood-based models; include theoretical argumentation or experiments.
Cite prior literature or give empirical evidence supporting the claim that diffusion models are not affected by manifold overfitting, instead of asserting it without references.

Clarify lines 140–146: provide a derivation or citation that explains why perturbations aligned with the tangent space correspond to small eigenvalues and orthogonal perturbations to large eigenvalues.

Make Figure 1 readable: specify whether it is at a fixed time t, explain why the x-axis is 'latent dimensionality', and detail how eigenvalues map to latent dimensionality (include steps linking Stanczuk et al. 2022 to your plot).
Define 'singularities' and 'cross-over events' in the context of your phase claims (line 172) and justify the statement that phase separation does not correspond to genuine phase transitions.
Define σ_k (sigma_k) where it first appears; if it denotes variances across manifold components, state that formally and give conditions or examples when σ_{k+1}^2 << σ_k^2 would hold.
Define 'asymptotic closure' clearly: state whether you mean the theoretical end time of Phase II can be infinite and how you approximate or operationalize this closure in practice.
Define the variable r at line 481 where it is first used and ensure all symbols are defined on first appearance.

Explain how spectral gaps in the Jacobian concretely affect the sampling/generative process: analyze how the score function and sampling trajectories change across the proposed phases and how that impacts sample quality.

Provide a more detailed temporal analysis of eigenspectra in experiments (e.g., show spectra at multiple t values and training epochs) and explain how these empirical spectra align with your theoretical three-phase framework.
Expand experimental validation beyond MNIST to at least one or two more complex datasets (e.g., CIFAR, CelebA) to demonstrate the generality of the observed eigenspectrum transitions and phases.

Strengthen the analysis behind Figure 7: explicitly link the observed MNIST Jacobian eigenspectrum transition to your three phases with quantitative metrics or additional plots that show phase membership over time.
Fix non-content issues that affect reproducibility and readability: use the provided paper template and correct/unify the bibliography (consistent citation format and complete references).

c_gen.text: Provide a clear theoretical explanation of how input compensation generalizes from the linear layer derivation to non-linear models (e.g., activation functions like ReLU/GELU, residual connections, and transformer attention).
Include a theoretical analysis (or formal conditions) that characterizes when IC is expected to be effective in non-linear models, not just empirical ablations.

Report a computational cost analysis comparing pruned models with and without IC, including memory footprint, additional parameters impact, and inference-time latency (wall-clock), across the evaluated architectures.
Report performance of the corresponding unpruned (full) models for all tasks so readers can assess absolute performance and trade-offs between pruning+IC and the original models.
Compare IC against other model compression techniques (at least distillation and quantization) or include a discussion/experiment showing how IC complements or differs from these methods.
Specify exactly how classification accuracy is computed (e.g., top-1 vs top-5) for each image classification result reported.
Specify exactly how perplexity is computed (token-level vs sequence-level, vocabulary/encoding used, and any batching or truncation details) for the language modeling experiments.
Provide exact details for FID computation: which Inception model/version is used, preprocessing applied to generated and real images, and the number of generated samples used to compute FID.
Provide missing optimizer hyperparameters for image classification experiments, specifically weight decay and the learning rate schedule used with SGD.
Provide missing optimizer/training hyperparameters for NLP experiments, specifically AdamW warmup ratio and the total number of training steps (or epochs) used.
Describe how the compensation pool is initialized for the input-independent IC variant (e.g., random initialization, pretrained values, fixed constants) and how it is learned/updated during training.

Specify the exact evaluation protocol for zero-shot NLP tasks (e.g., BoolQ, RTE): which metrics are reported (accuracy, F1, etc.), dataset splits or preprocessing steps, and any prompt/templates used.

Provide an explanation or additional experiments that clarify why input-dependent compensation outperforms input-independent compensation, beyond empirical ablation (e.g., analysis of capacity, per-input variability, or failure cases).

c_real.text: Add comparisons of computation cost and inference speed (e.g., latency measurements) between baseline pruned models and models with Input Compensation (IC).
Provide FLOPs and inference time analysis across various sparsity levels and model sizes, comparing pruned models with and without IC to show the accuracy vs computational cost trade-offs.
Measure and report the memory footprint of the compensation pool (including how it scales with model size) and discuss its impact on deployment and resource usage.

For CLIP experiments, quantify the additional cost of the extra encoder pass shown in Figure 1 and clarify the claim that the pruned image encoder is reused; include CLIP-specific compute/time comparisons.
Conduct structured sparsity experiments using the 2:4 pruning pattern (in addition to 4:8) and report results, since modern GPUs provide acceleration for 2:4 patterns.

Analyze how IC adapts to and interacts with different pruning algorithms and sparsity patterns (e.g., magnitude-based pruning, Wanda, SparseGPT, structured vs unstructured), and report comparative performance and cost.
Include experiments comparing combinations such as 'wanda+owl' vs 'wanda+owl+IC' to demonstrate whether IC can be effectively combined with OWL and similar pruning techniques.

Provide full implementation details required for reproducibility: exact initialization of the compensation pool, optimizer, learning rate schedule, batch size, random seeds, and other training hyperparameters.

Clarify the training procedure for the compensation pool: whether it is trained end-to-end with the pruned model or in a separate stage, and specify the training schedule and any freeze/unfreeze steps.
Explain precisely how the learned compensation vectors are applied to the input (include mathematical formulas, pseudocode, or algorithmic steps to show the input compensation mechanism).
Report wall-clock training time and hardware configuration for all reported experiments.
Release the code, model checkpoints, and scripts used for experiments (or provide a concrete plan/timeline for release) to improve reproducibility.
Expand and clarify the overall method description and figures (algorithmic workflow and component roles) so the proposed approach is clearly understandable and reproducible.
Quantify the additional computation and memory costs introduced by the encoder and attention-based compensation mechanism during inference (report absolute and relative numbers).
Provide a detailed breakdown of FLOPs and latency introduced by the encoder and attention-based compensation, measured separately and combined.
Measure and report inference time of the proposed method versus baseline pruning methods to quantify practical runtime overhead.
Report the memory footprint of the compensation mechanism (encoder + compensation pool + any extra buffers) and its impact on overall model memory usage.
Clarify the optimization process for the compensation pool, explicitly reconciling or distinguishing the approaches described in Sections 5.1 and 5.2.
Describe the initialization strategy for compensation pool parameters, including exact distributions and any special tricks (e.g., scaling, layer-wise schemes).
State the learning rates, optimizer choices, and number of training epochs used for both image and text compensation pools, and justify these hyperparameter choices.
Provide a sensitivity analysis showing how the method's performance varies with key hyperparameters (learning rate, optimizer, epochs, pool size, etc.).
Justify the loss functions used to train the compensation pool and provide a clear description of how the compensation pool is integrated into the overall training objective and pipeline.
Include evaluation on the structured 2:4 sparsity setting and compare results to existing structured pruning methods to assess practical relevance.

Clearly define how sparsity is computed: specify whether parameters of the encoder and compensation pool are included and whether reported sparsity refers to the entire system or only the original pruned model.
List the full experimental environment for reproducibility, including hardware (GPU/CPU models), software (OS, framework versions), and exact library versions used.
Report the performance of input compensation at 50% sparsity (in addition to 70%) and directly compare with the corresponding results reported in the original Wanda paper.
Analyze and report how a single shared compensation pool behaves across different input types and model architectures — e.g., show per-task or per-architecture compensation statistics or ablations.
Run direct experimental comparisons between the proposed input compensation method and prompt tuning methods to clarify their distinct advantages and limitations.
Report and compare computational cost and memory usage of input compensation versus prompt tuning across experiments.
Measure and report the number of trainable parameters and inference time differences between input compensation and prompt tuning.
Add a detailed ablation study on compensation pool design choices (e.g., number of candidate compensations and the attention mechanism) to show their impact on performance.
Include comparisons with fine-tuning-based sparse LLM strategies (for example DsNot) rather than only post-training sparsity methods.
Evaluate the method across a wider range of sparsity levels (e.g., 30%, 50%, 70%) to show how performance scales with sparsity.
Test the method on multiple model sizes to demonstrate generalizability across small to large LLMs.
Expand the task suite to include both classification and generation tasks to assess performance across different scenarios.
Provide a detailed analysis and comparison of computational cost and training time for input compensation versus other fine-tuning techniques.
Precisely define how sparsity of the matrix S is measured and applied (e.g., global magnitude pruning, structured pruning, pattern-based sparsity) in the paper.
Discuss the limitations of the low-rank plus sparse decomposition at high sparsity levels and whether the formulation remains valid across different sparsity regimes.
Analyze whether different types of sparse matrices (varying structure and distribution of nonzeros) can be adequately approximated by adding a low-rank matrix, with theoretical or empirical evidence.
Explain and/or demonstrate how the input compensation mechanism can recover pruned model performance given the potential limitations of the low-rank + sparse decomposition.
Explicitly clarify the novelty of Input Compensation (IC) by comparing it point-by-point to existing weight compensation techniques and justifying what is new.

Directly address the claimed duality between input and weight compensation and show how an IC formulation can be transformed into a weight-compensation formulation (or explain why it cannot) with formal derivations or examples.
Analyze the duality/transformations specifically for non-linear layers and state whether and when the input–weight duality holds or breaks down.
If baselines were not fine-tuned, re-run comparisons with those baseline models fine-tuned on the target dataset using the same procedure as IC.
Report and include in the paper the performance numbers for the fine-tuned versions of all baseline methods used in comparisons.

When reporting model sparsity, explicitly include the additional learnable parameters introduced by IC in the sparsity calculations and report both sparsity-without-IC-params and sparsity-with-IC-params.
Provide a rigorous analysis that explains how IC's input-dependency yields performance gains over input-independent weight compensation, including clear hypotheses and supporting experiments.

Perform a perturbation analysis (or comparable theoretical analysis) of the non-linear activation functions to justify why input-dependent compensation is expected to outperform weight-based compensation.
Evaluate IC's robustness by testing sensitivity to input noise and adversarial perturbations and compare these results to weight compensation methods.
Ensure the experimental protocol applies identical optimization procedures and hyperparameter settings to IC and all baseline methods to avoid training-procedure bias.
Report baseline performance both with and without fine-tuning so readers can see the isolated contribution of the IC technique.
Provide a detailed computational-cost analysis for IC including additional memory usage and runtime for training and inference, and compare these costs to weight compensation baselines.
Quantify and report the number and sizes of all additional learnable parameters introduced by IC and analyze how those parameters affect overall model sparsity and performance.
Study the impact of the compensation pool size by running ablation experiments that vary the pool size and reporting how performance and sparsity change.
Explore and report results for alternative compensation-pool architectures (e.g., different network depths, widths, or connectivity) and analyze their effects on performance and sparsity.

Analyze how sensitive IC is to the initialization of the compensation-pool parameters (run multiple initializations and report variance in performance) and describe any recommended initialization strategies.

Report how occlusions are handled, especially in real-world-like scenarios, and include experiments or analyses demonstrating robustness to partial occlusion and inter-object occlusions.

Provide comprehensive dataset statistics for the 360°-Motion Dataset: number of scenes, entity counts per scene, distribution of entity types, camera paths, lighting variations, and train/val/test splits.

Include detailed training details: loss functions, optimization settings, number of training steps/epochs, batch sizes, learning rates, and compute resources to improve reproducibility.
Provide clearer definition and justification of the evaluation metrics used for motion accuracy and video quality, and report confidence intervals or statistical tests for reported improvements.
Analyze and report failure cases systematically (e.g., when motion control breaks, artifacts appear, or object identity is lost) with illustrative examples and suggested remedies.
Detail how the entity-wise addition correspondence mechanism is implemented and justify why it is preferable to explicit matching or attention-based correspondence methods.
Provide experiments or analysis measuring how much the annealed sampling strategy trades off motion fidelity for visual quality, with objective metrics across annealing schedules.

If claiming to be the first to address 3D multi-entity motion control, expand the related-work discussion to explicitly compare and contrast with any relevant prior 3D motion or multi-entity generation works and clarify distinctions.
Report quantitative results for animal and other non-human categories separately (not only qualitatively) and include per-category metrics to support claims about entity diversity and customization.

c_real.text: Cite ControlNet and explicitly discuss its relevance to injecting conditional control into image/video diffusion models.
Expand the related work to include techniques that inject control signals into video foundation models via additional encoders that project control inputs into latent embeddings.
Include and discuss methods that achieve control by directly manipulating attention maps or video latents, not only encoder-based approaches.
Clarify and document how control signals are integrated into the base model (e.g., concatenation, addition, insertion) and which integration strategies were attempted or considered.
Compare the strengths and weaknesses of encoder-based control modules (learnable conv/linear/attention layers or frozen pre-trained encoders) versus attention/latent-manipulation approaches.
Broaden the dataset beyond human/animal categories and urban scenes to include generic object categories and diverse environments (e.g., forests, deserts, mountains).
Expand the dataset's motion diversity to include non-human/non-animal movements and more complex multi-object interactions.
Apply data augmentation strategies such as domain randomization or synthetic data generation to increase training diversity without prohibitive manual annotation.
Analyze and state whether the method is restricted to a small number of entities (e.g., N=2) and whether that restriction stems from training data distribution or model design.
Provide quantitative experiments or ablations that evaluate performance as the number of controlled entities increases (e.g., N=1,2,4,8).
Discuss concrete strategies to scale control to many entities, for example by using more powerful text encoders or new mechanisms to associate trajectories with specific text entities.
Add a detailed limitations section that documents challenges in generating videos with many diverse entities and how these limitations relate to the underlying video foundation model.
Relying primarily on synthetic data may hinder real-world generalization; evaluate and mitigate this reliance.
Using a dataset constrained to a 'city' setting limits output diversity; expand training domains beyond the city environment.
Unclear how the model performs beyond the MatrixCity platform; validate on external, real-world 3D/4D datasets.
No real-world evaluations or human perceptual feedback are reported; run user studies or human evaluations for perceptual quality.
Do not compare against recent 4D generation baselines (e.g., TC4D); add direct comparisons with such trajectory-controllable methods.
Clarify whether dataset and code will be released publicly after acceptance.
Expand training data to include a wider range of environments, object categories, and motion patterns.
Incorporate synthetic data variations that simulate different lighting, weather, and object-interaction conditions.
Increase the number and variety of assets to cover more object categories and associated motion characteristics.
Apply domain adaptation techniques (e.g., adversarial training) to reduce the synthetic-to-real domain gap.
Include at least small amounts of real-world video data in training or finetuning to improve generalization.
Develop methods to generate more complex, non-linear 3D trajectories rather than relying on simple linear motions.
Evaluate model robustness across variations in camera angles, lighting conditions, backgrounds, and occlusions.
Use data augmentation strategies to improve robustness to visual and motion variations.
Evaluate and report the model's ability to handle interactions among multiple entities in the scene.
Investigate transfer learning approaches to adapt the model to new environments and object types.
Include qualitative analyses (example videos, failure cases) assessing visual quality, realism, and adherence to specified 3D trajectories.
When comparing to baselines, report metrics focused on trajectory-control accuracy, perceptual realism, and computational efficiency.
Explore and report advanced evaluation metrics that better capture human perceptual quality (e.g., human-rated scores or perceptual-model-based metrics).
Expand dataset environments beyond the current City setting (e.g., add forests, deserts, indoor scenes) to improve generalizability.
Broaden the set of motion types beyond walking/jumping to include running, dancing, sports, social interactions, and navigation on complex terrain.
Acquire or synthesize a wider range of motion sequences (additional motion-capture datasets or procedurally generated motions) to realize the expanded motion diversity.
Resolve prevalent foot skating/floating by improving synchronization between entities' local (relative) motion and their global motion trajectories.
Align motion-capture animations explicitly with the 3D scene geometry during data generation so feet remain grounded and motion is spatially consistent.
Apply inverse kinematics (IK) to adjust limb poses based on global trajectories to produce more realistic foot placement and reduce sliding/floating.
Introduce pose and velocity constraints during data generation or training (e.g., limits on joint velocities, continuity constraints) to prevent unnatural movements.
Enforce explicit feet-ground contact checks or constraints (e.g., require contact events or bound the vertical distance of feet to ground) to eliminate floating artifacts.

Remove or justify the term 'ControlNet-like' for the Object Injector: either rename it or provide an accurate description of its architecture, initialization, and functional differences from a true ControlNet module.

Describe the Object Injector's exact placement (it is after the 2D spatial self-attention layer) and explain how that placement affects its behavior compared to a parallel zero-initialized ControlNet module.

Clarify and justify the statement on Line 334 that a static motion prompt 'amplifies movement' and that this reflects learning of 3D motion representations—provide mechanistic reasoning or empirical evidence.

Add analyses of the model's internal representations (e.g., activation patterns, ablations, or probing experiments) to support claims about learning 3D motion representations and responses to motion prompts.

Replace or supplement human-only pose evaluation with quantitative pose accuracy metrics: run a 3D pose estimator on generated videos, compare estimated poses to the Unreal Engine ground-truth, and report measures such as average joint distance error.

Investigate the poor alligator gait: analyze why the alligator's legs appear static and include diagnostics (e.g., frame-by-frame pose comparisons, visualization of predicted vs. ground-truth joint motions) to pinpoint the failure mode.
Evaluate the method with weaker, open-source base video generation models to test generality: run experiments on one or more less-capable models and report performance differences and failure cases.

Perform a systematic analysis of failure cases to identify patterns: characterize which types of trajectories, motion styles, or entity interactions lead to poor results and quantify their prevalence.
Expand the evaluation dataset by generating videos with a wider range of trajectories, entity types, and interactions so limitations can be identified across diverse scenarios.

Conduct ablation studies to determine which components are critical for success and which contribute to failures; report quantitative and qualitative differences when components are removed or altered.
Provide detailed computational resource and reproducibility information: report training time, hardware specs (GPUs/TPUs, CPU), memory usage, and inference costs for the reported experiments.

Discuss and/or experimentally evaluate scalability: assess whether the method can handle larger numbers of entities and more complex scenes, and describe limitations or necessary adaptations for scaling.

c_gen.text: Provide full implementation details to ensure reproducibility: specify the exact model architectures, layer sizes, activation placements, initialization, training hyperparameters, and the exact code or pseudocode used to compute the Jacobian J_N and the Data Information Matrix (DIM).

Increase experimental scale and statistical rigor: run experiments with larger sample sizes (beyond 100–250 points), multiple random seeds, and report standard errors or confidence intervals to support claims about behavior in high-dimensional spaces.

Include baseline comparisons to standard manifold-learning and representation methods (e.g., PCA, t-SNE, UMAP, and possibly autoencoders) so readers can assess the empirical advantages or differences of the proposed foliation approach.

Clarify and operationalize the construction of the "learning foliation": provide a clear, step-by-step algorithm or mathematical procedure that maps the theoretical distribution 𝒟 to an explicit foliation in data space used in experiments.

Formally define the proposed dataset distance metric based on the DIM spectrum, provide its mathematical properties, and justify design choices (e.g., which eigenvalues are used and how they are aggregated).

Validate the dataset distance metric statistically: run correlation analyses, hypothesis tests, and ablation studies to demonstrate significance and robustness of the observed relationship between DIM eigenvalues and transfer-learning performance.

Provide a concrete case study or application demonstrating practical implications (e.g., a full transfer-learning experiment or dimensionality-reduction pipeline) that uses the foliation to solve a real task and compares performance to established methods.

Detail the practical procedure for computing the DIM and estimating the distribution 𝒟 in high-dimensional input spaces (e.g., R^784), including computational complexity, numerical stability, sampling strategies, and implementation tricks.

Explicitly compare and contrast the learning foliation with traditional manifold-learning approaches: identify specific theoretical or empirical advantages, limitations, and situations where foliation-based modeling is preferable.

Demonstrate whether and how the foliation structure can be used for downstream tasks beyond transfer (e.g., clustering or visualization): provide algorithms, experimental results, or toy examples showing implementation and benefit for these tasks.

Evaluate sensitivity of the foliation structure and DIM to training procedure choices: run controlled experiments varying optimizer, learning rate, regularization, batch size, and report how these affect the foliation, rank behavior of J_N, and DIM spectrum.

c_real.text: Clarify the vague claim in Section 1 that refers to "concrete applications" and "more sophisticated mathematical modeling": explicitly name the target applications, the concrete challenges in those applications, and which mathematical tools or models are being proposed to address each challenge.

Reorganize Section 1: either remove or justify the discussion of general machine learning/deep learning topics and make the connection between that material and the rest of the paper explicit (why is it needed and how it relates to the proposed foliation approach).
Restructure Section 2 to present a clear, logical flow (e.g., subsections or a taxonomy) instead of a chaotic list; make the section's purpose and organization explicit to the reader.

Replace the list-style related work in Section 2 with a critical, synthesized review: categorize existing approaches, compare them to the proposed method, and highlight their limitations in the context of this paper.

Provide formal definitions for all mathematical notation used (e.g., explicitly define "span" as used in Eq. (3) and any other linear-algebraic or differential-geometry terms that are introduced without definition).
Make the results about singular points and their measure more accessible: include intuitive examples, concrete illustrations or visualizations, and step-by-step explanations of the main implications.

Discuss the physical/semantic meaning and potential applications of singular points: explain what these points represent in typical data spaces and how they impact knowledge transfer or learning tasks.

Address the generality of the theoretical results by discussing or analyzing whether and how they extend to other activation functions (provide proofs, counterexamples, or at least a clear discussion).
Strengthen the experimental evaluation by adding more varied and realistic datasets (beyond the few simple datasets used) to better validate the claims.
Demonstrate the practical utility of the DIM-eigenvalue / knowledge-transfer observation by applying it to a domain adaptation task and reporting quantitative transfer performance results.

Overhaul the introduction to focus tightly on the specific problem the paper addresses: state the precise problem, limitations of prior work, the paper's novel contribution, and the specific data types/applications targeted (avoid broad ML/DL background).
Explicitly state how the proposed method differs from existing approaches in terms of assumptions, what kinds of problems it can solve, and which gaps in the literature it fills.

Extend experiments beyond NIST: evaluate DIM and foliage analysis on more complex, higher-dimensional datasets (e.g., CIFAR, ImageNet subsets, non-Euclidean or graph-structured data) to test generalizability.

Run additional transfer-learning experiments that fine-tune more than just the last layer (e.g., fine-tune several top layers, whole network) and report results to determine whether observed transfer performance depends on pre-trained features or DIM.

Provide analyses that directly test correlation between DIM and knowledge transfer effectiveness (e.g., ablation studies, controlled baselines, correlation statistics, and significance tests) rather than inferring correlation from limited setups.

Augment Table 2 with detailed explanations: specify the experimental setups, which layers were fine-tuned, datasets used, metrics reported, and statistical significance so readers can interpret the claimed conclusions.

Explicitly discuss the theoretical assumptions about the neural network architecture: state which assumptions are required, their practical implications, and whether/how the theory extends to architectures with skip connections, attention, batch-norm, or other non-standard layers.

Either extend the theoretical/empirical analysis to other architectures or include experiments validating the theoretical predictions on at least one alternative architecture (e.g., ResNet with skip connections or a Transformer block) to assess applicability.

Cite and discuss prior dataset-distance methods (at minimum Alvarez-Melis & Fusi 2020 and Hua et al. 2023): explain methodological differences, potential relationships to DIM, and why DIM provides advantages or complementary information.

Clarify Figure 6's role: explicitly state how the visualization connects to the knowledge-transfer experiments, what conclusions should be drawn from it, and whether it provides predictive or merely illustrative evidence.
Unpack and clearly define the differential-geometry concepts for an ML audience: explicitly define 'distribution', 'leaf', 'foliation', and their relation to tangent spaces in the paper.

Provide a concrete, low-dimensional example that demonstrates integrability and the Frobenius theorem (show an integrable vs a non-integrable distribution and the resulting (non-)existence of leaves).
Precisely explain in what sense a 'data foliation' is more general than a 'data manifold'—state the formal relationship and give intuition or examples.
Fix the reused notation: D is used both for the dataset and for the DIM—use distinct symbols and update all references to remove potential confusion.

In Figs. 2 and 3, state the dimension of the distribution explicitly (if it is 9-dimensional) and clarify what is meant by 'moving tangentially to D' and 'same direction'—is this a sampled direction in dataspace or something else?

Explain what role integrability plays in producing Figs. 2 and 3: could equivalent visual directions be obtained for any activation function using definition (4), and how would a non-integrable distribution change those figures?

Report and analyze the DIM eigenvalue spectra on points that the model misclassifies versus correctly classified points to see if there are systematic differences.

Visualize and analyze eigenvectors corresponding to the smallest eigenvalues (and other salient eigenvectors) to give intuition for why those components correlate with generalization or validation accuracy.

Explore randomized algorithms for eigenvalue estimation as a way to approximate the DIM without forming the full matrix.
Investigate low-rank approximations of the Jacobian (or DIM) to reduce memory and compute costs and report the approximation error trade-offs.
Investigate block-diagonal approximations of the DIM that exploit CNN local receptive fields and evaluate their accuracy and resource savings.
Provide a detailed computational complexity and memory analysis of the proposed method and compare these metrics against existing dimensionality/geometric analysis approaches.

Evaluate whether DIM eigenvalues and foliation structure correlate with transfer learning performance on more complex datasets (not just MNIST-like data).

c_gen.text: Provide a clear hypothesis and/or mechanistic explanation for the origin of the distributional equivalence property (e.g., whether it arises from the training process, model architecture, data distribution, or distribution shift), and add targeted experiments or analyses to test these hypotheses.
Explicitly discuss the limitations of the complete neural collapse assumption used in the theory, including when it may not hold and how that affects the applicability of your results.

Run empirical experiments that break or reduce neural collapse (e.g., smaller models, different regularization, early stopping) and report how ensemble benefits and the distributional equivalence property change when collapse is partial or absent.

Provide a direct, self-contained empirical validation of the global gap (GG): define the GG metric precisely, show measurements and visualizations of GG across datasets and architectures, and demonstrate its relationship to ensemble performance.

Fix notational and terminological inconsistencies in the main text: explicitly define symbols like \hat{\mathcal{F}} vs \mathcal{F}, the meaning of \rho, and clearly state the exact forms of the Brier score and NLL used in each theorem.

Expand the interpretation of the Wasserstein distances and Kolmogorov–Smirnov (KS) p-values: explain what specific values imply about prediction distributions, justify their use, and provide guidance or thresholds for readers to interpret the results.

Analyze the implications of distributional equivalence for uncertainty quantification (UQ): include UQ metrics (e.g., calibration error, ECE, predictive interval coverage, sharpness), compare ensembles under your framework, and discuss whether and how UQ is improved or affected.

Include a detailed theoretical and empirical comparison between averaging-based ensembles and majority-vote ensembles: derive expected differences under your framework and report experiments comparing their performance across tasks.

Test the robustness of the distributional equivalence property to changes in the training regime by varying optimizers, learning rates, batch sizes, data augmentation, and regularization; report which settings preserve or break the property and the resulting impact on ensemble gains.

Evaluate the proposed two-model ensemble performance estimator on regression tasks and other non-classification settings to show whether the estimation scheme and distributional equivalence apply beyond classification.

Demonstrate the practical significance of the global gap (GG) in real-world scenarios by comparing it to other ensemble evaluation metrics (predictive performance, calibration, UQ metrics) and showing cases where GG provides unique or superior insight.

Provide theoretical relaxations or sensitivity analysis of the neural collapse assumption: derive bounds or modified results that hold under partial collapse, and state explicit conditions required for your closed-form expressions to remain approximately valid.

c_real.text: Clarify and rigorously define the distribution p_F: state its domain, assumptions, and how a draw F ~ p_F relates to the set over which you minimize (i.e., connect the minimization over ℱ with sampling from p_F).
Include the missing proof (or an explicit reference) of Proposition 3.1 in the main text or appendix.
Make the definition and semantics of \hat{f}(x) precise: formally define its domain, codomain, and interpretation (currently described informally as a 0/1 correct-prediction indicator).

Justify the application of φ to averages of binary indicators (e.g., φ(E[F(X)]) and φ(\hat{f}(x)) in Eq. (8)): explain what φ represents, why it is meaningful on averaged accuracies, and any assumptions behind this operation.

Address the implicit assumption in Lemma B1 and Theorem 4.1 that E_x[\hat{f}(x)] = E_x[\hat{g}(x)] for all \hat{f},\hat{g] ∈ \hat{ℱ} (i.e., equal accuracies across models): either remove/relax this assumption, prove it under explicit conditions, or state and justify it formally.
If p_F has support only on a subset of ℱ, change Theorem 4.1 to minimize over the support of p_F (or otherwise justify why minimization over the whole ℱ is valid).

Provide a formal criterion for membership in the set of 'trained models' ℱ and a construction of p_F (e.g., trained models defined as those achieving empirical loss below a threshold or target accuracy, and p_F induced by the stochastic training algorithm).

Prove (or give conditions for) that the distributional equivalence property is inherited by surrogate models \hat{f} from original models f: show the transformation f → \hat{f} preserves distributional equivalence, and state required assumptions.

Discuss the implications of distributional equivalence for model accuracy and generalization: explain how equal-distribution claims relate to accuracy on unseen data and to the notion of 'well-trained' models.

Clarify whether the observed distributional equivalence is a consequence of the specific training procedure (e.g., optimizer stochasticity) or a more general property of neural networks; provide analysis or experiments to support this.

Revisit the choice of using binary surrogates \hat{f}: consider using functions that operate on output probabilities instead of 0/1 indicators, and discuss advantages or trade-offs of this change for the theoretical results.

Provide empirical evidence measuring the distribution of model output probabilities (e.g., whether outputs collapse to one-hot): add experiments or statistics to justify assumptions about predicted-probability behavior.
Clarify and empirically demonstrate the generalizability of the main contribution (distributional equivalence) to other loss functions beyond those used in the paper.

Provide rigorous justification or relaxation for the distributional equivalence assumption that P(ĥf(x)) = P(ĥg(x)) for all models ĥf, ĥg and all inputs x; explain why this strong assumption is valid.
Extend the analysis in Section 4.1 to use fuller class-probability information—e.g., analyze l = p(y) - p(ŷ) or the full predicted distribution—instead of only the target-class probability.
State explicitly which loss function was used to train the models in Section 4.1.
Run experiments with alternative loss functions (for example label smoothing and mean squared error) and report how distributional equivalence and convergence behavior change.
Analyze gradients of different loss functions with respect to model parameters to explain how loss choice may influence convergence and the resulting output distributions.
Investigate scenarios where distributional equivalence breaks down by varying training regimes (e.g., learning rates, batch sizes) and report the effects on the degree of equivalence.
Evaluate the distributional equivalence property across different model architectures and datasets with varying complexity to assess robustness and limits.
Provide a theoretical analysis or derive bounds quantifying how approximate (rather than exact) distributional equivalence affects ensemble performance.
Introduce a practical metric or set of diagnostics that practitioners can use to quantify the extent to which output distributions of different models in an ensemble are equivalent.
If distributional equivalence does not generalize to certain losses or settings, state this limitation explicitly and discuss its implications for the applicability of the theory.
Experimentally explore interventions (e.g., adversarial training, data augmentation) that might promote distributional equivalence and evaluate whether they improve ensemble performance.

Provide a dedicated analysis or explanation for why the majority vote classifier produced unsatisfactory results after discovering the distributional equivalence property (either theoretical reasoning or targeted experiments to isolate the failure modes).
Evaluate whether the distributional equivalence property holds on larger, more realistic datasets (e.g., ImageNet or similarly large benchmarks) rather than only on CIFAR-10.
Investigate the effect of stronger regularization on the observed distributional equivalence by running experiments that include techniques such as dropout, weight decay, and batch normalization.

Design experiments to test whether the observed distributional equivalence is an artifact of model overconfidence on small datasets—e.g., vary model capacity, dataset size, and regularization to isolate the overconfidence effect.
Include results for Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL) in Section 4.4: provide theoretical derivations analogous to those for the Brier score and report empirical NLL results on the same experiments.
Add error rate plots to Figure 6 (or analogous figures) so readers can compare error rates alongside loss metrics and understand correlations between loss trends and actual accuracy.

Repeat key experiments using standard training practices that were omitted (SGD with momentum, Adam, and commonly used data augmentation strategies) to verify that findings are robust to typical optimizers and augmentation.

Either justify the choice of omitting momentum and data augmentation in the main experiments or move that simplified training setup to an ablation; show results under both simplified and standard training to demonstrate generality.

Provide theoretical or empirical arguments extending the distributional equivalence claim beyond high-accuracy/overconfident regimes (i.e., explain why the property should hold in general, not only when DNNs are highly accurate and overconfident).

When reporting ensemble performance, include analyses that separate variance vs. calibration effects (e.g., show calibration metrics or decomposition) to clarify whether improvements stem from variance reduction or improved probability calibration.

Clarify the phrase "only differ in the training stochasticity": explicitly state which sources of randomness are considered (e.g., random initialization, data shuffling, data augmentation, dropout, minibatch sampling) and which are held constant.

Provide an analysis of why homogeneous ensemble members exhibit distributional equivalence, testing hypotheses such as dataset bias, fixed architecture, regularization, or training protocol as potential causes.

Run experiments isolating random initialization: train multiple models with different initialization seeds while keeping data order and other factors fixed, and measure how initialization alone affects output distribution similarity.

Run experiments isolating data shuffling/order: train multiple models with identical initialization but different data shuffles (or minibatch orders) and quantify the effect on output distribution similarity.

Evaluate the effect of architectural choices on distributional equivalence by testing multiple architectures (e.g., depth, width, skip connections, normalization layers) and reporting whether and how the phenomenon changes.

Assess how optimization algorithms and hyperparameters (e.g., SGD vs Adam, learning rate schedules, weight decay, batch size) influence distributional equivalence by running controlled comparisons and reporting metrics.

Test whether distributional equivalence generalizes across dataset types: evaluate on varied datasets (e.g., balanced vs imbalanced, synthetic vs real, different domains) and report where the property holds or breaks down.

Incorporate generalization theory analyses: measure flatness of minima (using established metrics) for ensemble members and test whether flatter minima correlate with stronger distributional equivalence.

Investigate the relationship between loss-landscape sharpness and ensemble diversity: quantify sharpness and diversity metrics and analyze whether sharper minima correspond to greater or lesser output-distribution diversity.

Provide practical guidance for the proposed two-model ensemble-performance predictor: give a step-by-step procedure, required assumptions, and recommended diagnostics to decide when the predictor is reliable.
Quantify the computational cost and scaling of the two-model prediction method: report runtime, memory, and how estimation error changes as a function of ensemble size and model complexity.

Explicitly discuss limitations and failure modes of the method: identify conditions (data distributions, architectures, optimizers, degree of stochasticity) under which the two-model prediction may be inaccurate or inapplicable.

The theoretical framework depends on neural collapse assumptions; evaluate and report where these assumptions fail and how that affects the main results (e.g., limited-data or high-variability settings).
The paper lacks analysis of how distributional equivalence affects uncertainty quantification; add experiments and discussion on its implications for uncertainty estimation.
Experiments are limited to CIFAR-10 and TinyImageNet; run experiments on more complex domains (e.g., text datasets and multimodal data) to assess generalizability.
Analyze the sensitivity of the distributional equivalence property to training hyperparameters (learning rate, optimizer, initialization seed, batch size) via ablation studies.
Discuss and empirically evaluate the scalability of the two-model performance estimation method to larger-capacity models and to ensembles with many members.
Investigate the effect of label noise on distributional equivalence by running experiments with varying levels and types of label noise.
Evaluate how input dimensionality influences distributional equivalence by including higher-dimensional datasets (e.g., NLP or other non-image tasks).
Study the impact of class imbalance on distributional equivalence and report quantitative results across different imbalance ratios.
Quantify distributional equivalence using appropriate distance metrics (e.g., Wasserstein distance, KL divergence) and report these measures in experiments.
Measure ensemble prediction diversity explicitly (e.g., per-example variance across members) and correlate this diversity with ensemble confidence and calibration.
Analyze how distributional equivalence affects the ensemble's ability to capture aleatoric versus epistemic uncertainty, with concrete metrics and experiments.
Test whether distributional equivalence leads to overconfident predictions in certain scenarios and, if observed, propose and evaluate mitigation strategies.
Provide a detailed analysis of limitations and failure modes of the two-model estimation method, specifying conditions under which the estimator is reliable or biased.
Empirically evaluate whether using more than two models improves estimation accuracy and quantify trade-offs between computational cost and estimation performance.

c_gen.text: Add direct experimental comparisons to methods that estimate the noise level during training (e.g., Kim et al. 2022), reporting the same metrics and datasets so readers can judge relative performance and robustness.

Evaluate UNSURE on more challenging and higher-resolution datasets (for example CIFAR-10 and high-resolution medical image sets) rather than relying on MNIST, to demonstrate generalizability to complex, high-resolution images.

Provide a detailed theoretical derivation or formal justification for the generalization of UNSURE to Poisson-Gaussian and exponential-family noise models, explicitly stating assumptions and proving the estimator properties in these cases.

Quantify the computational overhead introduced by UNSURE: report wall-clock training time, per-epoch or per-batch time, number of estimator evaluations, FLOPs, and peak memory; compare these numbers to supervised training and other baselines.

Clarify how the proposed expressivity–robustness theoretical framework relates to and builds upon prior theoretical work: add a focused literature comparison and explicitly state what is novel versus previously discussed trade-offs.

Empirically and theoretically compare UNSURE to existing approaches specifically in Poisson-Gaussian and exponential-family noise settings, to establish novelty and relative performance in these regimes.

Expand experimental analyses to include sensitivity studies: vary key hyperparameters (kernel size, network capacity), different noise levels and SNRs, and report how performance changes to assess robustness.
Include experiments that test robustness to noise-level misspecification: compare UNSURE, level-estimating methods, and supervised baselines when the assumed noise parameters are wrong.

Report variability and statistical significance for reported results (e.g., mean ± std over multiple random seeds or cross-validation folds) to show whether improvements are consistent and significant.

c_real.text: Provide a detailed analysis of the computational cost (time and memory) of the proposed method during training and inference, and compare these costs quantitatively to relevant baselines.

Describe and evaluate strategies to reduce the additional computational burden (e.g., approximation techniques, reduced-frequency evaluations, caching, or algorithmic optimizations) and report their effect on performance and cost.

Explicitly discuss the assumption behind the construction in Eq. (14) that the set of s-dimensional covariance matrices includes the true covariance, and state that this implies a low-dimensional covariance structure.

Analyze the consequences when the true covariance does not lie in the s-dimensional plausible set (i.e., when the low-dimensional assumption fails), including whether the method effectively projects the true covariance and how this impacts estimator bias and performance.

Propose and evaluate possible solutions for the correlated-noise case when the low-dimensional covariance assumption fails (e.g., increasing s, using structured covariance models, regularized estimation, or robust covariance estimators) and report empirical results.

Temper or revise the claim that the method performs 'close' to SURE in Table 1: quantify the PSNR gap, report statistical significance, and rephrase any statements that might be misleading given a ~1 dB difference.
Clarify and enumerate the specific scenarios or noise-model conditions where the proposed method offers a clear advantage over existing SURE-based or other self-supervised techniques.

Expand the theoretical exposition: provide a fuller derivation and explanation of the use of Lagrange multipliers for constrained self-supervised losses, and explicitly show how the constraints affect the optimization objective.

Include an analysis of the optimization landscape (e.g., visualization, characterization of stationary points, or convergence behavior) that demonstrates how the constraints guide learning and influence convergence.

Add a broader and more rigorous empirical comparison that includes additional self-supervised and blind/noise-agnostic baselines (especially methods addressing unknown noise structures), and report results across those baselines.
Extend experiments beyond MRI reconstruction to a more diverse set of imaging tasks and modalities (e.g., natural image denoising, CT, ultrasound) to evaluate generalizability.

Evaluate the method under a wider range of noise types and levels, including non-Gaussian, heteroscedastic, and spatially correlated noise, and report performance trends as noise characteristics vary.
Add additional evaluation metrics beyond PSNR (at minimum SSIM, and if relevant perceptual metrics such as LPIPS) to better characterize visual quality and structural fidelity.
Construct and report results on a comprehensive benchmark suite that consolidates multiple datasets, noise models, and metrics so readers can assess consistency and robustness of the proposed method.
Discuss limitations of the proposed method explicitly in the manuscript: list failure modes, scenarios where performance degrades, and potential remedies for each listed limitation.

Investigate and report the sensitivity of the method to optimizer choices and hyperparameter settings; provide concrete hyperparameter selection guidelines (ranges, default values, and tuning recommendations).
Analyze robustness to different random initializations and optimizer settings (e.g., multiple seeds), and report variability (e.g., mean and std) of key performance metrics.

Provide concrete suggestions or modifications to the ZED estimator to handle high-entropy signal distributions (e.g., regularization, alternative estimators, dimensionality reduction, or entropy-aware modeling) and evaluate them empirically.

Summarize and tighten the related work section to focus on the most relevant prior methods and explicitly articulate the novelty and incremental contribution of the current work relative to those methods.
Evaluate the denoising method and noise-level estimation on experimental cryo-EM images (real cryo-EM data), not only on synthetic corrupted images.
Expand experimental validation to real-world datasets with complex noise characteristics (e.g., signal-dependent or non-stationary noise) rather than only synthetic noise models.
Test the algorithm on images with higher spatial frequencies and more detailed textures/structures to assess robustness and to check for artifacts or excessive blurring on high-detail content.

Analyze and report the limitations and accuracy of the noise-level estimator in extreme/low SNR scenarios, including quantitative evaluation of estimator bias and variance when noise dominates the signal.
Investigate and report the sensitivity of the noise-level estimation to choices of hyperparameters and network architecture (provide experiments or ablations varying those choices).

Discuss the stability of the proposed method when the dimension of range(A^T) is small, and specify theoretical or empirical limits on the range dimension required for accurate noise-level estimation.

Perform experiments that vary the dimension of range(A^T) (or simulate operators with different range dimensions) to empirically determine how small a range can be before noise-level estimation and denoising performance degrade.

Discuss and evaluate how an ill-conditioned operator A^T or limited measurements affect the method's noise-level estimation and denoising performance, and if applicable propose remedies or regularization strategies.
Use a more descriptive title that reflects the paper’s focus (e.g., "Unknown Noise Level Estimation via Self-supervised Stein's Unbiased Risk Estimator").
Analyze the sensitivity of the proposed method to different noise distributions (report performance across several noise models).
Evaluate the method on non-additive Gaussian noise types (e.g., Poisson, multiplicative, impulsive) and report results.
Evaluate the method when the noise level varies spatially across the image and report how performance degrades or adapts.
Test and report performance on other data modalities or datasets beyond the specific imaging inverse problems used in the paper to demonstrate generalizability.
Include empirical comparisons with other self-supervised denoising methods that do not rely on explicit noise-level estimation, and discuss advantages/disadvantages.
Provide full practical implementation details: list hyperparameters (learning rate, optimizer, batch size, number of epochs) used in experiments.
Report sensitivity analyses showing how key hyperparameter choices (e.g., learning rate, optimizer) affect performance.
Discuss and quantify the computational cost (runtime, memory) of the method and its scalability to larger images/datasets.
Clarify how the proposed loss and approach can be integrated into existing self-supervised frameworks and whether it can be combined with other self-supervised techniques.
Provide a theoretical analysis of the method’s convergence properties (e.g., conditions for convergence, convergence rates).
Derive and report bounds on the estimation error of the proposed noise-level estimator.
Investigate and report conditions under which the proposed loss is convex and whether optimization is guaranteed to reach a global minimum.
Analyze the relationship between the proposed method and existing theoretical frameworks for self-supervised learning, highlighting similarities and differences.

Provide a thorough theoretical analysis of the proposed estimator: state assumptions and derive its statistical properties (e.g., bias, consistency, mean-squared error or risk, and conditions under which it is well-defined).

Analyze convergence of the optimization algorithm used to fit the estimator: provide convergence guarantees (local/global), rates if possible, and conditions on the objective and algorithm that ensure convergence.

Move and expand the data/model description: explicitly define the learning framework (supervised vs self-supervised), state what data are available (y1,...,yN) and when clean targets are absent, and place this clarification at the start of Section 2 as suggested.

Avoid misusing the term “estimator” for population quantities that are not computable from the data; replace with more precise terminology (e.g., population risk, population objective, population minimizer) and consistently distinguish between population and empirical objects throughout the paper.
Add and cite a reference for the closed-form formula presented in Eq. (4) to help non-expert readers reproduce/verify the derivation.
Correct the apparent domain error above Eq. (5): replace ℝ^m with ℝ^n (or otherwise justify the current choice of ambient space) so the notation is consistent.
Fix formula (14): verify and correct the expression so the gradient of f appears where required (update derivation and surrounding text to reflect the corrected term).

Add a dedicated literature discussion connecting the method to empirical Bayes: cite relevant works, explain similarities and differences, and position the contribution relative to empirical Bayes approaches.

Address the potentially misleading title: either revise the title to accurately reflect that the paper studies estimators f for which SURE is independent of σ, or explicitly state this restriction in the title/subtitle and early in the abstract/introduction.

In the proof of Theorem 3, explicitly state and justify the use of strong duality: cite the exact theorem (e.g., the specific Luenberger result), verify the required constraint qualification (existence of a strictly feasible x with G(x)<θ) in the current setting, and if the condition does not hold, revise the proof or provide an alternative argument.

Provide a non-asymptotic statistical analysis: derive finite-sample bounds on the estimation error of the empirical estimator relative to the population minimizer and quantify the convergence rate as a function of sample size N and problem parameters.

Analyze optimization behavior when using neural networks as the function class: study conditions under which the training algorithm converges (e.g., landscape properties, implicit regularization), and discuss the influence of network architecture and optimization hyperparameters on convergence and generalization.

Explicitly define the self-supervised learning procedure in the introduction: explain how noisy observations are used to train the denoiser without clean ground truth, and include a concrete, worked example applying the method to a specific image reconstruction task.

Clearly distinguish and name the population version versus the empirical version of the estimator in the main text: state how each is computed/approximated, discuss practical implications of the distinction, and review/adjust terminology throughout the manuscript to avoid confusion.
