c_gen.text: Add a dedicated Methodology section that details the experimental procedures, training protocol, evaluation pipeline, and overall study design.
Add a Results section with quantitative performance metrics for each experiment, clear tables/plots, and concise summaries of findings.
Provide precise implementation details for each data augmentation technique, including the exact algorithm or library used.
For synonym replacement, specify the approach (thesaurus lookup, embedding nearest neighbors, language model-based), the source of synonyms, and selection criteria.
For random deletion, insertion, and swapping, report the exact rates/percentages, whether they are fixed or sampled, and how positions are chosen.
For back translation, state the language pairs used, the translation systems or models employed, and any post-processing applied.
Describe the three datasets in detail: data sources, provenance, sizes (utterances/characters/examples), train/validation/test splits, and any filtering or preprocessing steps.
Explain how the datasets differ in dialogue style, context, genre, and why they were chosen to evaluate generalization.
Include clear baseline comparisons, at minimum a model trained without any augmentation, and report performance differences relative to that baseline.
Compare results against existing methods in the literature (or standard augmentation/finetuning baselines) to contextualize the contribution.
Run and report ablation studies that isolate the individual impact of each augmentation technique on model performance.
Operationalize and define "robustness" explicitly (e.g., robustness to input noise, ability to preserve character voice) and state measurable criteria.
Provide an evaluation methodology for maintaining character-specific tone and linguistic habits (suggest metrics, annotation scheme, or human evaluation protocol).
Perform statistical validation of results (e.g., t-tests, ANOVA) and report significance measures, confidence intervals, and effect sizes.
Include an error analysis that identifies common failure modes, examples of model errors, and which augmentations reduce specific errors.
Justify the choice of LLaMA3 and LoRA, explaining why they are appropriate and discussing whether results are expected to generalize to other base models or adaptation methods.
Report reproducibility details: random seeds, hyperparameters, number of training steps/epochs, optimizer settings, batch size, and compute resources used.
Provide qualitative examples (input prompts and model outputs) illustrating how augmentations affect character fidelity and robustness, along with brief analysis.

c_real.text: Clarify the provenance of the training data: specify the sources, selection criteria, and curation pipeline used to assemble the dataset.
Describe how the proposed dataset relates to existing datasets and prior work, and clearly state which specific problems or gaps it is intended to solve.
Measure and report training data leakage: assess whether the TV shows in the dataset appear in common LLM pretraining corpora and quantify any overlap.
Include a baseline fine-tuning experiment without data augmentation so the effect of augmentation can be isolated and quantified.
Compare fine-tuning results to few-shot and other prompt-based scenarios (e.g., in-context learning) to show whether tuning is necessary or advantageous.

Explicitly describe the fine-tuning procedure used for character-specific models: how are training examples constructed and how is the autoregressive loss computed when multiple characters speak in a single example?
Shorten or relocate the extended background on low-rank fine-tuning (LoRA) and reallocate space to provide more experimental details and results.

Resolve the discrepancy between abstract and experiments: either include results for the third dataset mentioned in the abstract or revise the abstract to reflect which datasets are actually evaluated.
Fix the supplementary material packaging: provide a proper decompressible zip (or alternative readable format) so reviewers can access the supplementary files.
Evaluate how well prompt-based solutions perform on the same tasks and report quantitative comparisons (e.g., few-shot prompts, instruction tuning-free baselines).
Test whether the reported results generalize to existing dialogue datasets by running the same fine-tuning and evaluation pipeline on one or more public dialogue corpora.
Substantially shorten Sections 2 and 3 that describe past techniques (EDA, paraphrasing, LoRA); focus those sections only on methods directly used or on how prior work motivates your contributions.

Develop and evaluate novel data augmentation techniques specifically tailored to character-based dialogue rather than relying primarily on well-known methods like synonym replacement and backtranslation.
Include additional datasets covering a wider range of conversational styles, character personalities, or domains to demonstrate generalizability beyond the two narrow, character-specific datasets.
If adding more datasets is not possible, perform a detailed analysis of dataset characteristics and explain why particular augmentation techniques succeed or fail on these datasets.
Investigate and report on methods to mitigate paraphrasing overfitting, e.g., alternative paraphrasing algorithms, regularization strategies, or domain-adaptive paraphrasing.
Experiment with off-the-shelf large language models for data augmentation and evaluate them as stronger baselines in comparison to the current augmentation methods.
Explore sentiment-aware and context-sensitive paraphrasing approaches that preserve character voice and semantic constraints when generating augmented dialogue.

Provide a clear, explicit discussion of the computational trade-offs from using LLaMA3-8B: what memory/performance adjustments were made, and how these choices may limit model performance or generalization.

Fix invalid entries in the reference list (e.g., remove or correct 'Unknown Author') and expand the reference list to include more recent and relevant literature on augmentation and LLM-based augmentation.
Revise Figure 3 so it contains substantially more information or compress/remove it if it cannot justify occupying a full page.
Adjust Table 1 formatting (reduce font size or reformat) so the table does not appear artificially enlarged to fill space.
Add stronger experimental baselines (explicitly include LLM-based data augmentation and other competitive methods) to better contextualize the contributions.
Clarify the major claim/contribution of the paper: state a single, explicit research question and list the paper's contributions in the introduction.
Provide formal descriptions and detailed explanations of each data augmentation technique mentioned (e.g., include pseudocode, equations, or algorithmic steps) rather than only naming them.

Add complete experimental details: datasets used, preprocessing steps, train/validation/test splits, model architectures, hyperparameters, random seeds, and training procedures so experiments are reproducible.
Report full quantitative results in tables: include exact metric values, variance (standard deviation or confidence intervals) across multiple runs, and clear comparisons to appropriate baselines.

Improve all plots and figures: include clear axis labels, legends, units, error bars where appropriate, and captions that explain what is plotted; replace or redesign plots that currently show no discernible trends.

Discuss and analyze unexpected or 'mysterious' results: provide hypotheses, additional experiments, or diagnostic analyses to explain surprising outcomes or re-run experiments if results are unreliable.

Remove screenshots taken from other papers or explicitly cite and obtain permission for reused figures; if reusing results, recreate the figures with proper attribution and ensure they are clearly marked as reproduced.

The data augmentations only perform local word/structure permutations and do not address the thematic-framework distributional shift you motivate; design augmentations that alter theme-level content or otherwise diversify the thematic context to better target the stated core challenge.
The paper fails to attribute the augmentation methods to prior work; add proper citations and discuss how your augmentations relate to and differ from existing studies.
You did not evaluate a finetuning-only baseline (no data augmentation); add this baseline to isolate the effect of augmentations.

BLEU and ROUGE scores are extremely low — include qualitative examples of generated outputs and an analysis explaining why automatic scores are so low; consider additional or alternative evaluation metrics more appropriate for personalization.

The observed decreasing loss could reflect language collapse (e.g., model learning to output Chinese); explicitly evaluate forgetting and language collapse by reporting language-specific metrics (perplexity or accuracy per language) and the distribution of output languages.

The evaluation setup is unclear and may simply test phrase repetition in context; clarify the evaluation objective and add downstream role-playing evaluations (e.g., interactive/human evaluations or task-based measures) to demonstrate suitability for real-world roleplay.
The study uses only two role-playing characters and lacks broader coverage; expand experiments to more characters and to community datasets to better contextualize results.
There are no comparisons to simple baselines such as prompting-based roleplay; include comparisons to prompting and other straightforward baselines.

Sections and evaluations unrelated to personalization (e.g., Unsloth, external benchmark evaluations in Figure/Table 1) distract from the core contribution; move these to the appendix and prioritize space for substantive experiments on personalization.
Figure 3 has mislabeled or duplicated legend entries (e.g., 'BT Loss' repeated); fix all figure legends and verify that plots and labels are correct and unambiguous.
The references contain incorrect metadata/placeholders (e.g., 'Journal Name', 'Unknown Author'); correct citation details and ensure each referenced work has accurate author and venue information.

The manuscript devotes excessive space to background and delays concrete experiments until halfway through; compress or relocate background material and present the experimental setup earlier for clearer narrative flow.
There are duplicated or repeated phrases in the text (e.g., 'Wei and Zou (2019) Wei & Zou (2019)'); thoroughly proofread and remove duplicated content and other similar editorial errors.

c_gen.text: The theoretical analysis assumes linear manifolds only; extend the theory or provide formal arguments and experiments showing how (and when) the results generalize to curved/nonlinear manifolds.

Provide complete, precise model and training details (network architectures, layer sizes, activations, optimizer, learning rates, batch sizes, number of epochs, noise schedules, random seeds) to enable reproducibility.

Describe exactly how the Jacobian of the score function was computed from trained networks (autodiff vs. finite differences, per-sample vs. batched estimation, numerical stabilization), and release the code used for these computations.

Validate that spectral gaps observed on natural images reflect true latent manifold dimensionality rather than artifacts: e.g., apply independent manifold-dimension estimators, synthetic curved-manifold datasets with known dimension, or ablation verifying invariance to data preprocessing.

Compare the spectral behavior of diffusion models to other generative models (VAEs, GANs, normalizing flows) on the same datasets to contextualize claims about diffusion models avoiding manifold overfitting.

Expand ablation studies to include model architecture and training hyperparameters (network depth/width, regularization, learning-rate schedules, optimizer choice, noise schedule parameters) and report how these affect the spectral gaps and phases.

Demonstrate robustness of spectral-gap observations across multiple random seeds, different network architectures, and varied training regimes to rule out that the gaps are artifacts of a specific setup.

Provide principled guidance for choosing the parameter Δ that defines gap width: include a sensitivity analysis of results to Δ and propose statistical or information-theoretic criteria for selecting it in practice.

Quantitatively evaluate the phase transitions (trivial, manifold coverage, manifold consolidation): provide precise definitions, metrics for detecting transitions, statistical significance tests, and report how transitions correlate with sample quality metrics (e.g., FID).

Systematically compare results between synthetic and natural datasets: quantify gap clarity and significance (e.g., normalized gap sizes, confidence intervals), and discuss implications for the generality of the theoretical framework.
Release the experimental code, scripts for dataset preprocessing, and exact checkpoints used so other researchers can reproduce the Jacobian spectra and phase analyses.

c_real.text: Provide a stronger, detailed justification in Section 7 for the discrepancy between the analytical (replica) and empirical curves in Fig. 6, listing possible causes and diagnostics to distinguish them.
Run the same experiments on the isotropic case (where an analytic solution is available) to check whether the analytical/empirical discrepancy persists.
If the discrepancy persists in the isotropic case, investigate the numerical Jacobian computation as a potential primary cause (compare different Jacobian estimation methods and report results).
Study the effect of training dataset size on the empirical results: perform convergence experiments with increasing dataset size and report how the learned score and spectral measures change.

In Section 8, replace the qualitative claim that spectral gaps are smoothed by manifold complexity with quantitative analysis of the eigenvalue distribution (e.g., eigenvalue density plots, statistics of gap sizes) for CIFAR-10 and CelebA.

Explain why the estimated MNIST manifold dimensionality (≈700) is much larger than the number of classes: discuss whether estimates reflect local vs global dimensionality and how that relates to the method used.
Validate the manifold dimensionality estimates by applying alternative dimensionality-estimation methods and reporting comparisons and consistency (or lack thereof).

Clarify the definition and intended role of the 'support score' and the 'stable latent manifolds' in Eq. (5); if the support score is not used later, either remove it or explicitly state and justify its purpose.
Correct the wording at line 131 from 'radius √t' to 'radius proportional to √t' (or otherwise justify the exact scaling used).

Provide precise, testable definitions/criteria for the three phases (trivial, manifold coverage, consolidation), and show how these phase boundaries are identified in the experiments; reconcile the phenomenology described in Section 5 with the later plots.

Analyze training and model factors that could prevent a sufficiently expressive neural network from matching the analytic score (e.g., model capacity, optimization/early stopping, regularization, estimator bias) and report related diagnostics.
Specify in the Introduction that the Jacobian being discussed is the Jacobian of the score (and ensure this is made explicit wherever the Jacobian is used).

Add an explicit discussion of the limitations of the proposed approach (e.g., finite-sample effects, numerical Jacobian errors, model misspecification) and how these limitations affect the interpretation of the results.
Theoretical analysis is restricted to a Gaussian on a linear submanifold; extend the theory to non-linear manifolds and non-Gaussian densities to improve generalizability.
Explore perturbation methods or approximation techniques that connect the idealized Gaussian/linear setting to more realistic distributions.
Study specific more-complex cases such as mixtures of Gaussians and manifolds with curvature to see how the eigenspectra change under these conditions.
Provide theoretical bounds on the approximation error introduced when the score function is estimated by a neural network.
Report exact experimental details about training data size—specifically the number of samples drawn from the distribution used to train the score model.
Systematically vary the number of training samples and evaluate how dataset size affects the accuracy of the learned score and the resulting eigenvalue distribution.
Plot eigenvalue distributions for different dataset sizes and quantify deviations from theoretical predictions using explicit metrics (e.g., Wasserstein distance, KL divergence, or spectral norms).
Investigate and report whether discrepancies between empirical and theoretical eigenspectra are due to modeling limitations, insufficient data, optimization/training issues, or other factors.

Provide full neural network and training details for reproducibility: number of layers, layer sizes, activation functions, optimizer, learning rate schedule, batch size, regularization, and training epochs.
If the method fails to detect intrinsic dimensionality on real datasets, explicitly discuss potential practical uses of the eigenspectra analysis for training or understanding diffusion models.
Experiment with modifications that might improve applicability to real data, such as denoising the estimated score function or designing robust feature extraction from the eigenspectra.
Add a detailed limitations section and suggest alternative approaches for estimating intrinsic dimensionality of complex data manifolds.
The linear manifold assumption is overly restrictive; either relax this assumption or explicitly discuss its limitations and the implications for the generalizability of your theoretical results.

Extend the theoretical analysis to nonlinear manifolds (or provide a clear roadmap) by developing a more sophisticated mathematical framework (e.g., tools from differential geometry) that connects your results to curved manifolds.

Implement and analyze local linear approximations of nonlinear manifolds: compute the Jacobian spectrum in local tangent regions and attempt to aggregate these local analyses to make statements about the global manifold.

Explore kernel methods or other implicit feature mappings that might make the data manifold more linear in a higher-dimensional space and test whether your existing theoretical framework applies there.

Improve the presentation and interpretability of the CIFAR-10 and CelebA results (Figure 7): provide clearer figures, more informative captions, and quantitative metrics that support the claimed three-phase behavior.
Experimentally investigate how different noise levels and sampling strategies affect the observed spectral patterns on real datasets and report these sensitivity analyses.

Apply more robust spectral analysis techniques that are less sensitive to noise and outliers (e.g., robust PCA, trimmed spectral estimators, or random-matrix-theory-based denoising) and report whether conclusions change.
Compare your empirical spectral results to baseline manifold-learning and dimensionality-reduction methods (e.g., PCA, Isomap, diffusion maps, UMAP, LLE) to contextualize strengths and limitations.

Provide a detailed discussion of dataset-specific characteristics (such as hierarchical structure, class imbalance, or intrinsic dimensionality) that could influence the observed spectra on CIFAR-10 and CelebA.

Provide a clearer, more rigorous theoretical justification for the conjecture in Section 5: derive or argue formally how the Jacobian spectrum relates to subspace separation in tangent spaces of curved manifolds.

Validate the conjecture empirically on synthetic datasets with known manifold structures and controllable curvature to systematically test whether the observed phenomenology holds as curvature varies.

Clarify the paper's contribution and framing: explicitly state whether the work is primarily theoretical, empirical, or conjectural, and align claims and scope accordingly (e.g., temper claims if theory only covers linear models).
Discuss the limitations and potential challenges in extending your approach to more complex or highly curved manifolds, including practical and theoretical obstacles.
Introduce the relevant mathematics for manifolds earlier in the paper to clarify the manifold overfitting problem and improve logical flow.
Add a dedicated section or subsection early on that defines 'data manifold' and 'latent manifold' and explains their relevance to generative modeling and diffusion processes.
Explicitly clarify the distinction between the data manifold and the latent manifold and describe how the diffusion process interacts with each.
Provide concrete examples of how manifold overfitting manifests in likelihood-based models to motivate the problem and the need for alternative approaches.
In Section 6.2, clearly state the purpose of the linear random-manifold model (e.g., whether it is a toy theoretical framework or intended to approximate random datasets).
Spell out the assumptions and limitations of the linear manifold model and discuss how these assumptions affect the generality of the theoretical results.
Give an intuitive explanation for choosing the linear random-manifold model and comment on which observed phases or behaviors you expect to persist for more complex, non-linear manifolds.

Explicitly state whether the analysis in the linear random-manifold section is intended to study diffusion models encountering random datasets, and if so, describe the connection to empirical data distributions.
Provide a precise mathematical definition of M_t in Equation (5), including how it is constructed from the underlying data manifold and the noise process.
Clarify whether M_t is intended to be a union of spherical shells (e.g., shells of radius sqrt(t) around the latent manifold); if not, give a precise characterization of its geometry.
State whether M_t is assumed to be a smooth manifold or a more general geometric object, and describe how its geometric properties evolve with t.
Justify the introduction and use of M_t in the analysis: explain why considering this specific space is necessary for the interpretations derived from Equation (6).
Provide a more rigorous mathematical treatment of M_t (proofs or references) to support claims about where noised data lie and to remove ambiguity in the manuscript.
If M_t is a questionable modelling choice, either reconsider its use or explicitly defend the modeling strategy and discuss alternatives to improve mathematical clarity.
Clarify vague/unclear points throughout the paper by adding precise equations or citations where arguments are currently informal, especially for items only clarified later in Section 6.
Explicitly compare and contrast your findings with 'Diffusion Models Encode the Intrinsic Dimension of Data Manifolds' and state what is novel in your analysis versus that prior work.
Explicitly compare and contrast your results with 'Dynamical Regimes of Diffusion Models' and state which results in your paper are new or different from that analysis.

Add a dedicated section or paragraph that connects the proposed three phases to manifold overfitting, explaining step-by-step how each phase helps (or does not) prevent manifold overfitting compared to likelihood-based models.

Clarify the claim (lines 044–045) that separation/decomposition phenomena cannot be explained by critical phase transitions because they are linear processes — provide a clear argument or mathematical justification for this statement.
Clarify the distinction between your work and the referenced 'B. et al. 2024a' — state concretely how your explanation of subspace separation differs from theirs.

Elaborate on the statement (lines 091–095) that divergence of the data distribution implies model density becomes larger during training; provide a derivation or reference that links distribution divergence to increase in model density.
Provide empirical evidence or literature citations supporting the claim (lines 096–097) that diffusion models are not affected by manifold overfitting.

Provide a detailed derivation or intuitive explanation (lines 140–146) for why perturbations aligned to the manifold tangent correspond to small eigenvalues while orthogonal perturbations correspond to large eigenvalues.

Clarify Figure 1: state whether it is plotted at a fixed time t, explain why the x-axis is 'latent dimensionality', and provide the procedure or mapping used to associate eigenvalues/singular values to latent dimensionality (including the steps from Stanczuk et al. 2022 if needed).
Define the terms 'singularities' and 'cross-over events' in the paper's context (line 172) and justify the claim that phase separation are cross-overs rather than genuine phase transitions.
Define σ_k where it first appears and explain under what conditions σ_{k+1}^2 ≪ σ_k^2 would hold in your data/model assumptions.
Define 'asymptotic closure' as used in the manuscript and clarify whether the end time of Phase II can be infinite and how you approximate or detect this closure in practice.
Define the variable r at its first appearance (line 481) and state its role in subsequent equations/arguments.

Provide a more explicit explanation linking spectral gaps in the Jacobian to concrete changes in the generative/sampling process — e.g., detail how the score function changes across phases and how those changes affect sample quality or dynamics.

For Figure 7 and other experiments, analyze and show how the observed eigenspectrum transitions correspond to the paper's three-phase theoretical framework (provide time-resolved spectra and interpretation).
Expand empirical validation beyond MNIST to additional, more complex datasets, and report eigenspectra at multiple time points to test the generality of the three-phase behavior.
Fix formatting issues: conform to the provided paper template and standardize/correct the bibliography entries and formatting.

Describe the exact procedure for computing the Jacobian J_N and the Data Information Matrix (DIM): give formulas, numerical methods (autodiff vs finite differences), software/libraries, and computational cost/complexity.

Give an explicit algorithm or pseudocode for constructing the learning foliation from the distribution D in practice, including how leaves are identified, how rank changes are detected, and how this is implemented on finite datasets.
Release code and scripts used for training, computing the Jacobian/DIM, generating figures, and reproducing experiments (or include these in supplementary material) to enable reproducibility.

Expand experiments to larger and more realistic sample sizes (beyond 100/250 points), including results on full datasets or sufficiently large subsets, to assess statistical significance in high-dimensional settings.
Compare the proposed approach to standard manifold learning and representation baselines (e.g., PCA, t-SNE, UMAP, autoencoders) using quantitative metrics to evaluate relative effectiveness.

Provide a formal, precise definition of the dataset distance metric based on the DIM spectrum, including any normalization, weighting of eigenvalues, and a mathematical rationale for the chosen formula.

Statistically validate the proposed distance metric: report correlation coefficients with confidence intervals, perform hypothesis tests or permutation tests, and quantify effect sizes for the observed relationship with transfer accuracy.
Benchmark the DIM-based distance metric against existing dataset similarity measures from the literature and report comparative results to contextualize its performance.

Perform ablation studies to evaluate sensitivity of the foliation and DIM to training choices: vary optimizer, learning rate, regularization, batch size, model depth/width, and initialization, and report how the foliation/DIM change.

Detail the method for estimating the distribution D in high-dimensional input spaces (e.g., R^784): specify estimators used, smoothing or regularization choices, sample complexity considerations, and numerical stability measures.

Demonstrate practical applications with concrete case studies: show how the foliation can be applied for knowledge transfer, dimensionality reduction, clustering, or visualization, and report quantitative metrics for these tasks.
Report robustness across random seeds and model initializations: provide variance/error bars for the DIM spectrum and downstream transfer/validation metrics to show the stability of results.

c_real.text: Clarify the vague statement in Section 1 about "cases most relevant for concrete applications": specify which concrete applications and specific challenges are meant, and state which sophisticated mathematical tools are proposed to address each challenge.

Reorganize the introduction (Section 1): remove or tightly connect broad machine learning/deep learning discussion to the paper's focus, and make the logical flow from motivation → problem statement → limitations of prior work → proposed approach explicit.

Explicitly state the problem being solved, the limitations of existing approaches, and the novel contribution of this paper (what is new and why it matters) rather than using vague motivation language.
Specify the types of data and application domains the method is designed for (e.g., image classification, domain adaptation, sensor data), instead of referring generically to "concrete applications."

Restructure Section 2: replace the current unordered list of related works with a structured, critical literature review that groups prior work by approach and highlights their limitations relative to the proposed method.
In Section 2, explicitly identify the gaps in the literature that this paper addresses and clarify how your approach differs from existing methods in assumptions, goals, and applicable problem types.

Provide formal definitions for missing mathematical notation (e.g., define "span" and any other linear-algebra constructs used in Eq. (3) and elsewhere) to ensure the mathematics is precise and self-contained.
For the results on singular points and measure, add intuitive examples and visualizations (e.g., a simple low-dimensional manifold with singular points) to make the concepts easier to understand.

Discuss the physical or data-analytic meaning and potential applications of singular points and their measure: what do these points represent in real data, and how do they affect learning or transferability?

Investigate and report whether the theoretical results extend to other activation functions (e.g., ReLU, tanh, leaky ReLU): either present proofs or empirical evidence for other activations, or state limitations clearly.

Strengthen experiments: evaluate on more diverse and realistic datasets, and demonstrate the practical utility of the DIM-eigenvalue / knowledge-transfer connection—ideally by applying it to a domain adaptation task to show improved transfer performance.
Expand experiments beyond NIST datasets to include more complex, high-dimensional, or non-Euclidean datasets to test generalizability of the DIM and foliage analysis.

Run experiments that fine-tune more than just the last classifier layer (e.g., multiple layers or full fine-tuning) to determine whether transfer performance is due to DIM or pre-trained feature representations.
Include comparisons with other transfer learning methods and baselines to establish a stronger correlation between DIM values and knowledge transfer effectiveness.
Clarify and expand the descriptions in Table 2: explicitly state experimental setups, which layers were fine-tuned, hyperparameters, datasets used, and how results support the claimed conclusions.
Explicitly discuss the architectural assumptions underlying the theoretical results and their implications for applicability to other network types.

Attempt to extend the theoretical analysis (or provide sketches/limitations) to more general architectures, including networks with skip connections, attention layers, or other non-standard components.

Investigate and report how different architectural choices (e.g., residual connections, attention, activation functions) affect the data foliage structure and the validity of the DIM—either via analysis or targeted experiments.
Cite the related dataset-distance literature, specifically Alvarez-Melis & Fusi (2020) and Hua et al. (2023), and explain how this work relates to or differs from those approaches.

Provide a quantitative and methodological comparison between DIM and optimal-transport-based or dynamic-flow dataset-distance methods (including strengths/weaknesses and empirical performance comparisons).
For Figure 4, clarify how singular points are defined in networks using ReLU activations, or state and demonstrate whether the proposed method is compatible with ReLU-based networks.
For Figure 6, explicitly state how the visualization connects to the knowledge transfer experiments and clearly state the conclusions readers should draw from it.

Design control experiments to test whether observed transfer performance is driven by the DIM versus pre-trained feature quality (e.g., randomize or ablate pre-trained features, compare with randomly initialized networks).

Clarify and expand the exposition of differentiable geometry concepts (distribution, tangent spaces, foliation, integrability, Frobenius theorem) for a machine-learning audience, including simple, concrete examples.
Fix ambiguous notation: D is used both for the dataset and for the DIM in close proximity (line 113); choose distinct symbols and update all references.
Explain precisely what the blue lines in Fig.4 represent, why they are irregularly spaced, and the significance of the point colorings; update the figure caption and text accordingly.
Rewrite L227 to correctly state whether the foliation or the distribution fails to fill data space; explicitly define what 'naturally associated' refers to and the antecedent of 'it'.
Clarify the dimensionality of the distribution used in Figs.2 and 3 (e.g., if it is 9-dimensional per L193) and explain what is meant by 'moving tangentially to \mathcal{D}' in that context.
Define what is meant by 'same direction' in L212 (e.g., a sampled direction in data space, a principal eigenvector, etc.) and state how directions were chosen for the visualizations.

Explain the role of integrability in producing Figs.2 and 3: state what aspects of those figures rely on integrability, whether similar visual directions could be found for any activation, and what would change for a non-integrable distribution.
Clarify L306 notation: are p and N(x) equivalent? If so, remove redundancy or explain the difference and justify including both.
Qualify the claim that individual training points are 'identifiable'; add analyses measuring identification accuracy at the sample level versus population-level statistical differences.

Provide an analysis showing which eigenvalues drive the identifiability result on MNIST (largest vs smallest) and reconcile this with the observation that lowest eigenvalues correlate best with validation accuracy in Table 2.
Make explicit how 'data foliations' are more general than 'data manifolds': give a formal definition and examples illustrating the relationship and differences.
Improve or expand Figs.5 and 8: show more traversals, include starting points from other datasets, and explain the selection criteria and significance of the displayed traversals.

Describe practical computation of the DIM: give complexity and memory estimates, explain how automatic differentiation is used, and discuss feasibility on large models (e.g., ImageNet-pretrained ResNet).
Report and analyze the DIM eigenvalue spectra for misclassified points: indicate whether misclassified examples have distinctive spectra and discuss implications.

Tie the eigenvalue spectrum results to geometric properties: explain how eigenvalues relate to local curvature or intrinsic dimensionality at a point and support claims with analysis or illustrations.
Justify the choice of activation functions in the XOR experiment (ReLU vs GeLU): explain what specific properties are being compared and why these activations were selected.
Provide a more detailed discussion of the computational cost and memory requirements of the proposed DIM estimation method for high-dimensional inputs and deep architectures.

Present visualizations or analyses of eigenvectors corresponding to the smallest eigenvalues (not only eigenvalues) to help interpret the relationship between low-eigenvalue subspaces and generalization.

Revise the claim that training data points are identifiable to reflect statistical overlap in eigenvalue distributions; explicitly state limitations (e.g., need many samples) and quantify false positive/negative rates.

Wherever experimental claims are made (e.g., identifiability, significance of visualizations), add quantitative metrics, statistical tests, or ablations to support the conclusions rather than relying on visual inspection.
The DIM formulation is computationally infeasible for typical image sizes (e.g., a 224x224x3 image would require ~85GB memory); address this scalability bottleneck or justify practicality.
Experiments are limited to 28x28 grayscale images; evaluate the method on higher-resolution, realistic image sizes to demonstrate scalability.
Validation is restricted to simple MNIST-like datasets with 10 classes; extend experiments to more complex datasets such as CIFAR-100 and a subset of ImageNet.
Investigate how the number of classes affects the foliation structure and DIM eigenvalues by conducting experiments with varying class counts.
Analyze the correlation between DIM eigenvalues and transfer-learning performance on complex datasets, not just final accuracy but geometric properties of learned representations.
The theoretical analysis focuses on ReLU networks and simple architectures; extend the analysis to modern architectures such as ResNet and VGGNet.
Transfer-learning experiments only retrain the last layer; perform contemporary transfer protocols including partial fine-tuning and adapter modules.
Analyze how network depth and width influence the foliation structure and DIM properties across different architectures.
Explore approximation techniques for the DIM (e.g., randomized eigenvalue estimation or low-rank approximations) to avoid computing the full matrix.
Investigate block-diagonal or localized approximations of the DIM for convolutional networks that exploit local receptive fields to reduce memory and computation.
Provide a more detailed computational-complexity analysis of the proposed method and compare runtime/memory costs with existing approaches.
In the draft, use citation macros correctly (e.g., \citet and \citep) as requested.
When performing transfer-learning experiments, analyze geometric properties of representations before and after fine-tuning and report how these changes relate to transfer performance.
Provide a detailed analysis of the relationship between dataset complexity and the geometric properties of learned representations, with empirical evidence.

c_gen.text: The proof of Theorem 5.1 is incomplete: provide the missing final part of the derivation, fill any algebraic or logical gaps, and explicitly state all intermediate lemmas/assumptions used to obtain the final bound.

Provide substantially more intuitive explanation and examples for the weak MVI condition and the tangent residual: include geometric/operational intuition, simple toy examples, and discuss the implications of these concepts for convergence in practice.

Broaden experimental validation beyond two-player zero-sum matrix games: evaluate SOGRM+ (and baselines) on more complex or real-world settings (e.g., poker, GAN training tasks, continuous games, or LLM fine-tuning scenarios) to demonstrate practical impact.

Make the connection between theoretical convergence bounds and empirical results explicit: present direct comparisons (e.g., overlay theoretical rate curves with empirical residual decay), explain any discrepancies, and state whether empirical behavior matches the proved rates.

Add a summary of notation and key definitions (table or diagram): consolidate symbols, variable relationships, and assumptions in one place to reduce density and improve readability and reproducibility.

Include clear pseudocode or step-by-step implementation details for SExRM+, SPRM+, and SOGRM+: provide algorithm boxes, per-iteration computational complexity, and any implementation subtleties needed for reproduction.

Provide full experimental details for reproducibility: list all hyperparameters, initialization choices, random seeds, dataset generation procedures, and make code or scripts available (or state why not).

Clarify how the choice of quadratic regularizer psi(·) affects convergence: analyze theoretically and/or empirically how different regularizer parameters change convergence behavior and provide guidance for selecting psi in practice.

Clarify the constant C_0 in r^{dg}(x) ≤ C_0 r^{tan}(x): provide the exact value or usable bounds for C_0, state whether it depends on the game or algorithm, and include a derivation or empirical estimation procedure.

c_real.text: Expand the presentation to provide a clearer high-level explanation of the main ideas and proof strategy (use more space to motivate intuition before technical details).

Provide a detailed, evidence-based justification for the strong claim that "Neither of these techniques can be used alone to prove the last-iterate convergence of RM+ variants" or else soften/rephrase the claim to avoid overstating novelty.

Give a step-by-step, intuitive breakdown of the tangent residual argument: explicitly explain how the tangent residual is defined, how it bounds or relates to distance to the Nash equilibrium, and how that relationship is used to obtain last-iterate convergence.

Add a detailed comparison with prior methods (especially Cai et al. (2023)): list differences in assumptions, outline where previous proofs fail under only monotonicity or weak MVI, and state precisely what the new approach changes.

Include a concrete toy/example problem that demonstrates a scenario where previous techniques fail (or are inconclusive) while the proposed tangent residual approach succeeds, to make the distinction explicit.

Explain the mathematical obstacles that prevent direct application of other techniques (e.g., limit-point dynamics, analyses requiring strong monotonicity) to RM+ variants; identify exactly which steps fail and why.

Clarify the use of the term "OMD" for the update in Equation (5): either justify why the inclusion of the f_i(x_i) term still fits the OMD framework or rename the method to avoid misleading terminology.

Provide a detailed account of the modified OMD update's properties (with the f_i(x_i) term), explain how it relates to the standard OMD update, and state whether this modification is necessary for the convergence results (if necessary, explain why standard OMD would fail).
Clearly state which parts of the analysis and which results rely on the smoothness assumption, and explain how far the techniques and proofs can be stretched without smoothness.
Extend the theoretical analysis to non-smooth games (remove or relax the smoothness assumption) and identify which lemmas/proofs must change.
Investigate whether the concept of a tangent residual can be generalized to non-smooth settings or replaced by an alternative distance-to-equilibrium measure appropriate for non-smooth games.
Explore alternative techniques from non-smooth optimization and variational inequality literature to establish the relationship between loss and strategy profiles in non-smooth games.
Analyze specific classes of non-smooth games (e.g., games with piecewise linear payoff functions) to provide concrete examples, highlight challenges, and test proposed generalizations.
Derive asymptotic rates of convergence for the last-iterate analysis rather than only qualitative or non-asymptotic statements.
Leverage the claimed equivalence to OMD (online mirror descent) to attempt to obtain explicit convergence rates for the algorithm.
Analyze convergence behavior under stronger structural assumptions (e.g., strong monotonicity or other regularity conditions) to obtain sharper or faster convergence rates.
Provide a more comprehensive empirical evaluation by testing SOGRM+ on structured benchmark games such as Kuhn Poker and Goofspiel in addition to random zero-sum games.
Include experiments on games with mixed strategies and imperfect information to assess practical performance in more realistic settings.
Make the paper self-contained by describing each analyzed algorithm in detail: give update rules, key properties, and include pseudocode for every algorithm discussed.
Explicitly state in the experimental setup whether updates are performed simultaneously or in an alternating fashion.
Explain in detail why the paper's OMD update includes a qt term, since this deviates from the standard prox-mapping OMD formulation.
Clarify the use of a fixed Bregman divergence with a single generating function q in your OMD variant and how this relates to the standard OMD formulation.
Provide a citation or original source for the specific OMD variant used (the form with the qt term and fixed generating function), or justify its derivation.
Clarify the definition of 'asymptotic last-iterate convergence' used in the paper, distinguishing convergence of the duality gap from pointwise convergence of iterates to a Nash equilibrium.
Explicitly acknowledge in the text (and update Table 1) that your results prove duality-gap convergence to zero but do not establish point convergence of the iterates to a single Nash equilibrium.
Investigate whether restarting SExRM+, SPRM+, or your SOGRM+ admits game-dependent linear last-iterate convergence in zero-sum games, and report any findings or limitations.
Attempt to prove (or provide counterexamples for) point convergence of the iterates to a Nash equilibrium for your algorithms in monotone games, and report the outcome.
Report variance or confidence intervals for experimental results over multiple random seeds/runs to assess statistical robustness.
Include a parameter sensitivity analysis showing how algorithm performance depends on key hyperparameters (learning rates, regularization, restart frequency, etc.).
Compare the proposed algorithms against more baseline methods, specifically including algorithms known to achieve point convergence, to provide a more comprehensive evaluation.

More explicitly discuss the limitations of your theoretical results: specify the classes of games for which convergence guarantees hold and the challenges in extending the analysis to more general game settings.
Expand experiments beyond two-player zero-sum matrix games to evaluate SOGRM+ on a broader set of game types.
Include experiments on general-sum games (more complex payoff structures) to test applicability beyond zero-sum settings.
Evaluate SOGRM+ on games with non-convex strategy spaces to assess performance in non-convex optimization settings.
Test the algorithm on games with more than two players to demonstrate scalability and robustness to increased player count.
Provide a thorough hyperparameter sensitivity analysis focused on the learning rate η, showing how different η values affect convergence speed and stability.
Add an intuitive explanation (and, if possible, theoretical justification) for why SOGRM+ permits larger learning rates η compared to other methods.
Run experiments varying η across a wide range and report the resulting convergence behavior, including any instability or divergence thresholds.
Compare SOGRM+ against a broader set of state-of-the-art game-solving algorithms (not just RM+ variants) across diverse game types.
Discuss practical implications of the theoretical results: how they translate to real-world performance, example applications, and potential practical challenges.
Analyze and report the computational cost of SOGRM+ (e.g., runtime, memory, per-iteration complexity) and compare it to alternative methods.

Report performance of the unpruned (original) models for all evaluated tasks so readers can judge absolute performance and trade-offs between pruning+IC and the full model.

Provide missing implementation hyperparameters for reproducibility: for image classification list SGD specifics (learning-rate schedule, weight decay, momentum, batch size, number of epochs); for NLP provide AdamW details (learning rate, warmup ratio, total training steps, weight decay, batch size).

Specify the exact Inception model version used to compute FID and the number of generated images used for FID evaluation; if using multiple seeds/samples, report variance or confidence intervals.

Provide theoretical or empirical analysis identifying conditions under which IC is effective in non-linear settings (e.g., characterize classes of functions, sparsity regimes, or model architectures where IC provides gains).

Report a parameter-count and memory-breakdown table showing: baseline model params, pruned model params, compensation-pool params (absolute and percent increase), and corresponding memory (bytes) so readers can assess the practical cost of adding IC (2.3M figure alone is insufficient).

Provide ablations that quantify the runtime/memory overhead versus performance gain trade-off for different compensation-pool ranks and sizes (e.g., show accuracy/perplexity/FID vs. latency/memory for several pool sizes).

Clarify the exact evaluation protocol for the zero-shot NLP benchmarks (BoolQ, RTE, etc.): specify which metric is reported (accuracy, F1, etc.), prompt templates, data splits used, and any calibration or ensembling procedures.
Release code, random seeds, and exact training/inference scripts or provide detailed pseudo-code to enable reproducibility, and indicate the seeds used for the reported results.

c_real.text: Add quantitative comparisons of computation cost and inference speed (FLOPs and wall-clock latency) between the baseline pruned models and models with IC, reported across multiple sparsity levels and model sizes.
Explicitly measure and report the extra cost introduced by the additional encoder pass (the ViT run shown in Fig.1), and quantify its impact on end-to-end inference latency.
Report the memory footprint of the compensation pool (parameters and runtime memory), and analyze how it scales for large models and multiple tasks.
Run and report structured sparsity experiments using the 2:4 sparsity pattern and compare results (accuracy and speed) against the current 4:8 experiments.
Provide full implementation details for IC: exact initialization of the compensation pool, optimizer, learning rate schedule, batch size, number of training epochs, and any regularization used.

Clarify the training procedure for the compensation pool: whether it is trained end-to-end with the pruned model, trained in a separate stage, or trained on a different dataset, and provide the exact training protocol.
Describe precisely how learned compensation vectors are applied to inputs (mathematical formulation and data flow), including any normalization or position-specific handling.
Provide training time (wall-clock) for all reported experiments, broken down by stage if there are multiple training phases (e.g., pruning, compensation pool training).
Analyze and report how IC interacts with different pruning algorithms (e.g., magnitude pruning, Wanda, SparseGPT), including experiments showing effectiveness per pruning method.
Include experiments comparing combinations such as 'wanda+owl' versus 'wanda+owl+IC' to evaluate whether IC can be effectively combined with OWL-style sparsity methods.
Release code or provide detailed reproducibility artifacts (training scripts, configs, pre-trained compensation pools) to enable exact replication of results.
Provide a clear algorithmic description or pseudocode and a step-by-step workflow diagram for the IC method to remove ambiguity about the method's implementation and execution.
Explicitly report accuracy vs computational-cost trade-offs (e.g., accuracy vs latency and accuracy vs memory) so practitioners can assess real-world applicability.

Discuss hardware compatibility and deployment considerations: whether IC benefits from GPU acceleration, how it maps to accelerators that support structured sparsity (e.g., 2:4), and any modifications needed for efficient runtime.

Clarify how the compensation pool is shared across tasks: specify whether a single pool is trained on one large dataset and then reused, or whether pools are task-specific, and report any cross-task training protocol and results.
Quantify the additional computation and memory costs introduced by the encoder and attention-based compensation mechanism during inference.
Provide a detailed breakdown of the FLOPs and latency specifically attributable to the encoder and the attention-based compensation mechanism.
Perform the FLOPs and latency analysis on multiple hardware platforms (e.g., CPU, GPU, edge device) to assess practical runtime implications.
Measure and report end-to-end inference time of the proposed method and directly compare it to baseline pruning methods to quantify runtime overhead.
Provide a quantitative analysis of the memory footprint of the compensation mechanism and its impact on the model's overall memory usage.
Give a more detailed, step-by-step explanation of the optimization process for the compensation pool and explicitly clarify the differences between the approaches described in Sections 5.1 and 5.2.
Specify the initialization strategy for the compensation pool parameters, including the exact distributions and parameter settings used.
Report the learning rates, optimizer choices, and number of training epochs used for the image and text compensation pools, and justify these choices.
Discuss and justify any differences in hyperparameters and training strategies between modalities (image vs text) and model architectures.
Provide a sensitivity analysis showing how the method's performance varies with key hyperparameters for the compensation pool (e.g., learning rate, initialization, number of epochs).
Justify the choice of loss functions used to train the compensation pool and explain exactly how the compensation pool is integrated into the overall training and optimization pipeline.
Evaluate the method under the structured 2:4 sparsity constraint and compare results with existing structured pruning methods.

Clearly define how sparsity is calculated in reported experiments, explicitly stating whether encoder and compensation-pool parameters are included and whether reported sparsity refers to the whole model or only the original pruned parameters.
List the full experimental environment for reproducibility, including exact hardware (GPU/CPU models), software (OS, framework versions), and relevant library/configuration settings.

Analyze and report how the shared compensation pool behaves across different input types and model architectures, e.g., by showing compensation outputs or performance variation per input type and architecture.
Report results for input compensation at 50% sparsity and directly compare those results with the corresponding numbers from the original Wanda paper for a fair comparison.
Include a direct experimental comparison between the proposed input compensation method and prompt tuning methods to clarify distinct advantages and limitations.
Compare the two integration styles (addition used by your method vs concatenation used in prompt tuning) and report their relative impact on performance.
Include comparisons with sparse LLM fine-tuning strategies that use backpropagation (e.g., DsNot) rather than only post-training sparsity methods.
Provide a precise definition of the sparsity of matrix S (e.g., how sparsity percentages are measured and applied across layers/weights).
Analyze whether the low-rank plus sparse decomposition assumption holds across different sparsity levels, and report any breakdown points empirically.
Theoretically or empirically investigate whether different types of sparse matrices can be represented or compensated for by adding a low-rank matrix; provide counterexamples if not.
Report computational cost metrics (training FLOPs, inference FLOPs), memory usage, and latency when comparing input compensation to prompt tuning methods.
Report the number of trainable parameters and the observed inference time differences between input compensation and prompt tuning baselines.
Perform an ablation study on the compensation pool size (number of candidate compensations) and show its effect on performance and cost.
Perform an ablation study on the attention mechanism used to select/weight compensations and report how different attention designs affect results.
Expand experiments to a wider range of sparsity levels (e.g., 30%, 50%, 70%) to show how the method scales with pruning severity.
Run evaluations across multiple model sizes to demonstrate the generalizability of the proposed approach.
Include a broader set of tasks (both classification and generation tasks) to assess method performance across different application scenarios.
Provide a detailed analysis of computational cost and wall-clock training time of your method compared to other fine-tuning techniques for sparse LLMs.
Discuss the theoretical limitations of the low-rank plus sparse matrix decomposition, especially at high sparsity levels, and how these limitations affect your method.
Explain the mechanism by which input compensation recovers pruned-model performance given the limitations of the low-rank plus sparse decomposition, with empirical evidence.
Study and report how different patterns/types of sparsity (structured vs unstructured, layerwise distributions) impact the effectiveness of input compensation.

Explicitly discuss the duality between input compensation (IC) and weight compensation: state when they are equivalent, when they differ, and why IC is novel or advantageous (particularly for non-linear layers).

Provide a rigorous theoretical analysis (e.g., perturbation analysis of non-linear activation functions) that demonstrates how input-dependent compensation can yield advantages over input-independent weight compensation.

Ensure fairness in comparisons by fine-tuning all baseline methods on the target dataset using the exact same optimization procedure and hyperparameter settings as used for IC, then report those fine-tuned baseline results.
Report baseline method performance both with and without fine-tuning so readers can see the isolated contribution of the IC technique.
When computing and reporting model sparsity, explicitly include the additional learnable parameters introduced by IC (do not omit IC-related parameters from sparsity calculations).
Provide a detailed analysis of computational overhead introduced by IC, quantifying additional memory and time costs at training and inference, and compare these costs to weight compensation methods.
Experimentally evaluate the effect of the compensation pool size on performance and sparsity by reporting results across multiple pool sizes.
Explore and report results for different compensation-pool architectures, analyzing how architecture choices affect performance, sparsity, and computational cost.
Analyze sensitivity to initialization of the compensation pool parameters (e.g., report variance over multiple inits or include ablations showing initialization impact).
Evaluate robustness of IC to input perturbations by testing sensitivity to input noise and adversarial examples and compare those robustness results to weight compensation techniques.

Quantify and report the parameter overhead of IC (e.g., absolute extra parameters and percentage increase relative to the base model) and analyze how this overhead affects overall performance and sparsity trade-offs.

c_gen.text: Expand the experimental evaluation to additional tasks and domains beyond PDDL generation and TravelPlanner to demonstrate RePrompt's generalizability.
Increase the size of the TravelPlanner training set (or perform robustness checks like cross-validation) since 10 data points is too small to support the claimed robustness.
Include comprehensive comparisons to existing automatic prompt engineering (APE) baselines (e.g., PromptAgent, APE) in both evaluated domains, not just a single baseline in TravelPlanner.
Provide the exact prompt templates and prompt structures used in all experiments so others can reproduce the prompt-engineering setup.
Describe implementation details of the summarizer and optimizer LLMs (model variants, prompt templates given to them, decoding parameters, temperature, chain-of-thought settings, etc.).
Specify the convergence criteria and stopping conditions for the iterative optimization process (how you decide when to stop updating prompts).
Precisely define what constitutes an "epoch" in the context of RePrompt (what operations are performed per epoch and what data is processed).
Explain how the summarizer selects which parts of the chat history to use when generating prompt updates and state the criteria or heuristics used to identify "key insights."
Quantify the impact of the auxiliary checker that converts prompts into step-by-step format: report how often it is used and show performance with and without it.

Explain and evaluate how RePrompt handles tasks where the agent's output format is ill-defined or when the initial prompt is not step-by-step; provide adaptation strategies or experiments on such cases.
Provide a more structured limitations section and a clearer, prioritized roadmap for future work addressing the method's known weaknesses.

Propose and evaluate concrete techniques to mitigate hallucination errors (e.g., verification modules, retrieval augmentation, or fact-checking steps) since RePrompt currently cannot address hallucinations.
Perform targeted ablations that remove key components (e.g., the summarizer, chat-history feedback, prompt-updates) to isolate and quantify each component's contribution to performance.
Release code, seeds, hyperparameters, and exact experimental settings to enable reproducibility of results.

c_real.text: Rewrite the paper for clarity — improve organization and prose so readers can follow the method and experiments without pervasive comprehension challenges.

Formalize the method mathematically or algorithmically: introduce unambiguous notation for each component (feedback summarizer, prompt optimizer, LLM interaction) and state precise input/output definitions.

Include a concrete, step-by-step example that walks through a single RePrompt iteration using the new notation, showing how feedback is collected, summarized, and applied to update the prompt (including the exact prompt changes).
Explicitly define the claimed 'gradient analog' for prompt optimization: state what corresponds to gradients, directions, step sizes, and how semantic feedback maps to prompt updates.
Describe the PromptAgent baseline used in the Travel Planner experiment (architecture/behavior, hyperparameters, and how it was run) so its comparison is reproducible and interpretable.

Clarify convergence and termination criteria for the prompt update loop (lines 209–219): precisely define what 'converges' means, what signals terminate iterations, and whether convergence implies empty feedback or inclusion of all proposed solutions.

Explain the 'manually encoded solutions' referenced in the optimizer (line 226): list the common problems these encode and justify why manual encoding is preferable to letting the method discover or handle them.

Clarify the role of in-context learning and zero-shot generation in the experiments (lines 243 and 293): specify where ICL is used, what examples are provided, and how setups differ across experiments.
Clearly differentiate RePrompt from ReAct and Reflexion: explain structural and algorithmic differences, specifically how RePrompt operates without a solution checker compared to those methods.

Directly compare and contrast RePrompt with the cited prior works (e.g., TextGrad, automatic prompt optimization with gradient descent, and Promst): explain similarities, differences, and why RePrompt is novel relative to these papers.
Demonstrate with concrete examples or analyses how the summarization component prevents early-stage errors and reduces randomness compared to alternative feedback or reflection mechanisms.

Provide empirical or theoretical evidence that the specific design choices in RePrompt yield meaningful advantages over existing approaches, addressing the novelty concerns and showing measurable improvements.
Expand the empirical evaluation beyond the two datasets (TravelPlanning and Guan et al.) by adding multiple, diverse datasets to strengthen the generalizability of your claims.
Directly compare performance to the recent bank-of-critics work (https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.20625) and explain why your final pass rates (e.g., 3.89%) are much lower than their reported >20%.
Provide an analysis explaining the weak improvements on the Guan et al. dataset (only slight gains on the Household domain) and propose or test modifications to improve performance there.

Evaluate and report how well the intermediate-feedback approach handles domains with complex interdependent attributes (e.g., TravelPlanning attributes like budget and accommodation) where a single change triggers many downstream effects.
Analyze and report the scalability of the 'batch fix' method, including how many iterations are typically required and whether the approach remains practical as problem complexity grows.
Measure and report the computational cost of the iterative prompt-refinement process (CPU/GPU time, number of model calls) and discuss whether this cost is feasible in real-world deployments.

Test the method on tasks where the relationship between intermediate feedback and final outcome is less direct, to identify scenarios where intermediate feedback fails to substitute for a final critic.
Provide a detailed description of how 'batch-level fixes' are generated, including implementation details, decision rules, and examples showing their effect on performance.
Explore and evaluate alternative methods for generating batch-level fixes (e.g., rule-based systems, human-in-the-loop interventions) instead of relying solely on an LLM.
Perform a sensitivity analysis on the method's robustness to noisy or incomplete intermediate feedback: vary feedback quality/quantity and report performance degradation patterns.

Investigate and empirically validate whether the LLM reliably identifies the truly 'impactful' input parts; compare LLM-identified impactful parts to other techniques (explainable AI, human annotations).
Provide more transparency about the 'summarizing team': detail how their data was collected, annotation procedures, study/IRB approvals, and any instructions or training provided to annotators.
Discuss potential ethical implications and failure modes of using LLMs to automatically refine prompts, especially for sensitive domains, and propose mitigation strategies.

Clarify novelty relative to Reflexion: explicitly describe how RePrompt differs from Reflexion (not just superficially), or state up front that this work is an application/adaptation of Reflexion for APE.

If the core idea is 'summarization-based' prompt improvement similar to Reflexion, cite Reflexion in the introduction/abstract and explain the concrete technical differences (algorithmic steps, inputs/outputs, objective) between the two methods.

Either provide a rigorous mathematical formulation that justifies using terms like 'gradient-based' and 'loss optimization' (including explicit loss function and gradient computation), or replace those terms with accurate descriptions such as 'iterative prompt refinement' or 'feedback-driven prompt improvement'.

Explain what 'train RePrompt' (line 312) means: specify the training procedure, what parameters (if any) are updated, what is being learned, and provide pseudocode or an algorithmic description of the training step.
Provide empirical evidence that the PDDL verifier is too slow for iterative use (e.g., benchmark runtimes, complexity analysis) if that is the reason it was not used.

For Travel Planning, justify why existing verifiers/checkers cannot be used as final evaluation steps in your experimental setup, or explain any modifications to the problem that remove/limit verifier availability.

If verifiers are actually available for your datasets, reconcile this with the paper's motivation (scenarios with expensive/infeasible final evaluations) and either change the motivation or use datasets that better match the claim.
Include Gundawar et al. (LLM-Modulo) [3] and other relevant APE or prompt-engineering baselines in experiments, or provide a clear justification for why those works were excluded.
Compare RePrompt with additional APE/prompt-engineering methods (including those that use different assumptions) to better contextualize contributions and performance.

Address Reflexion/ReAct limitations inherited by RePrompt: either modify the example selection process to reduce dependence on exemplar-query similarity, or present experiments demonstrating RePrompt's robustness on novel and out-of-distribution queries.

Provide concrete examples of problem domains/settings where final prompt evaluations are expensive or infeasible (e.g., creative writing, long-running simulations, proprietary human evaluations), and explain why final evaluation is impractical in each example.

Add at least one experimental domain where final evaluation is genuinely difficult or impossible (or strongly justify not doing so), to demonstrate the claimed advantage of RePrompt in the intended setting.
Run an ablation study isolating the effect of the Act component: optimize prompts without Act, then evaluate inference with and without Act (i.e., four conditions) and report differences.

Provide a clear analysis and explanation of why and how intermediate Act feedback improves prompt optimization (mechanistic description and empirical evidence showing the influence of intermediate feedback on optimization).
Add a formal problem formulation and an algorithm box or pseudocode that defines inputs, outputs, variables, and each major procedure of the method to make it reproducible.
Resolve inconsistent component naming (e.g., 'LLM summarizer' vs 'summarization team') and adopt consistent labels for all LLMs and human roles throughout the paper and figures.
Precisely define the term 'focus point' (what it is, its data type and semantics) and show the exact prompt or procedure used to extract or label focus points.
Provide the exact prompts (or point to an appendix with them) used for every LLM component (summarizer, feedback provider, agents, etc.), or at minimum clear template prompts for reproducibility.

Specify exactly what the feedback provider outputs (format, content, examples), how that feedback is generated, and how it is incorporated into the prompt optimization loop (direct use or post-processed).

Explain how the framework handles LLM agents that generate across multiple rounds or involve multiple distinct LLMs, and clarify whether and how the Act component is applied in such multi-round/multi-LLM settings.

Correct and expand the comparison to prior work: accurately report what ProTeGi and APE do (e.g., minibatch optimization) and clearly state technical differences and similarities between your method and these baselines.

Provide empirical comparisons and analyses beyond accuracy: report computational cost, sensitivity to hyperparameters, and generalizability (performance across domains or tasks) relative to existing prompt optimization methods.

Include a focused ablation that compares optimizing prompts using only the final task outcome versus using intermediate Act feedback (to show whether intermediate signals add value beyond final outcome supervision).

Improve reproducibility by adding high-level pseudocode in the main text and moving long prompt texts or implementation details to an appendix; ensure descriptions are precise enough for application in other domains.
